The response involved various generator types, including non-scheduled, semi-scheduled, and scheduled generators, as well as utility-scale batteries that played a crucial role in absorbing excess energy. The event was managed successfully without leading to a system black, thanks to a combination of measures including the tripping of windfarms, controlled reductions by solar farms, and changes in operations by thermal generators and batteries. Its relevance level is: Level 1; Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (0-24 hours) following the incident. Its root_cause is: High winds causing transmission tower collapse. Its accident scale is: Localized impact with significant system disturbance. Its dates are: 31-01-2020. Its affected region is: South Australia and southwestern Victoria. Its affected key users are: South Australian electricity consumers and the Portland aluminium smelter. Its cause type is: Natural causes (extreme weather - high winds). Its causes are: Transmission tower collapse, system islanding, supply-demand imbalance, frequency instability.

title: Heat pushes QLD Scheduled Demand within 214MW of the all-time maximum level on 3rd February 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Heat pushes QLD Scheduled Demand within 214MW of the all-time maximum level on 3rd February 2020" posted at 03-02-2020 06:20:00 PM is: The article discusses the Queensland region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) experiencing a significant demand increase due to high temperatures, reaching 9,838MW, just 214MW shy of its all-time maximum level from February 2019; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact during the heatwave period. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat in the region. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-02-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand.

title: What happened with System Frequency with the SA Islanding on Friday 31st January 2020?
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2020 – 31st January – Islanding of South Australia after transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "What happened with System Frequency with the SA Islanding on Friday 31st January 2020?" posted at 03-02-2020 10:58:00 AM is: On January 31, 2020, South Australia experienced islanding after the destruction of transmission towers, causing a frequency instability event. The event led to South Australia being cut off from Victoria, resulting in SA's frequency spiking and Victoria's frequency dropping. This separation happened at 13:24:30 NEM time, causing a shift in power flows and tripping of rooftop PV systems in South Australia, which stabilized demand. There were guesses regarding the impact on the Portland aluminium smelter and local generators. The recovery took time and involved complex changes in power distribution between the regions; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing, with initial impacts on the day and subsequent implications for stability assessments. Its root_cause is: Transmission tower destruction causing system separation. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 31-01-2020. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Portland aluminium smelter and South Australian power consumers. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Transmission towers destroyed, SA islanding, frequency instability, power flow reversal, and tripping of rooftop PV systems.

title: 13 headline questions and observations following the long-term islanding, starting Friday 31st January 2020
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2020 – 31st January – Islanding of South Australia after transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "13 headline questions and observations following the long-term islanding, starting Friday 31st January 2020" posted at 02-02-2020 05:19:00 PM is: On January 31, 2020, South Australia faced a major grid islanding event following the collapse of transmission towers due to extreme winds, isolating the region from the National Electricity Market (NEM). This event raised questions about the durability of transmission infrastructure, repair timelines, and the impact of separated grids on market dynamics and supply reliability. It highlights the challenges faced by South Australia operating independently, with reduced import capacity and ongoing effects on key users like the Portland Aluminium Smelter and renewable energy producers. The broader implications include increased market volatility, potential supply disruptions, and higher operational costs. The situation underscores the importance of robust co-optimization between energy and ancillary service markets, as South Australia's region copes with technical and economic challenges during the repair period.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Two weeks of direct impact with longer-term implications. Root_cause is: Extreme winds causing structural damage to transmission infrastructure. Accident scale is: Significant, affecting statewide grid operations. Dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 31st January 2020. Affected region is: South Australia. Affected key users are: Portland Aluminium Smelter, renewable energy producers. Cause type is: Natural disaster-induced infrastructure failure. Causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Extreme winds, transmission tower failure, grid islanding, separation from NEM.

title: Out of the blue, another LOR2 – NSW region on Saturday 1st February 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Out of the blue, another LOR2 – NSW region on Saturday 1st February 2020" posted at 02-02-2020 12:30:00 PM is: On 1st February 2020, an unexpected Actual Level 2 Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) was declared in the New South Wales (NSW) region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) at 16:55, following a sudden drop in available generation capacity by approximately 650MW. The drop was primarily due to Snowy Hydro’s Colongra station withdrawing its capacity from the market, citing a reason to avoid "uneconomic start," which resulted in a high demand scenario coupled with diminished supply. This triggered immediate actions by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to seek market responses, with parties speculating about strategic bidding behaviors. Fortunately, the availability was restored shortly afterward, within 25 minutes, by putting the capacity back in the market. This scenario underlined the significant challenges in maintaining grid stability under unexpected constraints and raised concerns about future market dynamics as large-scale projects like Snowy 2.0 come online.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but resolved within approximately 25 minutes. Its root_cause is: Strategic withdrawal of generator capacity impacting reserve margins. Its accident scale is: A significant impact on grid reliability but quickly resolved. Its dates are: 02-02-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Strategic bidding behavior. Its causes are: Sudden withdrawal of capacity by Snowy Hydro’s Colongra station, lack of sufficient reserve margin, high electricity demand, and strategic market behavior.

title: Hot weather and high demand continues, with actual LOR1 in NSW region at 13:55 on Saturday 1st February 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hot weather and high demand continues, with actual LOR1 in NSW region at 13:55 on Saturday 1st February 2020" posted at 01-02-2020 02:20:00 PM is: The article discusses an 'Actual Lack of Reserve in Market Notice' in New South Wales (NSW) due to extreme weather conditions causing high demand. This situation led to an alert for LOR1, indicating the least severe level of Low Reserve Condition in the NSW region. The demand reached 12,707MW, with expectations to peak at 13,523MW during the 16:30 trading period. The Australian Energy Market Operator’s (AEMO) forecast for scheduled demand has been increasing, potentially leading to price spikes approaching the Market Price Cap.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (as the impact is closely tied to the ongoing weather and demand conditions). Its root_cause is: High electricity demand driven by hot weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) region in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Extreme weather and demand volatility. Its causes are: Extreme hot weather leading to increased electricity demand, grid system instability due to high load, LOR1 alert due to low reserve conditions in the electricity market.

title: White knuckle ride across the NEM on Friday 31st January 2020 (extreme NEM-wide demand)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2020 – 31st January – Islanding of South Australia after transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "White knuckle ride across the NEM on Friday 31st January 2020 (extreme NEM-wide demand)" posted at 31-01-2020 06:28:00 PM is: The article describes the extraordinary electricity demand experienced across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on January 31, 2020. Extreme temperatures in New South Wales and Victoria led to elevated electricity demand, reaching a peak NEM-wide Scheduled Demand level of 35,169 MW by 17:00. This surge in demand was noteworthy despite a significant amount of consumption being 'lost' due to various factors, including behind-the-meter rooftop PV contributions, transmission damages affecting the Alcoa's Portland Aluminium Smelter in Victoria, demand reduction in New South Wales, and the triggering of Reserve Trader mechanisms in NSW and VIC. These elements collectively contributed to over 2,300 MW of 'lost consumption.' The complexities of the situation were amplified by transmission tower destructions which led to the islanding of South Australia; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term (immediate impact on the day of the event). Its root cause is: Extreme weather conditions leading to elevated demand. Its accident scale is: Regional (affecting multiple states across the NEM). Its dates are: 31-01-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers, including residential users particularly affected by local outages. Its cause type is: Natural and Infrastructure. Its causes are: Elevated temperatures, transmission tower damage, rooftop PV contributions, transmission failures, NEM-wide elevated demand.

title: A further look at Thursday Jan 30th
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "A further look at Thursday Jan 30th" posted at 31-01-2020 03:42:00 PM is: The report analyzes the NEM (National Electricity Market) conditions on January 30th, where high demand and generation capacity issues led to significant price spikes across various regions. The data highlights challenges such as the Loy Yang A3 turbine trip, transmission constraints, and fluctuating renewable energy outputs due to extreme weather conditions. These factors, along with strategic bidding behaviors by generators, resulted in price surges, particularly in Victoria and South Australia, requiring intervention from AEMO to manage demand and supply balance. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High demand exceeding available generation, exacerbated by Loy Yang A3 turbine trip and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting multiple states. Its dates are: 30-01-2020. Its affected region is: Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales, Queensland. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Operational and weather-related issues. Its causes are: High demand, Loy Yang A3 turbine trip, generation capacity constraints, transmission limits, strategic bidding behaviors.

title: Buckle in folks, Friday afternoon/evening might be a bit bumpy
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2020 – 31st January – Islanding of South Australia after transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "Buckle in folks, Friday afternoon/evening might be a bit bumpy" posted at 31-01-2020 12:02:00 PM is: The article reports on the severe heatwave impacting Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) on January 31, 2020, which is expected to cause record-breaking electricity demand across regions, notably Victoria and New South Wales. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued alerts regarding potential Low Reserve Conditions (LOR1) due to extreme temperatures affecting generating capacity. High demand and declining supply availability could lead to significant electricity price spikes and increased grid instability. AEMO highlights the need for urgent cooperation from market participants to manage demand and supply effectively under the heatwave conditions, thereby averting possible grid failures. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate, specifically for January 31, 2020, and the following days). Its root_cause is: Extreme weather conditions (heatwave). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-01-2020 and subsequent days for related regions. Its affected region is: East coast of Australia, specifically Victoria and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Natural phenomenon. Its causes are: Forecast of extreme temperatures leading to potential electricity demand exceeding available supply, and high electricity prices.

title: Thursday 30th January 2020 sees demand reach 34,257MW and LOR2 notice issued for both VIC and SA
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Thursday 30th January 2020 sees demand reach 34,257MW and LOR2 notice issued for both VIC and SA" posted at 30-01-2020 08:40:00 PM is: On January 30th, 2020, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced a significant strain with demand peaking at 34,257MW. High temperatures across the NEM regions contributed to this surge in demand. A notable decrease in wind production, which was metered at a low of 460MW, and a decline in solar output exacerbated the situation. The Loy Yang A3 unit tripped, adding stress to the grid. This situation led AEMO to issue a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) notice for Victoria and South Australia and to begin Reserve Trader negotiations. It highlighted the rising risk levels in the NEM, emphasizing the need for sufficient dispatchable capacity. Furthermore, the dispatch prices in VIC and SA approached the Market Price Cap. The article anticipates further analysis from Allan O’Neil due to expected higher demand the following day.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: High temperatures and low renewable output. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 30-01-2020. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Industrial and residential users in VIC and SA. Its cause type is: Weather-related and equipment failure. Its causes are: High demand due to temperatures, low wind and solar output, Loy Yang A3 unit trip.

title: NEM-wide demand might exceed 34,000MW on Friday evening
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide demand might exceed 34,000MW on Friday evening" posted at 30-01-2020 01:03:00 PM is: The news discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) forecast for a significant demand spike across the National Electricity Market (NEM), projected to exceed 34,000MW on Friday evening, January 31, 2020. This potential peak is noteworthy as it has been three years since demand reached this level, primarily driven by forecasted high temperatures and reduced 'behind the meter' energy production. An LOR2 (Low Reserve Condition) notice was issued for Victoria, highlighting the pressure on reserves. AEMO is seeking market responses to address the situation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, specifically on January 31, 2020. Its root cause is: High forecasted demand due to increased temperatures and reduced behind-the-meter production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: January 31, 2020. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted high electricity demand. Its causes are: 1) Increased temperatures leading to higher energy use; 2) Reduced forecast for behind-the-meter production like rooftop PV.

title: 2019 was not a great year at the Liddell Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "2019 was not a great year at the Liddell Power Station" posted at 30-01-2020 12:00:00 PM is: The Liddell Power Station in Hunter Valley, acquired by AGL Energy, faced significant operational challenges in 2019. Units at the plant experienced frequent outages, with Liddell unit 4 undergoing a notable three-month outage. Despite claims that high temperatures are causing coal plant failures, the analysis from the Generator Statistical Digest 2019 did not support this narrative. Instead, issues such as capacity restrictions due to long-standing operational decisions played a larger role in the plant's performance challenges. The station has faced scrutiny and pressure towards closure, especially after the retirement of Hazelwood Power Station, with ongoing discussions about its future viability. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Approximately 2019, as discussed in the report. Its root cause is: Operational challenges and maintenance issues with the plant units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2019. Its affected region is: Hunter Valley, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and maintenance issues. Its causes are: Frequent outages, operational restrictions, long-standing capacity limitations, and perceptions around high ambient temperature effects.

title: Review of extremes in ‘Raw Off Target’ performance through 2019, with the Generator Statistical Digest 2019
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Review of extremes in ‘Raw Off Target’ performance through 2019, with the Generator Statistical Digest 2019" posted at 28-01-2020 04:34:00 PM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2019 evaluates the "Raw Off-Target" performance of various power generation units across Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). This measure indicates how closely units follow expectations set by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO). The report highlights that discrepancies in power output can lead to increased costs and complexities in the market due to the need for more Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS). Notably, while coal, gas, and hydro units show some discrepancies, semi-scheduled solar and wind units present the most extreme 'Raw Off-Target' events. The analysis underscores the necessity for in-depth analytics and reforms, particularly for the Semi-Scheduled category units, to enhance market sustainability as the shift towards renewable energy intensifies. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Throughout 2019. Its root cause is: Generation performance inconsistencies in relation to AEMO targets. Its accident scale is: None, as this pertains to ongoing performance analysis rather than a specific incident. Its dates are: 2019. Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Power generators within the NEM, AEMO, and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Operational performance and market policy. Its causes are: Under or over-production by generation units, especially semi-scheduled solar and wind, and potential market structure issues.

title: Generator Statistical Digest 2019 is released on Tuesday 28th January 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Generator Statistical Digest 2019 is released on Tuesday 28th January 2020" posted at 28-01-2020 03:08:00 PM is: The release of the Generator Statistical Digest 2019 (GSD2019) marks a significant enhancement of previous electricity market analyses by offering detailed statistical data covering a decade, with enhanced focus on the calendar year 2019. This digest is an evolution from the previous Generator Report Card 2018, now split into two parts for better insights: annual statistical reports and less frequent analytic updates. GSD2019 compiles comprehensive data on 304 DUIDs, providing invaluable insights into trends in solar, wind, and other traditional power systems within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Articles from several analysts highlight key insights from the digest, reinforcing its importance as a resource for market observers and policy advisers. Its classification relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing as it provides annual insights. The root cause is: Publication of yearly market data. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-01-2020. The affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). The affected key users are: Energy market analysts, consultants, and policy advisers. The cause type is: Strategic publication. The causes are: The evolution of prior generator reports, need for updated yearly statistical insights, and enhanced analytical approaches for users in the electricity market.

title: Understanding the NEM through the lens of the GSD 2019
author: Jonathon Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Understanding the NEM through the lens of the GSD 2019" posted at 28-01-2020 02:00:00 PM is: The article analyzes the complexities of the National Electricity Market (NEM) and highlights the importance of integrating various generation types for ensuring supply reliability and predictability. The Generator Statistical Digest 2019 provides insights into the generation market values of coal, gas, wind, and solar, showing the economic realities facing each. Despite the narrative to phase out coal, the data suggests a considerable ongoing reliance on coal-fired generation due to its reliability and predictability. Gas-fired generation, however, is challenged by newer wind and solar power plants, which is affecting their revenue and capacity factors. The transition from coal is significant but gradual, requiring numerous high-capacity wind and solar farms. The GSD 2019 emphasizes the importance of understanding these dynamics for anyone involved in the NEM. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since the publication date. Its root_cause is: The integration challenges and economic pressures associated with transitioning from coal-fired to renewable energy sources in the NEM. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 28-01-2020. The affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. The affected key users are: Energy producers and market operators. The cause type is: System dynamics and economic factors. The causes are: Transition from coal, integration of renewables, economic and market value shifts in different generation sources.

title: Selected initial observations from the Generator Statistical Digest 2019
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "Selected initial observations from the Generator Statistical Digest 2019" posted at 28-01-2020 11:17:00 AM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2019 provides a comprehensive analysis of the electricity market over a decade, with a particular focus on 2019. It introduces new metrics such as Price Energy Harvest and Connection Point Dispatch Price (CPD Price), highlighting the importance of location and constraints in pricing electricity. The digest also examines the capacity factors of various wind and solar farms, the profitability of the Hornsdale Power Reserve, and the performance of conventional and pumped hydro stations. Key observations include the variability of capacity factors across different regions and the strong market performance in January 2019. Overall, it provides valuable insights into generation trends and market dynamics.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term analysis (throughout 2019). Its root_cause is: Analytical report of historical data. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-01-2020 11:17:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market analysts, policy makers. Its cause type is: Report and analysis. Its causes are: Introduction of new metrics (Price Energy Harvest, CPD Price), analysis of capacity factors for renewable and non-renewable sources, examination of profitability and market performance of various energy resources.

title: What lies ahead for the NEM in 2020?  Some lessons from 2019
author: Ben Willacy
classified_content: The summary of the news "What lies ahead for the NEM in 2020? Some lessons from 2019" posted at 28-01-2020 09:57:00 AM is: The article reviews lessons from 2019 in the National Electricity Market (NEM) and anticipates key themes for 2020. It highlights challenges including connection difficulties, high storage costs, and commissioning delays which are expected to affect energy prices. Battery storage facilities generated significant revenue through high buy-sell spreads but are unlikely to drastically lower prices without increased competition and lower build costs. Operational complexities for renewable energy are increasing due to location-specific network constraints, and lengthy commissioning times for wind farms may delay energy market entry, potentially sustaining high wholesale prices; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions . Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2020. Its root_cause is: Operational and market challenges in NEM, such as connection difficulties and network congestion. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-01-2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market regions including Victoria, South Australia, New South Wales, and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Energy operators and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market operation and delays in renewable energy commissioning. Its causes are: Commissioning delays for wind farms, high storage costs, and low CPDs affecting renewable energy dispatch strategies.

title: How Good is Solar Farming?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "How Good is Solar Farming?" posted at 28-01-2020 07:54:00 AM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2019 highlights several challenges faced by utility-scale solar farms within the National Electricity Market (NEM), using Coleambally Solar Farm as a case study. These challenges include falling Marginal Loss Factors (MLFs), which reduce both energy revenue and Large-scale Generation Certificate (LGC) credits, discounted Volume-Weighted Average (VWA) prices due to increased daytime solar supply and reduced demand, impacts from constraints in the grid that can limit output, and high Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) costs stemming from variability in solar output. Collectively, these factors can significantly affect the profitability of solar projects, suggesting that future investors should approach solar farming with more caution. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid to Late 2019. Its root_cause is: Market and operational challenges faced by utility-scale solar farms within the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 28-01-2020. Its affected region is: Southern New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Utility-scale solar farms. Its cause type is: Market and operational factors. Its causes are: Falling MLFs, discounted VWA prices, constraint impacts, and high FCAS costs.

title: Factors contributing to events in NSW on Thursday 23rd January 2020 – taking a closer look…
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Factors contributing to events in NSW on Thursday 23rd January 2020 – taking a closer look…" posted at 25-01-2020 09:20:00 PM is: The article examines the electricity market conditions in New South Wales (NSW), Australia, on 23rd January 2020, highlighting challenges faced due to a tight supply/demand balance. In this period, several factors contributed to market instability: abnormal weather conditions, namely damaging winds and heavy rainfall, led to increased contingency risks. AEMO issued notices forecasting potential supply issues (LOR2) and began Reserve Trader (RERT) negotiations due to foreseen high demand and limited supply, impacted by coal units being offline, suboptimal wind generation, and constraints on interstate power flows. These conditions led to price spikes in the electricity market. The constraints set by AEMO to manage grid reliability in light of these challenges limited cheaper power dispatch, potentially driving up prices further. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day as the events of 23rd January 2020). Its root_cause is: A combination of severe weather events, offline coal units, and demand exceeding forecasts. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23rd January 2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electrical consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Multiple factors including weather and generator availability. Its causes are: Abnormal weather causing contingency warnings, offline coal-fired units, constraints on interstate power flows, and forecasting errors in demand and renewable generation output.

title: RERT dispatched in NSW this afternoon, plus other things (at 15:55)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT dispatched in NSW this afternoon, plus other things (at 15:55)" posted at 23-01-2020 03:57:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) activated the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) process in New South Wales (NSW) to maintain grid reliability due to insufficient reserves, marking an intervention event from 15:30 to 17:30 on 23 January 2020. The article discusses the implications of AEMO's intervention, market price discrepancies, and how Snowy Hydro's bidding strategies influenced the electricity prices across different regions. Despite expectations of higher prices, only a single dispatch interval reached the Market Price Cap. Additionally, the event highlighted issues related to grid constraints and energy conservation calls in NSW. It brings attention to the complexities and strategic decisions in the electricity market during peak demand periods. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the afternoon of 23 January 2020. Its root_cause is: Insufficient reserves leading to grid instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-01-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Market intervention due to grid instability and reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Insufficient reserves, market failure to balance supply and demand, grid constraints, and strategic bidding interventions.

title: Shaky afternoon ahead for the NSW Region of the NEM (as at 12:05 on Thu 23rd Jan 2020)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Shaky afternoon ahead for the NSW Region of the NEM (as at 12:05 on Thu 23rd Jan 2020)" posted at 23-01-2020 12:32:00 PM is: The NSW region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) is expected to face supply challenges due to increasing forecasted demand this afternoon, exacerbated by transmission limitations in the Snowy Mountains area affected by bushfires. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued market notices indicating intentions to negotiate additional reserve capacity through the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) process to address the Low Reserve Condition forecasted. Additionally, extreme weather conditions with temperatures reaching up to 42 degrees Celsius in the Sydney area are expected, further impacting electricity generation and supply. Affected parties, particularly market participants with generating plants, have been requested to review and update their generation levels according to the weather forecast. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the day, 23/01/2020). The root_cause is: Extreme weather conditions and transmission limitations due to bushfire damage. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 23-01-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales, particularly the Sydney area. Its affected key users are: Industrial and residential electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Natural and operational factors. Its causes are: Forecasted high temperatures, increased electricity demand, bushfire-affected transmission capabilities, and resultant low reserve conditions.

title: Queensland Scheduled Demand exceeds 9650MW today – within 400MW of the all-time record!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland Scheduled Demand exceeds 9650MW today – within 400MW of the all-time record!" posted at 21-01-2020 05:05:00 PM is: On January 21, 2020, Queensland experienced a surge in electricity demand, with Scheduled Demand surpassing 9,650MW for the first time that summer, nearing the all-time record of the previous summer. This spike in demand was accompanied by a high price point of $1,494.69/MWh earlier in the afternoon, with demand peaking at 9,657MW by 16:10, influenced partially by a decline in solar PV contributions and a thunderstorm in the region. Technical issues were noted with the AEMO's systems, and the event underscores the challenges of demand management under fluctuating weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term until weather conditions stabilize. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand due to hot/humid weather and decreased solar PV output. Its accident scale is: Localized to the Queensland region. Its dates are: 21-01-2020. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators and consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather and system-related. Its causes are: High temperature and humidity increasing demand, reduced PV output, AEMO system glitches.

title: Second time lucky?  The return (again) of Loy Yang A2 unit
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Second time lucky? The return (again) of Loy Yang A2 unit" posted at 21-01-2020 09:15:00 AM is: The Loy Yang A2 unit, which initially returned to service on 24th December 2019 following a long outage that started on 18th May 2019, has resumed operation again after tripping off on 27th December due to unresolved issues. The unit's recent operational status has been provided via the Unit Dashboard Widget in the ez2view application, and there is hope that previous teething issues have now been resolved. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, starting from 21-01-2020. Its root_cause is: Equipment outage and maintenance issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-12-2019 to 27-12-2019 and 21-01-2020. Its affected region is: Victoria, within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers dependent on the Loy Yang A power generation. Its cause type is: Technical/equipment failure. Its causes are: A long outage followed by a premature return to service and subsequent equipment failure.

title: Monday’s NSW rain/hail storms also dampens solar output
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Monday’s NSW rain/hail storms also dampens solar output" posted at 20-01-2020 03:03:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of a severe hailstorm in New South Wales, Australia, on January 20, 2020, which led to a significant drop in solar power output from both large solar farms and rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems. The storm resulted in an estimated reduction of 400MW to 450MW in rooftop PV output and an additional 100MW drop in large solar farms' output. The adverse weather conditions pose a challenge to energy management within the evolving energy market. Despite a forecasted Low Reserve Condition, subsequent notices indicated that this condition was later canceled. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact on the day of the storm. Its root cause is: Hailstorm causing reduced solar output. Its accident scale is: Regional impact on solar generation within New South Wales. Its dates are: 20-01-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar power generators and related energy stakeholders. Its cause type is: Natural event. Its causes are: Severe hailstorm leading to reduced solar energy generation capacities.

title: A first look at the impact of Sunday’s Dust Storms in NSW on the output of Nyngan Solar Farm
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A first look at the impact of Sunday’s Dust Storms in NSW on the output of Nyngan Solar Farm" posted at 20-01-2020 12:22:00 PM is: The dust storm on January 19, 2020, in western New South Wales significantly impacted the output of solar farms, including the Nyngan Solar Farm, causing steep drops in power generation. There were three notable incidents of output reduction during the afternoon, with the most significant being a fall to 0MW much earlier than the expected sunset time. The aggregate discrepancy in expected versus actual output spanned between 100MW and 200MW across solar farms in New South Wales, indicating not all were equally affected. The dust seemingly coated solar panels, potentially affecting future outputs not accounted for in forecasting models; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, with possible longer-term effects if dust coating persists. Its root_cause is: Dust storm leading to reduced solar energy generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-01-2020. Its affected region is: Western New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural event. Its causes are: Dust storm causing significant reduction in solar energy output, potentially affecting panel efficiency beyond the immediate impact.

title: About ERM’s two rule change proposals in at the AEMC
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About ERM’s two rule change proposals in at the AEMC" posted at 13-01-2020 05:36:00 PM is: The article discusses ERM Power's proposal for rule changes submitted to the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC), aimed at improving transparency and extending the duration of the Medium Term Projected Assessment of System Adequacy (MT PASA). The proposals focus on providing DUID-level (Dispatchable Unit Identifier) availability data and extending the MT PASA outlook horizon from 24 months to 36 months. The AEMC's draft ruling supports these proposals, which would enhance market participants' ability to perform independent analyses and create more informed business strategies.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. The root_cause is: Lack of transparency and information longevity in MT PASA data. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-01-2020. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Structural and regulatory changes. Its causes are: Limited visibility of generation availability data, need for extended forecast duration, and improved analytical capabilities for market participants.

title: What is the RRO and why has the South Australian Energy Minister just triggered it?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "What is the RRO and why has the South Australian Energy Minister just triggered it?" posted at 13-01-2020 03:36:00 PM is: The South Australian Energy Minister has triggered the Retailer Reliability Obligation (RRO) due to concerns about future electricity supply reliability. The RRO requires electricity retailers to hold sufficient hedge contracts if a system reliability gap is predicted, ensuring sufficient firm generation builds. This measure was previously part of the National Energy Guarantee (NEG), which was abandoned. The RRO process involves the AEMO and AER assessments and allows for the unilateral triggering by the South Australian Minister to issue a T-3 Reliability Instrument when a gap is identified. The Minister's decision has activated the Market Liquidity Obligation (MLO) with AGL, Engie, and Origin to offer small-volume futures contracts, potentially aiding smaller retailers' access to hedge contracts. This decision was partly precautionary due to high recent demand caused by extreme weather conditions, although current reliability projections are within the standard. The move might help reveal market views on reliability through actual contract prices, as South Australia's electricity contract market has low liquidity. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing until confirmed reliability gap is assessed. Its root_cause is: Concerns about electricity supply reliability in South Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-01-2020. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity retailers and potentially consumers. Its cause type is: Energy policy trigger. Its causes are: Future concerns about electricity supply reliability and potential high demand driven by extreme weather conditions.

title: Where’s Loy Yang A unit 2?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Where’s Loy Yang A unit 2?" posted at 13-01-2020 12:59:00 PM is: The article discusses the unexpected and prolonged outage of Loy Yang A2, a critical power unit in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, during the summer of 2019-20. The unit, which initially tripped offline on 18th May 2019 and returned to service briefly on 24th December, unexpectedly went offline again on 27th December and remained out of operation as of 13th January 2020, raising concerns about supply stability, especially during a period historically associated with high energy demand. The outages coincide with grid stress caused by bushfires and highlight the potential impacts of rule change proposals to improve market transparency. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Turbine/generator failures; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term; Its root_cause is: Generator failure (Loy Yang A2 unit); Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates is: May 18, 2019, December 24-27, 2019, January 13, 2020; Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia; Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Technical failure; Its causes is: Unanticipated unit trip, extended maintenance issues, bushfires causing additional grid stress.

title: Some insights about Loy Yang A unit 1 from CAL 2019 (using a draft of the ‘B’ page from the GSD2019)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some insights about Loy Yang A unit 1 from CAL 2019 (using a draft of the ‘B’ page from the GSD2019)" posted at 11-01-2020 11:00:00 AM is: The article discusses the upcoming release of the Generator Statistical Digest 2019, which builds on the previous Generator Report Card 2018. The update will include data up to the end of 2019 and will feature new units added in 2019, while retaining some original content and expanding it with additional analyses. The Loy Yang A unit 1 is highlighted as part of a sample analysis presented by a software engineer working on the project. The release of this comprehensive update is eagerly anticipated, as it will provide insights derived from extensive metrics for industry stakeholders and readers of WattClarity.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Long-Term Market Trends and Forecasts. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Update and expansion of industry data reports. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-01-2020. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Industry stakeholders, WattClarity readers. Its cause type is: Industry report update. Its causes are: Expansion of statistical data, Addition of new operational units, Anticipated insights from updated metrics.

title: Operating under siege
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Operating under siege" posted at 09-01-2020 03:50:00 PM is: The article discusses how AEMO managed system security in the face of extreme weather and bushfire threats during the summer of 2019-20 in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the complexities of maintaining grid stability while optimizing market dispatch under environmental stressors, such as bushfires impacting transmission assets, particularly in the Snowy Mountains region. AEMO applied constraints to limit power flows to enhance robustness before the separation of NSW and Victoria occurred due to bushfire impacts. Despite extreme prices and resource challenges, the system avoided a complete blackout, showcasing effective risk management. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate during the summer of 2019-20. Its root_cause is: Bushfires impacting transmission infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-01-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Victoria. Its affected key users are: NSW electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Bushfires, extreme weather leading to transmission asset tripping and regional separation.

title: More details on the bushfire-driven extremes in the NSW Region of the NEM on Saturday 4th January
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More details on the bushfire-driven extremes in the NSW Region of the NEM on Saturday 4th January" posted at 04-01-2020 07:51:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of severe bushfires on the New South Wales (NSW) region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on January 4, 2020. These bushfires, coupled with extreme temperatures, posed a significant threat to electricity transmission infrastructure in the region. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had issued several warnings throughout the days leading up to January 4, anticipating non-credible contingency events due to these extreme conditions. The bushfires eventually caused the tripping of interconnections, separating the NSW region from Victoria, leading to supply disruptions and substantial price spikes in the electricity market. The electricity price in NSW soared to the Market Price Cap of $14,700/MWh, affecting supply-demand balance and triggering an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition. AEMO dispatched Reserve Trader (RERT) services to manage the crisis, seeking urgent market responses to maintain system stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability, fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Immediate, with ongoing potential impacts due to infrastructure damage and continuing extreme conditions. Root_cause: Extreme bushfire conditions compounded by high temperatures affecting transmission infrastructure. Accident scale: Regional (NSW), with potential broader implications across NEM. Dates: 04-01-2020. Affected region: New South Wales, Australia. Affected key users: Electricity consumers in NSW, including major industrial users like Tomago Aluminium. Cause type: Environmental, infrastructure. Causes: Severe bushfires, extreme temperatures, transmission system failures, interconnection separation, reduced generation capacity.

title: Bushfires under interconnectors through Snowy Mountains cause separation of NSW from VIC region
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bushfires under interconnectors through Snowy Mountains cause separation of NSW from VIC region" posted at 04-01-2020 03:50:00 PM is: In early January 2020, the raging bushfires in the south-east of Australia led to the separation of the electricity transmission network between Victoria and New South Wales. At approximately 15:10 NEM time, bushfires affected key transmission lines, causing multiple unplanned outages. This significant disruption included outages at the Murray and Lower Tumut Substation. As a result, New South Wales was isolated from Victoria, leading to a price spike to the Market Price Cap. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and TransGrid reported four transmission line outages, significantly impacting electricity flow. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, significant on the day of the event itself, particularly during the afternoon dispatch intervals. Its root_cause is: Bushfires affecting transmission lines. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-01-2020. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in New South Wales. Its cause type is: Natural disaster-induced infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Bushfires causing outage of critical transmission lines, leading to the separation of NSW from the VIC region in the electricity network.

title: Price volatility in South Australia Thursday evening 30th December 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in South Australia Thursday evening 30th December 2021" posted at 31-12-2021 11:34:00 AM is: The article reports a significant price spike in the South Australian electricity market on the evening of December 30th, 2021, where prices soared above $100/MWh, reaching a peak of $13,001.39/MWh. The volatility was attributed to high demand levels in South Australia, which were uncharacteristic for the year, and a heavily fossil fuel-dependent energy mix with minimal contributions from wind and solar power. The limited activity of battery storage and the resultant rebidding by multiple energy providers further exacerbated the situation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High market demand and limited renewable energy supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-12-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Supply and demand imbalance. Its causes are: High market demand, low renewable energy input, limited battery activity, rebidding by energy providers.

title: Price volatility in South Australia on Thursday 30th December (2nd day in a row) – and High Temperature alerts broadened
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in South Australia on Thursday 30th December (2nd day in a row) – and High Temperature alerts broadened" posted at 30-12-2021 03:07:00 PM is: The article discusses a second consecutive day of electricity price spikes in South Australia, driven by a combination of high temperatures and reduced wind generation. The constraint on imports from Victoria, due to transient stability limits, exacerbates the situation, as market demand increases in response to rising temperatures. Additionally, a high temperature warning has been issued for several regions including South Australia, indicating potential further impacts on electricity demand and prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term effects spanning several days as high temperatures persist. Its root cause is: Extreme weather conditions, specifically unseasonably high temperatures affecting supply and demand dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-12-2021 to 03-01-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia, with broader temperature alerts for Queensland, Tasmania, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand, constraint on inter-regional imports, reduced wind generation, and ongoing high levels of gas and liquid fuel electricity generation.

title: How should the AER’s wholesale monitoring responsibilities change, to suit the changing market environment?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How should the AER’s wholesale monitoring responsibilities change, to suit the changing market environment?" posted at 30-12-2021 01:02:00 PM is: The article discusses a Rule Change Request submitted by the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) to the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) to reform the reporting framework for monitoring significant price events in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The AER seeks a shift from the current prescriptive requirements under clause 3.13.7 of the National Electricity Rules to a more flexible, principles-based approach. This change aims to enhance their ability to analyze and report on wholesale market outcomes more insightfully, given the rapidly evolving market conditions, such as technological changes and behavioral trends among market participants. The AER believes the new framework will allow for more holistic and adaptive reporting, ultimately supporting the National Electricity Objective. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The inadequacy of current AER reporting requirements to address evolving market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-12-2021 01:02:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), primarily Queensland. Its affected key users are: Wholesale electricity market stakeholders and policymakers. Its cause type is: Policy inadequacy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Inflexibility of current rules, rapid market changes, and need for a more adaptive and insightful reporting framework.

title: AEMO publishes (draft of) High Level Design for IESS (submissions due Fri 11th Feb 2022)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes (draft of) High Level Design for IESS (submissions due Fri 11th Feb 2022)" posted at 30-12-2021 12:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released a draft of the High-Level Design for implementing the Integrating Energy Storage Systems (IESS) rule change into the National Electricity Market (NEM), following the Australian Energy Market Commission's (AEMC) Final Determination on this reform. The implementation will be completed in two phases: a 'baseline' release on 31st March 2023, allowing new registration and dispatch models, and a 'final' release on 3rd June 2024, which will fully implement the IESS rule changes including hybrid systems' aggregate dispatch. AEMO is seeking stakeholder feedback by 11th February 2022 to refine these proposals. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Medium to long-term (spanning until June 2024). Its root cause is: Implementation of energy market reform. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31st March 2023 and 3rd June 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Market participants, including aggregators, hybrid system operators, and stakeholders in the energy market. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: Integration of energy storage systems into the NEM, changes to registration and dispatch models, allowance of aggregate dispatch conformance and ancillary services provision by aggregators.

title: Callide C4 updated RTS expectations on 5th November 2021 (now not expected back online until 7th April 2023)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C4 updated RTS expectations on 5th November 2021 (now not expected back online until 7th April 2023)" posted at 30-12-2021 11:22:00 AM is: An update on the expected return to service (RTS) of the damaged Callide C4 unit was published on 5th November 2021, indicating that the unit is not expected to be back online until 7th April 2023. This news came about eight weeks after an earlier update in September, highlighting issues such as the delayed dissemination of this information and a call out to the lack of coverage for Semi-Scheduled plants under the current system. This delay in RTS will potentially influence electricity pricing due to the extended outage period. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until 7th April 2023. Its root_cause is: The explosion, fire, and outage of the Callide C4 unit in May 2021. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-12-2021 and 5th November 2021. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational failure. Its causes are: Explosion, fire, and outage of Callide C4 unit.

title: Price spikes in South Australia above $9,000/MWh – due to unforecast cloud cover?!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spikes in South Australia above $9,000/MWh – due to unforecast cloud cover?!" posted at 29-12-2021 10:31:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant electricity price spike in South Australia, where prices soared from near $0/MWh to above $9,000/MWh on December 29, 2021. This unexpected surge was linked to unforeseen cloud cover impacting solar output, resulting in a drop in expected generation and highlighting both the volatility and challenges of balancing supply and demand in the electricity market. Understanding changes in renewable generation, particularly under varying weather conditions, exemplifies the potential for extreme price volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, immediate impact during the event on December 29, 2021. Its root_cause is: Unforeseen cloud cover negatively affecting solar generation output, leading to sudden supply-demand imbalances. Its accident scale is: Localized to South Australia. Its dates are: 29-12-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and suppliers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Environmental/weather-related. Its causes are: Unforecast cloud cover leading to sudden drops in solar power generation, coupled with existing limited gas-fired generation and maximum import capacity from Victoria, contributing to the rapid escalation in electricity prices.

title: ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria moves a shade lower on Tuesday 28th December 2021 … but not quite, for Operational Demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'Market Demand' in Victoria moves a shade lower on Tuesday 28th December 2021 … but not quite, for Operational Demand" posted at 28-12-2021 07:33:00 PM is: On December 28, 2021, Victoria experienced a slight dip in 'Market Demand' to 2,123MW, 21MW lower than the previous record on November 28, 2021. 'Operational Demand,' however, was at 2,389MW, 56MW higher than the November low. This period also saw negative electricity prices in Victoria and SA, but battery usage remained low, with transmission constraints affecting multiple generators. These factors indicate operational challenges rather than significant price changes in the NEM. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Low market demand and operational dynamics on that specific day. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-12-2021. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Record low 'Market Demand', slightly higher 'Operational Demand', negative electricity prices, minimal battery usage, and transmission constraints.

title: David Leitch’s review of GenInsights21
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "David Leitch’s review of GenInsights21" posted at 23-12-2021 12:46:00 PM is: David Leitch provides an early review of the GenInsights21 publication, emphasizing its analysis of the National Electricity Market (NEM) and pointing out key challenges such as increasing variability, uncertainty, and complexity. Leitch highlights the importance of forecasting and market analysis, considering the influence of various state policies on price signals in the energy market. He also discusses the transition towards renewable energy sources and the role of innovative technologies in managing grid services, supporting the findings with examples from the report's sections and appendices.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Analysis of market dynamics and future trends in electricity generation and policy. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-12-2021. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants, policy makers, and energy analysts. Its cause type is: Analytical review. Its causes are: Increasing complexity and variability in the electricity market; reliance on forecasting; impact of renewable energy policies on price signals.

title: How many Negawatts have been dispatched through the WDRM since its introduction?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "How many Negawatts have been dispatched through the WDRM since its introduction?" posted at 23-12-2021 11:42:00 AM is: The article investigates the application of the Wholesale Demand Response Mechanism (WDRM) introduced in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on 24th October 2021, which allows large energy users to bid demand reductions, termed as ‘Negawatts.’ By December 22, 2021, only two out of the three registered units, managed by Enel X, have been dispatched once on November 12, 2021, indicating the early stages of the implementation of this rule change. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as it is still early in the implementation with limited immediate impact. Its root cause is: Implementation of the WDRM rule change in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24th October 2021 (rule change came into effect) and 12th November 2021 (first dispatch). Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Large industrial energy users. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: Introduction and initial application stage of the WDRM in the NEM with limited dispatch occurrences.

title: Spot price volatility in QLD (and NSW) on Tuesday 21st December 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in QLD (and NSW) on Tuesday 21st December 2021" posted at 21-12-2021 07:00:00 PM is: The article discusses spot price volatility in the Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) regions on 21st December 2021, highlighting a significant price jump in NSW due to constrained imports from Victoria (VIC) and a substantial drop in solar generation, coinciding with an unexpected increase in battery charging. Prices in QLD and NSW, and potentially in VIC and South Australia (SA), are projected to remain high this evening. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Constrained imports from VIC and a drop in solar generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-12-2021. Its affected region is: QLD and NSW, with mentions of VIC and SA. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Market and resource availability. Its causes are: Constrained VIC imports and reduced solar production.

title: The 28 x ‘Deep Dive’ Appendices included in GenInsights21
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The 28 x ‘Deep Dive’ Appendices included in GenInsights21" posted at 21-12-2021 03:40:00 PM is: The article discusses the release of the GenInsights21 report, which is an extensive update and extension of previous analytical content by Global-Roam Pty Ltd, in collaboration with Greenview Strategic Consulting. Released on December 15, 2021, this 622-page report is structured into distinct sections, including 28 detailed 'Deep Dive' Appendices that explore various aspects of the National Electricity Market (NEM). The appendices cover topics such as the history of renewables, coal decline, frequency performance, and wind diversity. The report aims to provide insights into the energy transition challenges over the years within the NEM, and it is available in both digital and hard copy for clients. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Compilation and analysis of historical and current electricity market data. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-12-2021. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy analysts and stakeholders in the electricity sector. Its cause type is: Planned reporting and analysis. Its causes are: Release of an in-depth report on the electricity market including historical data, market analyses, and future insights.

title: Spot price and demand up in QLD on a hot and humid Monday 20th December 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price and demand up in QLD on a hot and humid Monday 20th December 2021" posted at 20-12-2021 05:41:00 PM is: The article details a significant increase in electricity demand and spot price in Queensland on 20th December 2021, triggered by hot and humid weather conditions. Market Demand rose to the orange zone, leading the spot price to elevate to $1,300/MWh. The decline in solar energy during the afternoon compounded the tight supply-demand balance, as constrained imports from New South Wales left limited spare capacity. AEMO issued a Low Reserve Condition forecast for later that evening due to insufficient reserve capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Hot and humid weather causing increased electricity demand and reduced solar generation. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 20-12-2021. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Queensland electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand and supply dynamics. Its causes are: Hot and humid weather, reduced solar output, and constrained power imports from New South Wales.

title: Price volatility in South Australia on Thu 16th December 2021 reinforces observations in GenInsights21
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in South Australia on Thu 16th December 2021 reinforces observations in GenInsights21" posted at 16-12-2021 10:38:00 AM is: The news discusses a price spike in the South Australian region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the 10:10 dispatch interval on December 16, 2021, highlighting a trend of fluctuating electricity prices that were predicted in the GenInsights21 report. The volatility was attributed to decreasing wind power generation and low levels of gas generation due to operational synchronous condensers. The report emphasized the growing complexity and risk in electricity markets. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Decline in wind power generation and low gas generation levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-12-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Electricity generation issues. Its causes are: Low gas generation levels and declining wind power generation.

title: GenInsights21 released, on Wednesday 15th December 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights21 released, on Wednesday 15th December 2021" posted at 15-12-2021 07:46:00 AM is: GenInsights21, a comprehensive 622-page report, was collaboratively produced by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting and released on 15th December 2021. It updates and extends a previous 180-page analytical component of the Generator Report Card 2018. This independent report examines historical data from the National Electricity Market (NEM) to provide insights for future developments, particularly focusing on the transition towards 100% renewable energy and the impacts of rooftop PV on utility-scale solar farms. The report is organized into summaries, aggregations, and 'Deep Dive' appendices, providing valuable insights into the complexities and challenges of energy transition in the NEM. Its relevance level is: Level 3; Its category is: Energy policy; its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown; its root_cause is: Release of analytical insights into NEM transition and renewable energy; its accident scale is: Unknown; its dates are: 15-12-2021; its affected region is: Unknown; its affected key users are: Unknown; its cause type is: Analytical insights; its causes are: Challenges of transitioning to renewable energy, impact of rooftop PV on commercial generation, and the complexities associated with the energy transition.

title: A quick look at the operation of Hornsdale Power Reserve (in ENERGY) for Monday 13th December 2021
author: Paul McArdle

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low points remain (stubbornly low) in NEM-wide wind harvest" posted at 14-12-2021 01:44:00 PM is: The article highlights the persistently low levels of wind energy production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) amidst high solar generation, with wind output hitting a low point of around 300MW. While improvements are expected with new capacities, the article notes that wind generation still represents a small percentage of the total installed capacity.
