title: Part 2 – What’s running in Victoria today (apart from strong rooftop PV)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Part 2 – What’s running in Victoria today (apart from strong rooftop PV)?" posted at 31-12-2023 12:47:00 PM is: The article discusses the exceptionally low market demand for electricity in Victoria on December 31, 2023, which has reached levels below previous records. Electricity supply is primarily from rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems and brown coal units operating near their minimum load. Prices in both Victoria and South Australia are low, dissuading additional generation sources from operating. Although the grid currently has sufficient inertia to remain stable, the high output from rooftop PV systems poses the risk of coal units going offline if demand drops further, potentially destabilizing the grid's operational state. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing. Its root cause is: Excessive rooftop PV generation reducing demand for other sources, leading to minimum load challenges and potential grid stability issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-12-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity producers and consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Systemic operational issue. Its causes are: Low electricity demand, high rooftop PV output, and coal units operating at minimum load contributing to potential grid instability.

title: Demand in Victoria drops to a new lowest point on Sunday 31st December 2023, and AEMO flags possible intervention (Part 1?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand in Victoria drops to a new lowest point on Sunday 31st December 2023, and AEMO flags possible intervention (Part 1?)" posted at 31-12-2023 11:28:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reported a record low in market demand in Victoria, reaching 1,615 MW, below the secure threshold of 2,000 MW, mainly due to an increase in rooftop solar PV output. This posed a risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state from 11:00 to 14:00. Consequently, AEMO may need to intervene by directing scheduled generation, curtailing non-scheduled generation, or increasing operational demand to maintain system security; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (same day as the event). Callide C4, which has been offline since an explosion on 25th May 2021, is expected to return by 19th May 2024. Recent developments include court proceedings initiated by Sev.en seeking further investigation into the explosion, potentially paving the way for claims against CS Energy and the Queensland government.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. title: Looking back at the high demand levels seen in the past two days in Queensland
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking back at the high demand levels seen in the past two days in Queensland" posted at 30-12-2023 03:18:00 PM is: The news highlights the exceptionally high electricity demand experienced in Queensland on the 28th and 29th of December, 2023. Despite the cooler temperatures on the 30th of December leading to reduced demand, the days prior witnessed peak 'Market Demand' levels, approaching all-time highs, due to seasonal factors and disruptions from recent weather events. Notably, this demand surge occurred during a typically low-activity holiday period. The peak demands were mainly due to reduced solar contributions as the sun set and continued high consumption despite storm and flood-induced outages. The situation led to spot price volatility triggered by the limited supply options. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, spanning the affected days. Its root_cause is: Seasonal demand surge magnified by weather-related outages and reduced solar contributions after sunset. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-12-2023, 29-12-2023, and 30-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, particularly southeast and Far North regions. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland, with attention to those relying on solar and grid supply. Its cause type is: Demand-related. Its causes are: High demand during holiday period, storm and flood outages, reduced solar generation after peak sunlight, and resultant supply-demand imbalances.

title: Queensland electricity demand climbs higher still, on Friday 29th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland electricity demand climbs higher still, on Friday 29th December 2023" posted at 29-12-2023 05:36:00 PM is: The article discusses the rise in electricity demand in Queensland, reaching a peak of 9,773MW, surpassing the previous day's high of 9,753MW. Remarkably, this demand occurred during the typically low activity period between Christmas and New Year and was further complicated by power outages in several regions due to recent storms and flooding. Additionally, spot prices spiked to $14,936.02/MWh as demand increased with the decline in solar output. The supply was stretched further with the unplanned trip of the Tarong North coal-fired unit amid low wind production. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: High demand coinciding with reduced solar output and a generator trip. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 29-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-induced and operational. Its causes are: High demand due to holiday period, reduction in solar power availability, storms and flooding power outages, generator trip, and low wind production.

author: Paul McArdle

This surge is notable considering the typically low activity period between Christmas and New Year, suggesting continued strain on the electricity market. title: QLD ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 9,500MW on Thursday 28th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 9,500MW on Thursday 28th December 2023" posted at 28-12-2023 05:29:00 PM is: The electricity market demand in Queensland reached a significant peak of approximately 9,753MW on December 28th, 2023, amid high temperatures in Brisbane and reduced commercial and industrial activity due to the holiday period. This demand was notably close to breaking the all-time record of 10,119MW set in March 2022. The demand levels were influenced by approximately 50,000 homes in the Gold Coast area still being without power following Christmas storms. The situation also saw some price volatility resulting from the setting of solar generation and the introduction of various peaking power sources. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand driven by extreme weather conditions. title: An initial exploration of QLD’s spike at 14:30 on Thursday 21st December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "An initial exploration of QLD’s spike at 14:30 on Thursday 21st December 2023" posted at 22-12-2023 02:12:00 PM is: On December 21, 2023, an unexpected price spike to $4,164.92/MWh occurred in Queensland at 14:30. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reported a non-credible contingency event involving the trip of circuit breakers in the QLD region. The main contributor to the price increase was identified as a reduction in the QNI interconnector's target flow. Lightning near Armidale caused AEMO to reclassify potential simultaneous trips of power lines as credible, invoking new constraints that affected the interconnector's capacity. The Middle Ridge circuit breaker trips also coincided with the event, although lightning around Armidale was the more significant factor. No load shedding was instructed, and the cause remains unknown.

Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 21/12/2023 until further notice. Its root_cause is: Lightning-induced credible contingency event and grid constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21/12/2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), and New South Wales (NSW1). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related and grid constraint. Its causes are: Lightning-induced credible contingency classification, trip of circuit breakers at Middle Ridge, QNI interconnector constraint, reclassification of power line trips due to weather conditions.

title: Very fast FCAS markets continue to grow
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very fast FCAS markets continue to grow" posted at 22-12-2023 01:53:00 PM is: The introduction of the 1-Second Raise and 1-Second Lower contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets since 9 October has witnessed significant growth in both the capacity and enabled volumes. As of late December, the RAISE1SEC capacity has exceeded 700MW, while LOWER1SEC capacity is approaching 600MW. Regular assessments by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) have progressively increased requirement caps to ensure adequate availability, with RAISE1SEC’s cap now set at 225MW and LOWER1SEC’s at 100MW. Availability generally surpasses requirement levels, but uncapped RAISE1SEC requirements remain high, highlighting the importance of these caps. A notable diurnal pattern is observed for RAISE1SEC requirements, influenced by PV generation and grid demand variations. Additionally, the Victorian Big Battery's commitment to the System Integrity Protection Scheme affects availability during specific periods, partly compensated by the Torrens Island Battery's deployment. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 9 October 2023 onwards. Its root_cause is: Implementation and adjustments in the FCAS markets. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9 October 2023 to 22 December 2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy providers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Market adjustments and operational planning. Its causes are: Introduction of 1-Second Raise and Lower FCAS markets, adjustments to requirement caps by AEMO, diurnal pattern due to PV generation and grid demand, and changes in availability due to battery obligations.

title: Barron Gorge Hydro Station damaged and offline after flooding from Cyclone Japser in North Queensland
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Barron Gorge Hydro Station damaged and offline after flooding from Cyclone Japser in North Queensland" posted at 20-12-2023 03:23:00 PM is: The Barron Gorge Hydro power station in North Queensland has been rendered inoperable due to damage from flooding caused by ex-tropical Cyclone Jasper, specifically the destruction of the water intake structure at the Kuranda Weir. This damage has left both units offline since December 18, 2023, with operations expected to resume by July 1, 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until July 1, 2024. Its root_cause is: Flooding caused by Cyclone Jasper. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: December 18, 2023. Its affected region is: North Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Water intake structure failure due to flooding.

title: Consultation on ‘Orderly Exit Management Framework Framework’ for coal units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Consultation on ‘Orderly Exit Management Framework Framework’ for coal units" posted at 16-12-2023 12:19:00 PM is: The New South Wales Government has released a consultation paper on an opt-in Orderly Exit Management (OEM) Framework designed to manage the transition of coal units in the National Electricity Market (NEM). This framework aims to extend the operational timeframe of thermal generators temporarily to secure energy reliability during the transition to cleaner technologies and an enhanced transmission network. While part of a Federal Government communication, the framework serves to ensure a structured energy market transition, with consultation open until 2 February 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Intentional policy development for market transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24 November 2023 (start of consultation) to 2 February 2024 (end of consultation), main date same as posted date. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy stakeholders and policymakers. Its cause type is: Policy/Administrative measure. Its causes are: Government initiative to manage coal unit transition, consultation for maintaining energy reliability and security.

title: AEMO forecast for LOR2 in NSW on Tuesday 19th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecast for LOR2 in NSW on Tuesday 19th December 2023" posted at 15-12-2023 10:11:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast for a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in New South Wales for Tuesday, 19th December 2023. This forecast indicates that between 2:30 PM and 4:00 PM on that day, the capacity reserve requirement will be 730 MW, with only 232 MW available, suggesting a significant shortfall. AEMO is seeking a market response to address this potential reserve shortfall, although it has not yet determined whether it will need to intervene directly. The situation follows a similar LOR2 condition the previous day, raising concerns for the summer period of 2023-24.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability . Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (specific date of 19th December 2023). Its root_cause is: Anticipated insufficient reserve capacity in the grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-12-2023, 19-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast grid reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Anticipated reserve shortfall of 498 MW (capacity requirement of 730 MW vs. available reserve of 232 MW) on 19th December 2023.

title: AEMO released draft of 2024 Integrated System Plan … cue the media headlines
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO released draft of 2024 Integrated System Plan … cue the media headlines" posted at 15-12-2023 08:35:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published the draft of its 2024 Integrated System Plan (ISP), emphasizing the need for urgent investment in electricity infrastructure to ensure reliable and secure energy. The draft suggests that significant expansion in firming capacity from dispatchable storage, hydro, and gas-powered generation is necessary by 2050. The release has sparked extensive media coverage and public discussion, highlighting challenges such as the rapid reduction of coal power and the urgency in building replacement capacities. The draft ISP is open for stakeholder consultation until mid-February, with a final version expected by June 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Medium to Long-term (up to 2050). Its root cause is: Strategic planning for future energy infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 15-12-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Industry stakeholders, government, energy consumers. Its cause type is: Policy and planning. Its causes is: Release of draft ISP by AEMO emphasizing investment needs for future energy infrastructure, media reaction, stakeholder consultations.

title: Disallowed flow north on VIC1-NSW1 another significant factor in tight supply-demand in NSW + QLD
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Disallowed flow north on VIC1-NSW1 another significant factor in tight supply-demand in NSW + QLD" posted at 14-12-2023 07:14:00 PM is: The article discusses complications arising from the VIC-NSW interconnector, which has been unable to flow north due to constraints, leading to tight supply-demand conditions in New South Wales and Queensland. With multiple constraint equations invoked, including 'N>>NIL_970_051', 'N>>NIL_4', and 'N>>NIL_998', the interconnector was effectively prevented from exporting electricity northward over a crucial period. This restriction could have contributed to the necessity of activating the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) mechanism. The inability to capture south-westerly generation efficiently to the Sydney load center is highlighted as a key issue; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Transmission constraints and negative settlement residues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Interconnector flow restriction. Its causes are: Negative settlement residues, multiple constraint equations, inability to export northward on the VIC-NSW interconnector.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind production ramps down in NSW, coincident with Increased Demand (and then Actual LOR2)" posted at 14-12-2023 06:34:00 PM is: The report highlights a significant reduction in wind power generation in New South Wales (NSW), which contributed to the region reaching 'Actual LOR2' status due to increased electricity demand and other factors, such as restricted energy flow between Victoria and NSW. Between 15:45 and 18:25, wind production declined significantly from 1,463MW to 918MW, exacerbating the supply-demand gap in NSW. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural variability in wind generation. Its causes are: Rapid drop in wind power generation, increased electricity demand, restricted energy flow between regions.

title: Actual LOR2 in NSW from 17:30 on Thursday 14th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual LOR2 in NSW from 17:30 on Thursday 14th December 2023" posted at 14-12-2023 06:15:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in New South Wales due to increased market demand on 14 December 2023. This emergency reserve notice was issued due to the reserve capacity required being 690 MW, while the minimum available was only 678 MW. However, the condition was canceled shortly at 18:10 after market response proved sufficient to address the deficit; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on 14 December 2023 but resolved within approximately 40 minutes. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Operational/system condition triggered by high demand. title: NSW ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 13,000MW – and IRPM drops – but prices stay moderate
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ climbs past 13,000MW – and IRPM drops – but prices stay moderate" posted at 14-12-2023 06:02:00 PM is: In New South Wales (NSW), the electricity market demand has risen above 13,000MW, yet the spot prices remain moderate, contrary to earlier predictions. The Inter-regional Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) between New South Wales and Queensland has decreased to 14% due to constrained interconnectors preventing flow between VIC1-NSW1 regions. The highest point for the electricity price today reached $662.97/MWh.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term impact. Its root_cause is: Constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector and demand pressure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland interconnected regions. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Grid/system operational constraints. Its causes is: High market demand, interconnector constraints due to grid stability equations, and moderate price forecasts.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ squeezes 12,500MW as price passes $500/MWh" posted at 14-12-2023 04:54:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant market demand in New South Wales (NSW), where the electricity demand peaked at 12,464MW, leading to a spot price exceeding $500/MWh. Initially expected to reach 13,000MW, the demand forecast was revised down, and while a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) was initially warned, it was downgraded to LOR1, indicating a less severe than anticipated stress on the system; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT negotiations underway in NSW … at the same time as the NSW spot price is negative and forecast spikes have disappeared" posted at 14-12-2023 04:22:00 PM is: The article discusses the situation in the New South Wales electricity market where the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) was preparing to initiate Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) negotiations due to anticipated high demand on December 14, 2023. title: RERT negotiations commenced in NSW by AEMO on Thursday 14th December 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT negotiations commenced in NSW by AEMO on Thursday 14th December 2023" posted at 14-12-2023 03:50:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has initiated negotiations for Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) contracts in New South Wales (NSW) due to high electricity demand and tight supply conditions. The decision follows a market notice indicating the intention to seek additional reserves for the period between 15:30 and 19:30 on December 14, 2023. The region experienced a surge in market demand, exceeding 11,000MW, with forecasts nearing 13,000MW by evening, amidst high temperatures of around 39°C in Sydney. Its relevance level is: Level 2. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ over 11,000MW – and forecast evening demand warming back towards 13,000MW" posted at 14-12-2023 03:28:00 PM is: The article discusses the high electricity demand forecasted in New South Wales (NSW), with market demand surpassing 11,000MW and potentially reaching 13,000MW in the evening. Although there was a slight price increase earlier in the day, overall price volatility has decreased as forecast prices have been revised downward, indicating a positive market response. The article notes the persistence of a forecast Lack of Reserve (LOR2) condition but highlights that AEMO's forecast process aims to encourage increased capacity offers to stabilize prices; Its relevance level is: Level 2. author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at forecasts for available wind generation in NSW for this evening 14th December 2023" posted at 14-12-2023 12:30:00 PM is: The article discusses the forecast for wind generation availability in New South Wales (NSW) for the evening of December 14, 2023. It highlights a tight supply-demand balance in the region, with particular attention to the Period of Low Reserve (LOR2) conditions forecasted by AEMO, alongside generator outages. title: Demand forecast moderating for NSW for Thursday evening, 14th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand forecast moderating for NSW for Thursday evening, 14th December 2023" posted at 14-12-2023 11:17:00 AM is: The demand forecast for electricity in New South Wales for the evening of December 14, 2023, has moderated compared to earlier predictions. The updated forecast indicates that peak market demand is expected to reach 12,754 MW at 18:00, which shows a reduction from previous estimates. Although volatility is still anticipated, it is not projected to reach previous extreme levels, and the expected LOR2 (Lack of Reserve) conditions are forecasted to persist but for a shorter duration. The monitoring of these conditions will continue to ensure stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. title: Forecast supply-demand for NSW for Thursday evening 14th December 2023 remains tight
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast supply-demand for NSW for Thursday evening 14th December 2023 remains tight" posted at 13-12-2023 02:42:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has forecast a Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for New South Wales (NSW) expected between 3:30 PM and 7:30 PM on December 14, 2023. The market demand in the region is predicted to slightly exceed 13,000 MW, with a minimum available capacity reserve of 344 MW against a requirement of 1,259 MW. Despite this, most major generators will remain functional, except for the Mt Piper 2 unit undergoing unplanned maintenance. AEMO is seeking market response to balance this supply-demand tightness. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (specific event duration). Its root_cause is: High market demand coupled with limited reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 13-12-2023 and 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. title: AEMO requests generator recall information in NSW and QLD for this Thursday 14th December 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO requests generator recall information in NSW and QLD for this Thursday 14th December 2023" posted at 12-12-2023 11:58:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued market notices requesting generator recall information for New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) for December 14, 2023, indicating anticipated tight supply-demand conditions in these regions. A generator recall procedure allows AEMO to be prepared for potential intervention in the electricity market. The notices, published as notices 112200 and 112201, require generators to report on additional capacity that can be made available. The forecast includes a Lack of Reserve 2 (LOR2) condition for NSW between 15:30 and 20:00, while an initial LOR1 condition for QLD has been downgraded. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (around December 14, 2023). Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand conditions anticipated in NSW and QLD. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-12-2023 for notice issuance, with impact expected on 14-12-2023. Its affected region is: NSW and QLD. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Market operational procedures for supply-demand management. title: Generator Compliance Management – Is it the “monster under the bed”?
author: Aditya Upadhye
classified_content: The summary of the news "Generator Compliance Management – Is it the “monster under the bed”?" posted at 11-12-2023 12:23:00 PM is: The article discusses the importance of compliance management with Generator Performance Standards (GPS) for large-scale electricity generation assets. It emphasizes the recent focus by the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) on enforcing compliance with national electricity rules, resulting in significant penalties for non-compliance. The dynamics of technical non-compliance, such as inadequate processes and inadvertence, can lead to severe financial and reputational consequences for power generators. The article highlights various instances of penalties imposed on companies like AGL Energy Ltd and Hornsdale Power Reserve for breaches related to compliance with dispatch instructions and frequency control ancillary services. The discussion includes risks related to non-compliance, such as potential damage to investor confidence and project viability, and suggests proactive compliance management as a strategic approach. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, as compliance issues can continuously impact the market. Its root_cause is: Compliance issues with Generator Performance Standards. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31st October 2023 and 28th June 2022 refer to specific incidents cited in the article. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity generators and market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory and operational non-compliance. Its causes are: Insufficient processes, practices, and inadvertence in complying with dispatch instructions and performance standards.

title: NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ peaks above 30,000MW on Friday 8th December 2023 … first time since July
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ peaks above 30,000MW on Friday 8th December 2023 … first time since July" posted at 08-12-2023 08:03:00 PM is: The article discusses the significant rise in NEM-wide market demand, which peaked at 30,034MW at 17:05 NEM time on December 8, 2023. This is the first instance of such a peak since July 24, 2023. Both the New South Wales and Queensland regions largely contributed to this surge in demand, with NSW reaching 11,673MW and QLD reaching 8,728MW. The rise in demand marks the beginning of the summer season, suggesting the potential for demand levels to climb higher as the season progresses; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially continuing throughout summer 2023-24. Its root_cause is: Increased electricity consumption marking the start of summer. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-12-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market behavior. Its causes are: Seasonal increase in demand, higher electricity consumption in NSW and QLD.

title: Sydney forecast to become ‘island of extreme heat’ tomorrow Saturday 9th of December 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sydney forecast to become ‘island of extreme heat’ tomorrow Saturday 9th of December 2023" posted at 08-12-2023 04:29:00 PM is: The article reports an anticipated extreme heat event in Sydney, New South Wales, expected to occur on December 9, 2023. Temperatures in the Sydney metropolitan area are forecasted to reach between 44°C to 48°C due to urban heat island and foehn effects, potentially leading to exceptionally high electricity demand in the region. The P30 forecast anticipates a total demand of 12,344 MW in NSW by 5:30 pm NEM time, highlighting the potential strain on the electricity market. Its relevance level is: Level 1. title: Price volatility also in NSW on Friday 8th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility also in NSW on Friday 8th December 2023" posted at 08-12-2023 04:07:00 PM is: The article reports significant price spikes in NSW on 8th December 2023, occurring at 10:05 AM ($14,248.59/MWh) and 14:50 PM ($10,485.97/MWh) due to various transmission constraints and drops in wind output. The constraints impacted interconnector flows and led to a reduction in wind power generation, contributing to the extreme price levels. The constrained units include BOCOROCK, GULLRGWF, and COLWF, and the outages on the Ravine to Yass line invoked constraint equations, exacerbating the price spikes. Future demand spikes are anticipated as market demand might peak above 30,000MW later in the day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Likely same day as the spikes occurred and possibly extending due to ongoing transmission constraints. Its root_cause is: Transmission constraints and wind output reduction. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-12-2023. Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users are: Key electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Physical infrastructure and operational constraints. Its causes are: Transmission constraints, reduction in wind output, invocation of constraint equations due to line outage.

author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Run of extreme prices in the SA region on Friday 8th December 2023" posted at 08-12-2023 12:19:00 PM is: The article highlights a significant spike in electricity prices in South Australia on December 8, 2023, with prices nearing the market cap due to severe weather conditions. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) invoked the ‘I-VS_250’ constraint set following the classification of potential non-credible contingency events as credible, citing thunderstorms, elevated temperatures, and threats of bushfires as contributing factors. Extreme weather has been identified as a key driver of these price fluctuations. The market notice issued at 10:50 am declared ongoing monitoring for further security measures; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Thunderstorms, elevated temperatures, damaging winds, and bushfire threats.

title: Case Study (Part 2) of Low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (Part 2) of Low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023" posted at 07-12-2023 09:30:00 AM is: The article discusses a significant event during the first week of July 2023, when the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia experienced sustained low levels of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) penetration. During July 3rd and 4th, VRE supplied less than 10% of total demand more than half of the time due to low solar and wind production, exacerbated by increased underlying demand. Cloud cover and low wind speeds across eastern Australia contributed to reduced solar irradiance and wind generation, highlighting the vulnerability of VRE-dependent systems during such 'Dunkelflaute' conditions. While forecasts anticipated different outcomes, actual VRE availability was significantly lower, stressing the need for system planners to address these challenges. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: July 3rd and 4th, 2023. Its root cause is: Extended cloud cover and low wind speeds impacting solar and wind generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: July 3rd and 4th, 2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, particularly eastern Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Low solar irradiance due to cloud cover, reduced wind speeds due to a high-pressure system.

title: Return to service for Callide C3 delayed by 17 days (now to 24th January 2024)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Return to service for Callide C3 delayed by 17 days (now to 24th January 2024)" posted at 06-12-2023 03:59:00 PM is: The return-to-service for the Callide C3 unit has been postponed by 17 days, now expected on January 24, 2024, due to various factors including recent rain, hot weather, resource availability issues, COVID-19 impact, and global supply chain disruptions attributed partially to international humanitarian efforts and domestic challenges in India. This delay brings the timeline closer to the expected high-demand summer period. The investigation into Callide C4's failure by Dr. Sean Brady has no new updates. CS Energy administers the units, and both Callide C3 and C4 remain key focal points in the electricity market due to their prolonged outages following incidents since 2021 for C4 and 2022 for C3. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Expected to impact during early 2024, especially summer. Its root_cause is: Cooling tower failure, adverse weather, resourcing issues, and supply chain disruptions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-12-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown (though Callide Power Station is in Queensland, Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational delays due to infrastructure and external challenges. Its causes are: Cooling tower failure, adverse weather conditions, COVID-19, and global supply chain issues.

title: Case Study – Severe Weather Challenges Day-Ahead Forecasts for 7, 8, 9 September 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study – Severe Weather Challenges Day-Ahead Forecasts for 7, 8, 9 September 2023" posted at 06-12-2023 10:00:00 AM is: The article examines the impact of severe weather conditions from September 7th to 9th, 2023, on electricity forecasts in south-eastern Australia. During this period, strong winds and storms associated with a low-pressure system disrupted forecast accuracy for operational demand, wind, and solar power availability. Specifically, on September 8th, operational demand exceeded forecasts by over 2,000 MW due to lower-than-expected rooftop PV performance and colder temperatures increasing demand for heating. Wind and solar availability showed significant deviations from forecasts over the three days, influenced by local weather patterns and cloud cover. The case study highlights the challenges in predicting electricity market behavior under extreme weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during and slightly after the event from September 7-9, 2023. Its root_cause is: Severe weather conditions caused by a low-pressure system leading to forecast inaccuracies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: September 7-9, 2023. Its affected region is: South-eastern Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market operators and consumers in the NEM (National Electricity Market). Its cause type is: Natural (weather-related). Its causes are: Severe weather including strong winds, storms, lower-than-expected rooftop PV, and colder temperatures increasing demand.

title: Worth a read – ESIG Report ‘Weather Dataset Needs for Planning and Analyzing Modern Power Systems’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Worth a read – ESIG Report ‘Weather Dataset Needs for Planning and Analyzing Modern Power Systems’" posted at 06-12-2023 08:43:00 AM is: The article discusses insights from an ESIG Webinar, highlighting the increasing dependence of modern power systems, such as the North American grid and the NEM, on weather patterns. The discussion emphasized challenges in weather modeling critical for planning and operational forecasting of renewable energy generation. Key points from Justin Sharp include the necessity for better planning to mitigate variability, challenges with modeling weather data, and the complexity of wind and solar forecasting. The focus was on needing accurate datasets to assist in managing the uncertainties inherent in renewable energy generation and its impact on the power grid.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Growing importance of accurate weather data for power system planning and analysis. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its date is: 06-12-2023. Its affected region is: Worldwide, with a focus on North America and the NEM. Its affected key users is: Energy planners and analysts. Its cause type is: Data and modelling challenges in weather-dependent power systems. Its causes is: Challenges in accurate weather forecasting, increasing reliance on weather-dependent renewable energy sources, and the need for precise data in power system planning and analysis.

title: Brief period of spot volatility in QLD and NSW on Tuesday evening 5th December 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief period of spot volatility in QLD and NSW on Tuesday evening 5th December 2023" posted at 05-12-2023 06:58:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced a brief period of spot price volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on the evening of December 5, 2023. This volatility was linked to elevated demand levels and logistical constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, which formed an 'Economic Island' and resulted in an Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) of less than 15%. Additionally, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) low reserve condition for NSW. A prior dispatch process glitch was noted as contributing to the issue. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector and AEMO dispatch process glitch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, elevated demand levels, and a glitch in AEMO's dispatch process.

title: Rooftop Solar is crushing* the returns for Large-Scale VRE
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Rooftop Solar is crushing* the returns for Large-Scale VRE" posted at 05-12-2023 10:30:00 AM is: The report discusses how rooftop solar installations have significantly affected the returns of large-scale Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) generators for the third quarter of 2023, highlighting the stark difference between Volume-Weighted Average (VWA) prices and Time-Weighted Average (TWA) prices across regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The report points out the particularly low VWA prices for large solar farms compared to TWA prices and notes the occurrence of negative VWA prices in some regions. This trend underscores the challenges of the 'Solar Correlation Penalty' and the inadequacy of the Capacity Factor metric for comparing solar farm performance. Wind farms faced similar issues, though with a less severe impact, due to the 'Wind Correlation Penalty', particularly in South Australia and Victoria. The discussion also includes varying opinions on incentivizing rooftop PV and the implications of potential policy changes. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing in Q3 2023. Its root_cause is: Rooftop solar installations affecting VRE prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2023. Its affected region is: Nationwide across the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Large-scale VRE operators. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Dominance of rooftop PV installations, lower VWA prices for large-scale VREs, solar and wind correlation penalties.

title: GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q3 2023 released

classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q3 2023 released" posted at 05-12-2023 10:00:00 AM is: The GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q3 2023, collaboratively produced by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting, is now available. Its appendices analyze historical data and discuss issues like demand forecasting, market interventions, and coal unit reliability. The update also provides insights into the evolving challenges of integrating renewable resources and managing grid stability. Readers interested in these reports can order an integrated PDF or an Executive Briefing for further insights. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Analysis of quarterly energy market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Subscribers and stakeholders in the NEM. Its cause type is: Analytical report release. Its causes is: Collaborative effort to provide detailed insights into the Q3 2023 NEM dynamics.

title: SA Islanding: Managing solar on the island
author: Blake Ashhton
classified_content: The summary of the news "SA Islanding: Managing solar on the island" posted at 01-12-2023 09:30:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges and strategies involved in managing South Australia's electricity grid as it becomes "islanded" during high-rooftop-solar-generation periods, especially when disconnected from the Victorian Heywood interconnector. Notably, during a severe storm event in November 2022 that knocked out the interconnector for nearly a week, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had to curtail solar power to maintain grid stability as variable renewable energy peaked at over 91%. SA Power Networks employed several measures, including directly instructing connected solar generators to shut down, using the government's Relevant Agent arrangements for remote disconnection, and adjusting substation voltages to manage older systems. The ongoing development of the Flexible Exports option aims to optimize energy flows by employing Dynamic Operating Envelopes, enhancing SA's capacity to handle high renewable energy penetration. The state's plans for the new SA/NSW transmission interconnector to be completed by 2026 are also mentioned as a future safeguard. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term during the outage and long-term with the introduction of new measures. Its root_cause is: Severe storm causing the loss of the Heywood interconnector. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: November 2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in South Australia, especially those with rooftop solar installations. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Severe storm event leading to the loss of the Heywood interconnector, resulting in the requirement for grid stability management measures like solar curtailment.

title: Do we really need Eraring when we’ll have 5,500MW of batteries by 2026?
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "Do we really need Eraring when we’ll have 5,500MW of batteries by 2026?" posted at 30-11-2023 03:00:00 PM is: The article discusses the future of the Eraring Power Station in the context of increasing battery storage capacities and renewable energy sources in New South Wales (NSW). With the NSW Government's Capacity Investment Scheme awarding contracts for 1,075MW of batteries and 2,980MWh of demand response projects, along with upcoming wind and solar projects, the article argues that the closure of the Eraring Power Station might not have as detrimental an impact on the state's power supply as previously feared. New renewable capacities are projected to outpace the generation lost from closing both the Eraring and Liddell stations, addressing concerns over supply gaps and potential blackouts. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: By 2026, when the new battery and renewable capacities are operational. Root_cause is: Transition from coal to renewable energy sources and battery storage. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates is: 30-11-2023. Affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Affected key users is: General electricity consumers in NSW. Cause type is: Energy transition policy. Causes is: Closure of Eraring and Liddell power stations, government policies on renewable energy, delays in new transmission projects, introduction of new battery storage capacities.

title: What’s happening around Wagga?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s happening around Wagga?" posted at 30-11-2023 09:30:00 AM is: The article discusses ongoing issues within the National Electricity Market (NEM) related to counter-intuitive energy flows between New South Wales (NSW) and Victoria. The core problem centers on consistent southward flows of electricity from more expensive NSW into cheaper Victoria, driven by limitations in the transmission infrastructure, especially around Wagga, where increased solar generation is affecting the capability of the existing network to manage power flows securely. These constraints, particularly related to the 051 line and the 132kV network, result in significant negative financial settlements termed "inter-regional settlement residues" (IRSR). The article suggests potential solutions such as network upgrades or implementing a "runback scheme" to curtail generation during grid stress, but notes that these issues may persist in the absence of substantial infrastructure enhancements. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing. Its root_cause is: Transmission constraints around Wagga. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 3 November 2023 (specific examples given). Its affected region is: Southern New South Wales and Northern Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Structural and operational inefficiencies. Its causes are: Increased solar generation around Wagga, outdated transmission network capacity, 051 line constraint, and counterintuitive dispatch outcomes.

title: Thunderstorms in South Australia impact ~30,000 electricity customers on Tuesday 28th November 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Thunderstorms in South Australia impact ~30,000 electricity customers on Tuesday 28th November 2023" posted at 29-11-2023 02:51:00 PM is: Super-cell thunderstorms hit Adelaide and surrounding regions in the early hours of Tuesday, resulting in severe power outages affecting approximately 30,000 customers due to unplanned outages and trips in the electricity network. Significant market events coupled with the weather conditions led to a spike in the energy price ceiling of $16,600/MWh as system constraints clamped energy exports from Victoria. By the evening, only 1,000 customers were still without power. Extreme weather conditions, confirmed by the ABC and Weatherzone, included flash flooding, with over 43,000 lightning strikes recorded, marking the wettest November day in 18 years for Adelaide. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact on the same day, persisting potentially for days. Its root_cause is: Super-cell thunderstorms causing equipment failures and network strain. Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting approximately 30,000 customers. Its dates are: 28th November 2023 for the main events. Its affected region is: South East South Australia, including Adelaide and surrounding towns. Its affected key users are: Approximately 30,000 electricity customers. Its cause type is: Natural event (extreme weather). Its causes are: Super-cell thunderstorms, flash flooding, and multiple network trips and unplanned outages.

title: Federal Government announces expansion of the Capacity Investment Scheme on 23rd November 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Federal Government announces expansion of the Capacity Investment Scheme on 23rd November 2023" posted at 23-11-2023 02:01:00 PM is: The Federal Government, led by Climate Change and Energy Minister Chris Bowen, has announced a significant expansion of the Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) along with the National Energy Transformation Partnership (NETP), aiming to transition from the current Renewable Energy Target scheme set to end in 2030. The expanded CIS will introduce 9 GW of dispatchable and 23 GW of variable capacity, totaling 32 GW nationwide, with half being subject to state negotiations. Various industry leaders have expressed support, noting the potential positive impacts on investment, electricity prices, and reliability, though some have flagged risks and areas for further work. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, likely through and beyond 2030. Its root cause is: Government policy changes aimed at supporting renewable energy investment. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-11-2023. Its affected region is: National (Australia). Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and investors. Its cause type is: Strategic policy decision. Its causes are: Expiration of the Renewable Energy Target Scheme, the need for increased renewable capacity, and the Federal Government's renewable goals.

title: AEMO holds 2023-24 Summer Readiness Briefing on November 13th 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO holds 2023-24 Summer Readiness Briefing on November 13th 2023" posted at 16-11-2023 11:10:00 AM is: The AEMO conducted its annual Summer Readiness Industry Briefing on November 13th, 2023, focusing on electricity and gas system readiness in anticipation of the 2023/24 summer. Presentations covered a range of topics including weather forecasts, network readiness, and bushfire preparations, highlighting heightened risks of blackouts due to extreme power demand driven by a strong El Nino. The session emphasized the role of renewables and battery storage in managing potential electricity grid stress. Media reports expressed confidence in the energy system's ability to withstand challenges if current plans succeed. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 16-11-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural and market dynamics. Its causes is: Strong El Nino conditions, increased electricity demand, power system preparations.

title: Case Study (part 1) of Aggregate Scheduled Target on Friday 8th September 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (part 1) of Aggregate Scheduled Target on Friday 8th September 2023" posted at 14-11-2023 12:29:00 PM is: The article discusses a notable event in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during Q3 2023, specifically a significant 5-minute increase in the Aggregate Scheduled Target (+960MW) between 13:35 and 13:40 on 8th September 2023. This spike is connected with a notable drop in the aggregate output from large solar units in New South Wales (NSW) within a 10-minute window, which likely caused fully scheduled units to dispatch more electricity. The NSW region price experienced a decline during this period, dropping to just below $0/MWh by 13:40 due to these market dynamics; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within the day of the event). Its root_cause is: Drop in solar output leading to adjustments in scheduled electricity dispatch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 8th September 2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational adjustment due to solar output variance. Its causes are: Drop in large solar output, resulting in dispatch adjustments to meet electricity demand.

title: Forecast low System Strength of concern in NSW for Tuesday 14th November 2023 … first time ever?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast low System Strength of concern in NSW for Tuesday 14th November 2023 … first time ever?" posted at 13-11-2023 03:54:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a Market Notice indicating a possible intervention in the New South Wales (NSW) electricity region to manage power system security due to forecasted low system strength. The synchronous generating units expected to be online might be inadequate to maintain a secure operating state on the evening of November 14, 2023, prompting potential directions for additional units to operate. This scenario is more common in South Australia and sometimes Victoria, but unusual for NSW. Current inertia levels in NSW are low, and upcoming unplanned maintenance outages for key units might be contributing to the situation; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term, specifically on November 14, 2023. Its root_cause is: Insufficient system strength due to low inertia and potential inadequate synchronisation of generating units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-11-2023, with significant impact expected by 14-11-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and grid operators in NSW. Its cause type is: Operational/System security. Its causes are: Forecasted inadequate synchronisation of synchronous generating units, low current inertia levels, and upcoming unplanned maintenance outages.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria’s ‘Market Demand’ low point drops further–on Sunday 12th November 2023" posted at 12-11-2023 11:14:00 AM is: The article reports a significant drop in 'Market Demand' in Victoria on November 12, 2023. This event marks a further decline from the previously noted low point on September 16, 2023. 'Market Demand' reached a minimum of 1,724.66 MW, whereas 'Operational Demand' was notably higher at 2,119 MW for the half-hour ending at 13:00. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in South Australia on Thursday evening 9th Nov 2023" posted at 10-11-2023 12:31:00 PM is: The article discusses significant spot price volatility in South Australia on the evening of November 9, 2023. The interconnectors, 'V::S_SETB_TBSE_2' and 'V^^SML_NIL_3', were constrained, limiting power flow westward on Heywood and Murraylink, respectively. Contributing factors included the absence of solar generation since nighttime, and lower-than-expected wind generation. These conditions led to a shift of generation volume into higher-priced bid bands, highlighting a pivotal shift in market dynamics during this period. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Bigger or better: Are newer wind farms outperforming older ones?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bigger or better: Are newer wind farms outperforming older ones?" posted at 09-11-2023 11:56:00 AM is: The article examines the anticipation that advancements in wind farm technology will lead to higher capacity factors, thus improving efficiency over time. It discusses the CSIRO’s projections of increased future capacity factors for wind farms, yet analysis shows no consistent trend in improvement over the last two decades, with various factors like site scarcity and curtailment counteracting technological gains. Thus, newer wind farms are not necessarily outperforming older ones in terms of capacity factors. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study (part 1) of low % VRE NEM-wide on 3rd and 4th July 2023" posted at 06-11-2023 12:48:00 PM is: The article provides an analysis of the low percentage of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) supply across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on July 3rd and 4th, 2023. During these two days, VRE supply reached daily minimum levels of 3.4% and 3.8%, respectively, with maximum levels only at 27.1% and 20%. These values are notably lower than usual, with over half of the dispatch intervals recording VRE supply under 10% of the total. The focus on VRE percentage rather than total renewable percentage is emphasized to better understand challenges in the energy transition. The article suggests referring to the GenInsights 2023 Q3 report for a more comprehensive discussion on these metrics.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the 3rd and 4th of July 2023. Its root_cause is: Low VRE production due to circumstances within those days. Its affected key users are: NEM stakeholders and energy market participants. Its cause type is: Market and operational analysis. Its causes are: Low percentage of VRE supply across NEM, low VRE production on specified days.

title: Bouldercombe BESS charges for 2 hours at 5MW on Friday 3rd November 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bouldercombe BESS charges for 2 hours at 5MW on Friday 3rd November 2023" posted at 04-11-2023 12:19:00 PM is: The Bouldercombe Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in Queensland experienced controlled charging activity for 2 hours at 5MW on Friday, November 3rd, 2023. This charging event occurred as part of a broader initiative to commence final commissioning tests and operations following prior fire-related disruptions. Tesla had conducted a root cause analysis (RCA) remotely and subsequently through physical inspections on their affected Megapack unit. Genex Power indicated that 38 out of 40 Megapack units would be utilized for the recommencement of operations, anticipated by mid-November. Its relevance level is: Level 1.
