title: A run of high prices seen in SA this morning Wednesday 28th June 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A run of high prices seen in SA this morning Wednesday 28th June 2023" posted at 28-06-2023 11:28:00 AM is: High electricity prices were observed in South Australia on the morning of June 28th, 2023, peaking at $12,922.49/MWh at 07:55 (NEM time). Despite a forecasted Lack of Reserve (LOR2) condition from 7:00 am to 8:30 am, which did not materialize, price spikes occurred due to low wind generation and reduced interconnector flow between VIC and SA. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Low wind generation and limited interconnector flow. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-06-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Supply constraints. Its causes are: Low wind output, interconnector flow limitation.

title: LOR3 forecast flickers on and off for South Australia for tomorrow evening 27th of June 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "LOR3 forecast flickers on and off for South Australia for tomorrow evening 27th of June 2023" posted at 26-06-2023 03:44:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued and later downgraded a Lack of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) forecast for South Australia for the evening of 27th June 2023, indicating a tight supply-demand balance. The situation evolved with the latest forecasts showing a LOR2 notice for the same evening and another forecast for LOR2 on Thursday morning, indicating potential tight electricity supply conditions later in the week.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, impacting 27th June 2023 evening and potentially affecting conditions later in the week. Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand balance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-06-2023 (for the forecast and downgrade notice), and 27-06-2023 (for the impact). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System/ Grid issue. Its causes are: Oscillations in LOR forecasts, evolving supply-demand conditions in South Australia.

title: Increasing curtailment of Wind and Solar across the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Increasing curtailment of Wind and Solar across the NEM" posted at 24-06-2023 11:01:00 AM is: The article discusses the increasing occurrence of curtailment of Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) sources, specifically wind and solar, across the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. On the 24th of June 2023, wind and large solar production approached 10,000 MW, contributing to a supply of 18,584 MW across the NEM, which led to subdued prices in regions like QLD, VIC, and SA. Economically-induced curtailment is affecting prices negatively in these areas. The article also explores the historical trends of curtailment, noting a significant increase in both absolute and relative terms from Q1 2022 to Q1 2023. The key reasons for curtailment are market dynamics and optimization strategies by VRE operators, which complicate estimation efforts. The piece anticipates challenges for project business cases and market operations due to this trend. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2023. Its root_cause is: Economically-induced curtailment of wind and solar energy due to subdued demand and other market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-06-2023. Its affected region is: NEM regions of QLD, VIC, and SA. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic and market-driven. Its causes are: Increasing production of wind and solar energy, economically induced curtailment, market optimization by operators.

title: AEMO (oscillating in) forecasting LOR2 in South Australia on Tuesday 27th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO (oscillating in) forecasting LOR2 in South Australia on Tuesday 27th June 2023" posted at 23-06-2023 06:15:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) initially declared a Forecast Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for South Australia for the period from 18:30 to 19:00 on 27th June 2023, due to a slight shortfall in the capacity reserve requirement. However, this forecast was briefly canceled but then reinstated later the same day. This oscillation in reserve forecasts underscores the increasing risk and supply-demand tightness within the National Electricity Market (NEM).; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically the evening of 27th June 2023. Its root_cause is: Supply-demand uncertainty leading to reserve shortfall prediction. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23rd June 2023 (the notice date) and 27th June 2023 (the forecast date for the event). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted electricity reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Oscillations in supply-demand forecasts leading to tight reserve margins in South Australia.

title: Prices up in the mid-afternoon on Thursday 22nd June 2023 (a rare instance –  with solar generation lower)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Prices up in the mid-afternoon on Thursday 22nd June 2023 (a rare instance –  with solar generation lower)" posted at 22-06-2023 02:37:00 PM is: The article discusses an uncommon occurrence of increased electricity prices in the afternoon of June 22, 2023, attributed to reduced solar generation, which is 1,500 MW lower than usual. This drop in generation is partly due to cloud cover affecting solar farms in the southern NEM region, although the northern solar farms in Queensland remain unaffected.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Decreased solar generation due to cloud cover. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-06-2023. Its affected region is: Southern NEM region. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM region. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Reduced solar generation, cloud cover affecting solar farms.

title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Wed 21st June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Wed 21st June 2023" posted at 21-06-2023 05:49:00 PM is: Spot prices in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) experienced significant volatility on the evening of 21st June 2023, driven by a combination of cold winter weather increasing demand above 30,000MW and constrained exports on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. Wind energy contributed reasonably, helping to maintain a higher Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) compared to the previous day. The constraints led to elevated pricing actions in QLD and NSW, with some impacts in VIC and SA, and a brief period where the NEM-wide IRPM dropped below 15%. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Cold weather increasing demand and constraints on interconnectors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-06-2023; 20-06-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System and grid constraints. Its causes are: Increased winter demand, constrained VIC1-NSW1 interconnector due to network outage, insufficient solar output, and wind power contribution.

title: A cold evening drives NEM-wide demand high, and IRPM low, on Tuesday 20th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A cold evening drives NEM-wide demand high, and IRPM low, on Tuesday 20th June 2023" posted at 20-06-2023 06:14:00 PM is: On the evening of June 20, 2023, NEM-wide market demand surged to 32,238MW due to cold weather, particularly affecting TAS, NSW, and QLD, with available generation at 36,024MW and an Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) at a low 11.74%. This low IRPM and elevated demand, exacerbated by low variable renewable energy (VRE) yields, led to spot prices reaching $1,000/MWh across mainland regions, highlighting the need for more firming capacity amid the energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing. Its root_cause is: Elevated electricity demand and low VRE yield. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-06-2023. Its affected region is: TAS, NSW, QLD. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in mainland regions. Its cause type is: Weather and generation-related. Its causes are: Elevated demand due to cold weather, low solar and wind generation, insufficient dispatchable capacity.

title: What happened to the  ‘N::N_DTKV_2’ constraint equation leading to whiplash on VIC1-NSW1 interconnector on Sunday 18th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What happened to the ‘N::N_DTKV_2’ constraint equation leading to whiplash on VIC1-NSW1 interconnector on Sunday 18th June 2023" posted at 19-06-2023 04:25:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant shift in electricity flow on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector on June 18th, 2023, due to the 'N::N_DTKV_2' constraint equation involved in the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley line outage. This constraint limited exports from Victoria to New South Wales, causing a rapid change when the TUMUT3 hydro unit became a cheaper energy source, leading to a market price floor scenario; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate impact during the dispatch interval on June 18th, 2023). Its root_cause is: Outage on the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley 330kV line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2023. Its affected region is: VIC1-NSW1 interconnector area. Its affected key users are: Typically electricity market participants and operators. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure. Its causes are: Constraint equation 'N::N_DTKV_2', line outage on Dapto to Kangaroo Valley 330kV, market reaction to price changes.

title: Industrial Action at Longford
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Industrial Action at Longford" posted at 19-06-2023 12:01:00 PM is: The article discusses the resolution of a Protected Industrial Action (PIA) involving Esso Australia's Longford gas production, as announced by AEMO. Although the PIA initially affected operations, the gas production levels have returned to normal following discussions at the Fair Work Commission. The vessels Skandi Darwin and Skandi Feistein, which support gas production from Bass Strait operations, have been exempted from the PIA. However, the Skandi Darwin's work for maintenance and decommissioning remains subject to the PIA but has no expected impact on gas production.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as gas production has returned to planned levels. Its root_cause is: Protected Industrial Action affecting gas production operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-06-2023. Its affected region is: Longford, Bass Strait operations. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Industrial action. Its causes are: Industrial action affecting support operations for gas production vessels.

title: Whiplash on VIC1-NSW1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Whiplash on VIC1-NSW1" posted at 18-06-2023 07:40:00 PM is: The article describes significant changes in electricity flow across the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, where a reversal of 1,404MW in target flow occurred between the 17:00 and 17:05 dispatch intervals on 18th June 2023. At 17:00, 1,043MW was exported from Victoria to New South Wales, which reversed to 361MW being imported from New South Wales to Victoria by 17:05. This change was influenced by the ‘N::N_DTKV_2’ constraint due to an ongoing outage on the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley 330kV line. Simultaneously, NSW generation increased by 1,464MW, while Victoria's generation decreased by 818MW amid rising market demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Line constraint due to an outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2023. Its affected region is: VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Constraint-induced flow reversal. Its causes are: Reversal in target flow due to the 'N::N_DTKV_2' constraint, outage on the Dapto to Kangaroo Valley line.

title: Transmission outage of the Dapto -to- Kangaroo Valley transmission link in southern NSW (line 18) … and spot volatility
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Transmission outage of the Dapto -to- Kangaroo Valley transmission link in southern NSW (line 18) … and spot volatility" posted at 14-06-2023 01:27:00 PM is: The article discusses the transmission outage on the Dapto-Kangaroo Valley transmission link (‘line 18’) in southern NSW, which occurred from May 15 to June 3, 2023, and its impact on electricity spot price volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). This outage was identified as a contributor to the spot price fluctuations due to its limitation on secure northward power flows, inducing volatility during peak demand periods. The outage is part of a broader set of factors, including other transmission constraints and plant retirements like Liddell, impacting the electricity market. Constraint sets, including ‘N-DTKV_18_WG_CLOSE’ and ‘N-DTKV_18_WG_OPEN’, were invoked during this period, demonstrating the complexity of the National Electricity Market's operations. The outage resumed in June and is expected to cause potential volatility through the end of that month. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid-May to the end of June 2023. Its root_cause is: Transmission line outage. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 15-05-2023 to 03-06-2023 (initial outage) and resumed 13-06-2023 to 19-06-2023 and 30-06-2023. Its affected region is: Southern New South Wales, impacting interconnections with Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Infrastructure maintenance/outage. Its causes are: Prolonged transmission line outage, reduced flow capabilities, constraints from line limits, plant retirements, and generation limitations due to seasonal factors.

title: Spot price volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 12th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 12th June 2023" posted at 12-06-2023 07:09:00 PM is: Volatility in electricity spot prices in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) was observed on June 12, 2023, due to multiple factors including the outage of Kogan Creek and Millmerran 2 generators. This led to prices exceeding $1,000/MWh starting from the 17:10 dispatch interval. A key issue was the 'N>>NIL_33_34' transmission constraint, affecting interconnectors between VIC, NSW, and QLD, thereby preventing lower VIC prices from influencing NSW and QLD. Additionally, the decline in solar and wind energy contributions around sunset led to increased demand for alternative energy dispatch. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, on June 12, 2023. Its root_cause is: Outage of multiple generators and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-06-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Infrastructure and demand-related issues. Its causes are: Generator outages, transmission constraints, declining solar and wind energy contributions.

title: Four coal unit outages in the past 24 hours
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Four coal unit outages in the past 24 hours" posted at 12-06-2023 02:55:00 PM is: The article reports on the unexpected outages of four coal units within the past 24 hours in the National Electricity Market (NEM), specifically Kogan Creek, Loy Yang A2, and Yallourn units 3 and 4. These outages were not planned in advance, as evidenced by the lack of pre-determined outage forecasts. The outages contribute to the ongoing challenges faced by the NEM in managing the energy supply following the closure of the Liddell coal plant. Observations from the GenInsights Quarterly Update series indicate that while there is a significant portion of coal unit unavailability, there hasn't been an increase in the frequency of these trippings over time. Additionally, differences in planned maintenance schedules are highlighted between the units affected. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting current operations and potentially influencing short-term electricity supply reliability. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of coal units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-06-2023. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) region in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Unplanned outages of Kogan Creek, Loy Yang A2, Yallourn units 3 and 4.

title: Record for NEM-wide wind production nudged slightly higher on Thu 8th June 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Record for NEM-wide wind production nudged slightly higher on Thu 8th June 2023" posted at 09-06-2023 11:57:00 AM is: The article reports a new record for NEM-wide wind production on June 8, 2023, reaching 7,324 MW, surpassing the previous record set in August 2022. This wind production level had a significant share of over 30% of the grid demand. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared a "Protected Event" in South Australia due to destructive wind conditions on June 7, but this was later canceled. Despite operational challenges, such as potential transmission element losses, wind production is expected to increase further due to added capacity. The report emphasizes the potential for increased wind generation records in the coming months and encourages readers to follow trends using updated software tools. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Less Relevant. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High wind production reaching unprecedented levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 8th June 2023. Its affected region is: NEM-wide with specific constraints in South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational and environmental factors. Its causes are: High wind speeds, unmet generation forecasts, and additional wind capacity.

title: Another research paper into solar and wind droughts in Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another research paper into solar and wind droughts in Australia" posted at 08-06-2023 05:04:00 PM is: The article highlights the release of a new research paper from the UNSW Climate Change Research Centre that explores solar and wind energy droughts in Australia, analyzing periods when sunlight and wind are insufficient for energy generation. This research ties into previous studies and discussions on the impact of such renewable energy droughts on the electricity market, including insights from various prior studies. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Natural variations leading to low solar and wind energy output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-06-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural/Climate related. Its causes is: Insufficient sunlight and wind conditions affecting renewable energy output.

title: Reader question about current (planned) outage at Millmerran unit 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reader question about current (planned) outage at Millmerran unit 2" posted at 02-06-2023 02:04:00 PM is: The article discusses the planned outage at Millmerran unit 2, which is part of the black coal units covered by the ez2view software's 'Generator Outages' widget, showing current and near-term operational status. The Millmerran unit 2 outage, planned since July 2022, is forecast to be resolved by Monday, June 5, 2023, as confirmed by the MT PASA DUID Availability data set. The discussion notes the utility of new reporting features for identifying planned versus forced outages, now included in the GenInsights Quarterly Updates. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term (as the outage is planned to be resolved by June 5, 2023). Its root cause is: Planned maintenance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-06-2023 and likely resolved by 05-06-2023. Its affected region is: Millmerran, NEM (National Electricity Market), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical/Operational. Its causes are: Scheduled maintenance planned since July 2022.

title: AER writes ‘Stanwell fined for allegedly putting power system security and stability at risk’ … re May 2021 Callide Explosion
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER writes ‘Stanwell fined for allegedly putting power system security and stability at risk’ … re May 2021 Callide Explosion" posted at 01-06-2023 03:56:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has announced that Stanwell Corporation is fined for allegedly compromising power system security and stability due to the Callide C4 Explosion on May 25, 2021. Although infringement notices have been paid by Stanwell, this does not reflect an admission of liability. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its root_cause is: Alleged negligence in maintaining system security. Its accident scale is: Unknown. title: AEMO releases outlook for Winter 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases outlook for Winter 2023" posted at 01-06-2023 02:40:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released a media statement indicating an improved energy system outlook for winter 2023, although certain risks remain. The update highlights the interconnection between electricity and gas during the winter months, where higher demand for gas-fired heating and electricity generation could pose potential supply challenges. AEMO's reference to a previous media release from 16th March 2023 reinforces the ongoing concerns about gas supply. This information is crucial for various stakeholders; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Winter 2023. Its root_cause is: Increased demand for gas-fired heating and electricity generation during winter. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-06-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity and gas consumers, energy market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Potential fuel supply challenges. Its causes are: Interconnection of electricity and gas demand, higher winter heating requirements, potential gas shortages.

title: Digging further into ‘Aggregate Scheduled Target’, to understand what Dispatchable Capacity is actually required
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Digging further into ‘Aggregate Scheduled Target’, to understand what Dispatchable Capacity is actually required" posted at 31-05-2023 06:00:00 PM is: The article delves into the necessity of establishing sufficient dispatchable capacity to replace retiring coal plants, emphasizing the challenges of incentivizing new and existing dispatchable plants to participate in the market. It outlines trends in the Aggregate Scheduled Target (AggSchedTarget) from 2009 onward, revealing a decreasing demand for scheduled plants and increased unpredictability due to weather dependencies. The piece highlights different seasonal requirements, noting more variability in summer necessitating flexible commercial support, while winter demands are consistently predictable around peak evening hours when solar output fades. This comprehensive analysis stresses the importance of understanding the full opportunity for dispatchable capacity within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since the analysis covers years from 2009 to Q1 2023 and looks to the future. Its root_cause is: Transition from coal to renewable energy sources causing a shift in dispatchable capacity needs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-05-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) wide. Its affected key users are: Power companies and electricity market regulators. Its cause type is: Structural change in energy supply systems. Its causes are: Shortage of firming capacity, high dependency on weather conditions, retirement of coal plants, and rising renewable energy integration without adequate replacement of dispatchable capacity.

title: GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023 released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023 released" posted at 31-05-2023 02:49:00 PM is: Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting have collaboratively released the GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q1 2023 on 31st May 2023. This fourth report of its series, now 258 pages long, continues to explore various aspects of the energy transition in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). The update includes an Executive Summary with key observations and six detailed appendices that cover metrics like system inertia, grid demand changes, performance of wind and solar assets, dispatchable capacity analysis, coal unit operations, and network dynamics. These analyses are aimed at understanding the evolving challenges faced by the NEM in terms of energy production, reliability, and transition towards renewable resources. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to Long term. Its root_cause is: N/A. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-05-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity Market Analysts, Policymakers, Energy Consultants. Its cause type is: Analytic/Report Release. Its causes are: Detailed performance reports and analysis on NEM, including system inertia, grid demand, renewable energy integration, and coal unit dependability.

title: ABC says ‘coldest May minimum temperatures on record’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ABC says ‘coldest May minimum temperatures on record’" posted at 31-05-2023 01:09:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced volatility in May 2023, particularly in Queensland and New South Wales, with potential contributing factors being the coldest May minimum temperatures on record and commercial influences like the closure of the Liddell power plant units. This highlights the complexity of the NEM, where no single factor typically results in market fluctuations. Market volatility was observed on several dates in May, though similar issues were noted in South Australia; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: May 2023. Its root_cause is: Coldest May minimum temperatures and closure of Liddell units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st, 17th, 20th, 23rd-26th, and 30th May 2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related and infrastructure. Its causes are: Coldest May temperatures, Liddell power plant closure.

title: Spot volatility again, in QLD and NSW, on Tuesday evening 30th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot volatility again, in QLD and NSW, on Tuesday evening 30th May 2023" posted at 30-05-2023 06:36:00 PM is: The electricity markets in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) have experienced significant spot price volatility on the evening of May 30th, 2023, with prices spiking above $1,000/MWh. This recent trend of evening volatility has been occurring frequently over the last couple of weeks, with similar instances noted on multiple occasions earlier in May; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing in the short term. Its root_cause is: Increased demand and possible supply constraints during evening peak periods. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: May 30, 2023 (and earlier noted dates include May 17th, 20th, 23rd, 24th, 25th, and 26th, 2023). Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: High electricity demand during cooler weather and supply constraints leading to price spikes.

title: The never-ending (horror) story at Callide C … more long delays in expected Return-to-Service
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "The never-ending (horror) story at Callide C … more long delays in expected Return-to-Service" posted at 30-05-2023 11:25:00 AM is: The Callide C Power Station, which suffered a massive explosion outage on May 25, 2021, is experiencing further delays in its return to service for units C3 and C4. Originally scheduled to resume operations in late 2023, the timelines have now shifted considerably. Unit C3 is expected to partially return on January 7, 2024, and fully by February 18, 2024. Unit C4 faces more significant delays, with an initial return on May 19, 2024, and full capacity expected by July 6, 2024. These slippages are due to necessary rebuilds, especially of the cooling towers and other critical component replacements. The delays are likely to impact electricity markets by maintaining or exacerbating power price volatility due to reduced generation capacity in a critical period; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until mid-2024 and potentially longer due to unforeseen commissioning challenges. Its root_cause is: The explosion and outage at Callide C4 occurring on May 25, 2021. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same as the main date, with updates from March 8, 2023, and related to events on May 25, 2023, and back in 2021. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but likely regional electricity consumers and market participants. Its cause type is: System/component failure. Its causes are: Explosion incident at Callide C4, technical challenges in re-commissioning power generation units, and corporate/administrative hurdles related to ownership and management of the power station.

title: Spot Price volatility in QLD and NSW on Friday 26th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot Price volatility in QLD and NSW on Friday 26th May 2023" posted at 26-05-2023 07:06:00 PM is: The news reports on spot price volatility in Queensland and New South Wales, observing price spikes similar to those on several preceding days in May. title: Vedran’s note today, about ‘more wind than hydro in Tasmania’ for early morning Thursday 25th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Vedran’s note today, about ‘more wind than hydro in Tasmania’ for early morning Thursday 25th May 2023" posted at 26-05-2023 07:00:00 PM is: The article discusses a notable energy update from Vedran Kovac at Hydro Tasmania, highlighting that during an early morning period on Thursday, 25th May 2023, wind energy surpassed hydro energy in Tasmania. This observation was part of a broader analysis using NEMreview software and social media commentary, with a particular focus on a 5-hour time frame within the week. Imports to Tasmania through Basslink, which often consists of Victoria's brown coal, were repurposed and prominently displayed. This emphasizes Tasmania's increasing reliance on wind energy. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically a 5-hour period on 25th May 2023. Its root_cause is: A shift in energy production where wind energy output exceeded hydro energy. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-05-2023. Its affected region is: Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational dynamics in energy production. Its causes are: Increased wind energy production compared to hydro energy.

title: Spot market volatility on Thursday 25th May 2023 (and Actual LOR2 in NSW)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot market volatility on Thursday 25th May 2023 (and Actual LOR2 in NSW)" posted at 25-05-2023 05:30:00 PM is: The QLD and NSW regions experienced spot market volatility in the evening of 25th May 2023, following the sunset of solar generation. This volatility led to an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition being declared in the NSW region by the AEMO, noting a discrepancy between the forecast and available capacity reserves, which called for a prompt market response. The LOR2 condition began at 20:00 and ended at 20:45 due to a timing error, later corrected by AEMO notice.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact on the market, specifically during the evening of 25th May 2023. Its root_cause is: Insufficient reserve capacity post solar generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-05-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW requiring uninterrupted supply during peak hours. Its cause type is: Operational condition. Its causes are: Spot market volatility due to solar generation offset, LOR2 condition declaration demanding market response and possible intervention.

title: AEMO declares Scheduling Error (incident #54) with respect to post-Liddell snag
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO declares Scheduling Error (incident #54) with respect to post-Liddell snag" posted at 25-05-2023 04:41:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) identified a scheduling error related to the de-registration of Liddell Power Station, which led to unintended price spikes on May 1, 2023. The error occurred when constraints used default values causing overestimation of available capacity, reducing power dispatch and interconnector flow while increasing electricity prices in New South Wales (NSW). AEMO corrected the constraint settings to prevent further issues. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, from May 1, 2023. Its root_cause is: Incorrect default constraint values post-Liddell de-registration. Its accident scale is: Regional impact. Its dates are: 01-05-2023 (same as the incident). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market operators in NSW. Its cause type is: Technical/System Error. Its causes are: Incorrect default constraint values, tied to unit de-registration defaults.

title: An imminent improvement to the bidding and dispatch process for Semi-Scheduled units
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "An imminent improvement to the bidding and dispatch process for Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 25-05-2023 01:48:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) is set to implement a change to the dispatch process for Semi-Scheduled units, initially scheduled for mid-June but later postponed to 12 July 2023. This modification involves re-enabling the 'MaxAvail' figure in bids, which will now limit the available capacity fed into the market dispatch process, enhancing transparency and correctness of unit availability. This change addresses the unique bidding issues faced by Semi-Scheduled units, allowing better communication of real-time capacity limits, especially in anomalous circumstances like high temperatures or local technical challenges.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to Medium Term. Its root_cause is: Lack of transparency and operational challenges in the dispatch process of Semi-Scheduled units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-05-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants and operators of Semi-Scheduled units. Its cause type is: Systemic inefficiency and operational issues. Its causes is: Inaccurate capacity reporting of Semi-Scheduled units, market instability due to lack of information, and inefficient bidding processes in the electricity market.

title: (Early!) winter evening volatility returns to QLD and NSW on Wednesday 24th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early! winter evening volatility returns to QLD and NSW on Wednesday 24th May 2023" posted at 24-05-2023 06:02:00 PM is: The article reports on the recent volatility in electricity prices in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) due to market dynamics on May 24, 2023. Volatility began around 17:30 with price spikes nearing the Market Price Cap, despite relatively modest market demand and increased wind production. Constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector have led to price differentials, impacting the flow of electricity southward, while the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) for the combined QLD and NSW economic island dropped to 7%, contributing to the volatility and resulting in an Actual Lack of Reserve (LOR1) condition in NSW.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as the volatility and conditions mentioned are affecting prices on the same day. Its root_cause is: Constrained interconnector flow and low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 24-05-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: System constraints and market conditions. Its causes is: Price spikes due to constraints on interconnector flow and low IRPM in the region.

title: IRPM drops below 15% on Tuesday 23rd May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "IRPM drops below 15% on Tuesday 23rd May 2023" posted at 23-05-2023 06:46:00 PM is: The article discusses the decline of the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) to below 15% in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on the evening of 23rd May 2023, driven by increased market demand from colder weather, outages in thermal units, and low wind energy generation. This situation resulted in elevated spot prices over $300/MWh in mainland regions and warnings of Actual LOR1 reserve levels for New South Wales. Highlighted as concerns are the lack of sufficient replacement dispatchable capacity following the Liddell closure and a trend towards tighter supply-demand balances, prompting expectations of more frequent occurrences of such scenarios. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially increasing in frequency until new capacity is built. Its root_cause is: Cold weather, thermal unit outages, and insufficient wind harvest leading to tight supply-demand conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-05-2023. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market, focusing on QLD, NSW, and VIC. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the NEM, especially in the mainland regions experiencing high spot prices. Its cause type is: Weather and capacity-related. Its causes are: Increased market demand due to cold temperatures, thermal unit outages, low wind generation, and insufficient replacement capacity following the Liddell closure.

title: AEMO’s MMS 5.2, ez2view v9.7, Semi-Scheduled MaxAvail in the Bid, and a self-forecasting example
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO’s MMS 5.2, ez2view v9.7, Semi-Scheduled MaxAvail in the Bid, and a self-forecasting example" posted at 23-05-2023 01:01:00 PM is: The article discusses several developments in the electricity market related to the upgrade of the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) Market Management System (MMS) to version 5.2. This update will support various market enhancements, including the introduction of MaxAvail for Semi-Scheduled Generators, improvements in self-forecasting practices, and market developments such as the fast frequency market and MT PASA Generator Recall Times. The update aligns with the release of ez2view v9.7, which will be the first compatible version with MMS v5.2, aiming to integrate these enhancements into visual software. The changes address issues like self-forecasting accuracy and regulatory compliance, potentially impacting generator operations and energy market strategies.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: By 9 October 2023. Its root_cause is: Market enhancements requiring system upgrades. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-05-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled Generators, operators using AEMO's services, and ez2view clients. Its cause type is: System update and market policy changes. Its causes are: The need for improved forecasting and bidding practices, introduction of new market rules, and software compatibility for enhanced data support.

title: Brief burst of volatility in QLD and NSW on Wednesday 17th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief burst of volatility in QLD and NSW on Wednesday 17th May 2023" posted at 17-05-2023 06:25:00 PM is: A brief episode of market volatility was experienced in Queensland and New South Wales on the evening of May 17, 2023, due to constrained flow on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, leading to low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) in the 'QLD+NSW Economic Island'. The situation subsided by 18:00, although low IRPM alerts remained. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. title: Too Early to Say?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Too Early to Say?" posted at 15-05-2023 04:17:00 PM is: The closure of the Liddell Power Station, a major electricity plant, has led to significant changes in spot prices and supply levels in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Spot prices rose in all regions, with South Australia and Victoria experiencing the largest increases. This was influenced by outages and flow restrictions on the Heywood interconnector, unrelated to the Liddell closure. Supply from black coal increased in New South Wales post-closure, while gas and hydro outputs rose as well. Demand also increased NEM-wide, further influencing price changes. Despite these movements, drawing conclusive insights from the short timeframe remains premature; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal). Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Closure of Liddell Power Station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-05-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), predominantly South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity suppliers, consumers, and market participants. Its cause type is: Operational change in electricity supply infrastructure. Its causes are: Closure of Liddell Power Station, regional interconnector outages, changes in supply and demand dynamics, higher reliance on alternative energy sources.

title: We’re not building enough replacement dispatchable capacity
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "We’re not building enough replacement dispatchable capacity" posted at 14-05-2023 05:45:00 PM is: The article discusses the ongoing challenges in the energy transition, focusing on the insufficient replacement of dispatchable capacity following the closure of coal power stations like Liddell and potentially Eraring in New South Wales. The analysis highlights trends within the energy market, such as the decline in the minimum and average levels of the 'Aggregate Scheduled Target' over time, and the inadequacy of new dispatchable sources to cover the deficit left by retired coal plants, posing risks to grid stability. The data suggests that despite an increase in renewable energy, there is still a pressing need for substantial dispatchable capacity to maintain reliability during low renewable output periods.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Closure of coal power stations and insufficient replacement of dispatchable capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-05-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers dependent on grid stability. Its cause type is: Policy and Market Dynamics. Its causes are: Closure of Liddell and potential closure of Eraring power stations, and lack of sufficient new dispatchable energy capacity.

title: How dispatch works … it’s much more than just a bid stack
author: Ryan Esplin
classified_content: The summary of the news "How dispatch works … it’s much more than just a bid stack" posted at 11-05-2023 02:35:00 PM is: The article discusses the transition of the power system from large thermal generators to smaller, dispersed wind and solar generators, highlighting the importance of understanding dispatch constraints and their effects. It explains the complexities of the NEM dispatch engine (NEMDE) and showcases various case studies, comparing different scenarios with and without constraints to illustrate their impact on dispatch prices, quantities, and flows. The article emphasizes the critical need for accurate modeling of constraints on dispatch outcomes, particularly for market participants, investors, and regulators, as the power system continues to evolve; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing transition with implications as constraints become binding. Its root_cause is: Transition from traditional to renewable energy sources and the introduction of more constraints in the power network. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-05-2023. Its affected region is: Potentially all regions within the NEM (National Electricity Market), with specific examples in Victoria, Tasmania, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants, investors, regulators, and developers interested in energy economics. Its cause type is: Structural market transition and operational constraints. Its causes are: Increase in renewable energy generation, transmission network constraints, interregional and intra-regional congestion, forced curtailment, and economic offloading.

title: Renewable curtailment – forced and not quite so forced
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Renewable curtailment – forced and not quite so forced" posted at 09-05-2023 02:20:00 PM is: The article discusses the distinction and recent trends in renewable energy curtailment within the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Curtailment can be categorized into "forced," due to network constraints, and "economic offloading," involving generators self-limiting output based on market price conditions. The analysis highlights that forced curtailment is a significant factor for solar farms in the northern regions, while economic offloading due to low daytime prices is more prevalent in the southern regions, particularly affecting wind farms. It suggests that some economic offloading might be a result of indirect network constraints, emphasizing an underlying need to resolve transmission issues for better energy dispatch; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues, fuel prices, coal mine accidents, market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term systemic impact due to market and infrastructure influences. Its root_cause is: Network constraints and market price conditions leading to either forced curtailment or economic offloading. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (not an accident). Its dates are: 09-05-2023. Its affected region is: Northern and Southern regions of the NEM in Australia (including Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia). Its affected key users are: Renewable energy generators, particularly solar and wind farms. Its cause type is: Economic and infrastructure constraints. Its causes are: Network constraints causing forced curtailment and market price conditions leading to economic offloading.

title: Currently forecast LOR2 for South Australia for Wednesday 10th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Currently forecast LOR2 for South Australia for Wednesday 10th May 2023" posted at 08-05-2023 01:58:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast for a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) in South Australia on 10th May 2023, during the morning and evening. This is due to expected low wind and solar generation capabilities, resulting in potential reliance on electricity imports. Additionally, the Murraylink interconnector outage and a limitation on the Heywood interconnector are compounding the forecasted low reserve condition. AEMO is seeking market responses to address this capacity shortfall.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term impact for the specific periods on 10th May 2023. Its root_cause is: Low reserve capacity due to low renewable generation and interconnector limitations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-05-2023, 10-05-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Grid limitations and low renewable output. Its causes are: Low wind and solar generation, Murraylink outage, limitation of Heywood interconnector.

title: Liddell’s ghost stalks the market
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Liddell’s ghost stalks the market" posted at 05-05-2023 09:38:00 AM is: The article discusses the unexpected price spikes in the National Electricity Market (NEM) following the closure of Liddell power station, particularly due to a "handover" problem within the Australian Energy Market Operator’s (AEMO) market systems and software. This issue arose when the system components failed to properly transition from utilizing the Liddell units' input data, causing constraints to inaccurately default to assuming higher output levels, leading to higher electricity prices; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: May 1, 2023, onwards. Its root_cause is: Software handover problem in AEMO's market systems. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: May 1, 2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants in NEM. Its cause type is: System/Software failure. Its causes are: Liddell's closure and subsequent data handling issues within AEMO's systems leading to conservative default values in constraints.

This updated rule, finalized on May 4, 2023, builds on the original IESS rule established in December 2021. Detailed documentation about this determination is available for review on the AEMC website under Project ERC0351; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its causes are: Amendment of Integrating Energy Storage Systems rule within NEM.

title: A spike at 16:15 hints at volatility to resume in SA with the sun setting, on Thursday 4th May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A spike at 16:15 hints at volatility to resume in SA with the sun setting, on Thursday 4th May 2023" posted at 04-05-2023 04:55:00 PM is: The article reports on a significant spike in electricity prices in South Australia, with prices exceeding $1,000/MWh due to low wind conditions and constrained imports from Victoria. These conditions have persisted since the previous night and are expected to continue until Saturday evening, potentially leading to further price volatility as solar generation decreases with sunset.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Weather and grid constraint. Its causes are: Low wind conditions, import constraints from Victoria due to outage, sunset reducing solar power availability.

title: A second (still rushed) look at volatility in NSW very early on Monday 1st May 2023 … a possible snag with Liddell retirement from market data systems?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A second (still rushed) look at volatility in NSW very early on Monday 1st May 2023 … a possible snag with Liddell retirement from market data systems?" posted at 02-05-2023 07:22:00 PM is: The article discusses unusual volatility in electricity prices in New South Wales (NSW) in the early hours of May 1, 2023, potentially linked to the retirement of Liddell power station from market data systems. This volatility was notably associated with the 'N>>NIL_33_34' constraint equation, which seemed to have played a significant role in price spikes surpassing $1,000/MWh at certain intervals. The RHS (Right-Hand Side) of the constraint equation dropped drastically and unexpectedly early that morning, causing the constraint to bind and the NSW Regional Reference Price (RRP) to increase. Speculation surrounds issues with network terms, emergency ratings, and feedback terms concerning the Liddell units, which were deregistered. There is a hypothesis that the disappearance of Liddell's capacity from market systems might have influenced the equation's behavior. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term volatility observed on 1st May 2023. Its root_cause is: Possible market data system discrepancies linked to Liddell's retirement. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st May 2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Systemic/Technical issue. Its causes are: Sudden drop in RHS of the 'N>>NIL_33_34' constraint equation, implications of Liddell's deregistration, potential issues with emergency ratings and feedback terms in market equations.

title: Australia’s Renewable Energy Boom –  The Good, The Bad and the Downright Ugly
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "Australia’s Renewable Energy Boom –  The Good, The Bad and the Downright Ugly" posted at 02-05-2023 03:06:00 PM is: The article examines Australia's significant shift toward renewable energy investment post-2016, led by a boom in wind and solar projects, which has transformed the electricity market and reduced carbon emissions. However, it highlights several challenges, such as transmission constraints, grid connection issues, and curtailment due to negative market prices, impacting project performance. Despite these issues, the article underscores the potential of renewable energy technologies with successful examples, while also stressing the need for improved policy and planning to support infrastructure and grid capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions, fuel prices, grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 2017-2025. The root cause is: Transmission constraints, grid connection delays, and market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2017-2025. The affected region is: Australia, with focus on South Australia, Queensland, Victoria, and northern Queensland. The affected key users are: Commercial renewable energy project investors and developers. The cause type is: Market and grid infrastructure challenges. Its causes are: Transmission capacity constraints, grid connection and commissioning delays, market curtailment due to negative spot prices, and inadequate policy and planning.

title: Spot price volatility in NSW and SA on Monday 1st May 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in NSW and SA on Monday 1st May 2023" posted at 01-05-2023 08:12:00 PM is: The article discusses significant electricity spot price volatility in New South Wales (NSW) and South Australia (SA) within the National Electricity Market (NEM) on May 1, 2023. Prices in these regions breached the $1,000/MWh alert criteria, with NSW experiencing volatility in the early morning and SA seeing increased volatility as demand ramped up. In NSW, this volatility is partially attributed to the 'N>>NIL_33_34' Constraint Equation, which resulted in low CPD prices for affected units. Further detailed explanations are anticipated in follow-up articles; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on May 1, 2023, with potential ongoing effects pending further analysis. Its root_cause is: Market constraints and demand fluctuations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-05-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy suppliers and consumers within these regions. Its cause type is: Constraint-bound market conditions. Its causes are: 'N>>NIL_33_34' Constraint Equation in NSW, demand fluctuations in SA.

title: Relatively low wind harvest through Sunday 30th April is a reminder of challenges in the energy transition
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Relatively low wind harvest through Sunday 30th April is a reminder of challenges in the energy transition" posted at 30-04-2023 07:00:00 PM is: The article discusses the recent closure of the last three units at Liddell and highlights the challenges in energy transition, particularly with renewable energy sources. It observed a high solar contribution driving prices negative during daylight hours and a low wind energy production contributing to electricity prices exceeding $100/MWh at night. Network constraints may have also limited wind production. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing due to the transition from coal to renewables. Its root_cause is: Low wind energy production and transitioning energy sources. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24th April 2023 to 30th April 2023. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational challenges and network constraints. Its causes are: Closure of coal units, low wind energy production, network constraints, high solar production during daylight.

title: That’s all folks: the last unit of Liddell Power Station closes
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "That’s all folks: the last unit of Liddell Power Station closes" posted at 28-04-2023 11:34:00 AM is: The Liddell Power Station, Australia’s oldest utility-scale coal-fired plant, ceased operations of its last unit at 10:30 am AEST, marking its full retirement after nearly 52 years. The closure, announced over seven years ago, completed with Unit 1's shutdown following the prior sequential closures of Units 3, 4, and 2 earlier in the week. This event coincides with AEMO's report on renewable energy contributing to lower wholesale energy costs and an LOR2 forecast for NSW. Media outlets have widely covered the closure, noting it as the end of an era and accompanied by remarks from various industry figures and entities on social media. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing due to transition in energy sources and potential market adjustments. Its root cause is: Scheduled phase-out of ageing coal-fired power plant. Its accident scale is: N/A - no accident involved. Its dates are: 28-04-2023 for the final unit closure. Its affected region is: Liddell, New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: New South Wales electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Policy-driven infrastructure transition. Its causes are: Final retirement of Liddell Power Station in accordance with planned closure, reflecting shift towards renewable energy sources.

title: AEMO’s QED for Q1 2023 + AER’s analogous report released
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO’s QED for Q1 2023 + AER’s analogous report released" posted at 28-04-2023 09:53:00 AM is: The AEMO's Quarterly Energy Dynamics report for Q1 2023 highlights the positive impact of renewable energy on wholesale electricity prices, driving them down and setting new records for minimum grid demand while reducing emissions. The report emphasizes the need for investment in new transmission and firming technologies to enhance the connectivity and efficiency of renewable energy sources, especially in regions like Victoria's Murray River Renewable Energy Zone. Renewable energy led to negative or zero wholesale prices for 12% of the time across the NEM, with significant occurrences in South Australia and Victoria. The AER's complementary report further underscores these findings, showing price reductions across most regions; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing, as investment in infrastructure and renewable energy continues to affect the market. Its root cause is: Increased renewable energy penetration and insufficient transmission infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-04-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions, particularly South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and stakeholders in the electricity market. Its cause type is: Market and infrastructure. Its causes are: Increased renewable energy output, insufficient transmission infrastructure, and resultant negative/zero wholesale price occurrences.

title: AEMO intermittently forecasting LOR2 (Low Reserve Condition) for NSW for Saturday 29th April 2023 – the day after the last Liddell unit is scheduled to close
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO intermittently forecasting LOR2 (Low Reserve Condition) for NSW for Saturday 29th April 2023 – the day after the last Liddell unit is scheduled to close," posted at 28-04-2023 09:50:00 AM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) forecast of a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) for New South Wales, scheduled for the afternoon of April 29, 2023. This forecast emerged as the last unit at the Liddell Power Station is set to close, reducing reserve capacity and highlighting the pressures expected on the grid as older coal-fired plants retire. The minimum reserve capacity requirement was forecasted to be 1136 MW, but only 1081 MW was available, indicating a shortfall and potential need for a market response. The situation reflects concerns about ongoing reliance on aging coal infrastructure and its impact on grid stability as these plants are phased out. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing, especially influencing market operations and reserves on 29/04/2023. Its root_cause is: Closure of the last Liddell unit and the subsequent reduction in reserve power generation capacity, prompting concerns about the availability of reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Main date is 29/04/2023; forecast initiated on 28/04/2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Power infrastructure retirement/closure leading to low reserve margins. Its causes are: Closure of Liddell Unit 1, reduced coal-fired power capacity reserve, and reliance on aging infrastructure.

title: Timestamping Renewable Energy Certificates is a Bad Idea.
author: Tom Geiser
classified_content: The summary of the news "Timestamping Renewable Energy Certificates is a Bad Idea" posted at 27-04-2023 03:05:00 PM is: The article critiques the concept of timestamping renewable energy certificates (RECs), arguing it complicates trading, reduces accuracy, and distorts energy markets. Timestamping creates 8760 tradeable products annually, increasing transaction complexity and costs. It standardizes certificate trading but decreases accuracy and fails to facilitate independent verification. Instead, the author suggests using established accounting methods for real-time renewable accounting. The EU's intention to legislate timestamping could face backlash, and Australia should monitor developments before adopting it. The article also highlights potential oversupply concerns in renewable deployment due to EU policies.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Policy proposition on timestamping RECs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-04-2023. Its affected region is: Primarily the EU and potentially Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers involved in REC trading. Its cause type is: Policy and legislative decision. Its causes are: Proposal of timestamping RECs, increased complexity and cost, reduced accuracy, potential market distortion, and legislative developments in the EU.

title: Fat lady singing for three down (with LD02 on the way out) and one to go at AGL’s Liddell Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Fat lady singing for three down (with LD02 on the way out) and one to go at AGL’s Liddell Power Station" posted at 26-04-2023 08:47:00 PM is: The article discusses the gradual shutdown of the Liddell Power Station's LD02 unit on 26th April 2023, marking a significant milestone in the station's decommissioning process. The declining performance of the unit reflects issues with coal availability and previously reduced boiler pressures to manage risk. LD01 is scheduled to be the last unit to close by 28th April. The shutdown impacts the revenue as shown by a spike in trading prices on that day. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact, expected closure by 28th April 2023. Its root_cause is: Closure of coal-fired power station units leading to decreased power generation. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 26th April 2023, with final closures by 28th April 2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Power grid operators and consumers relying on Liddell Power Station. Its cause type is: Operational transition. Its causes are: Declining coal availability, aging infrastructure, and strategic policy decisions to close coal-fired power plants.

title: Beryl Solar Farm (BERYLSF1) shut down with grass fires
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Beryl Solar Farm (BERYLSF1) shut down with grass fires" posted at 25-04-2023 02:06:00 PM is: The Beryl Solar Farm in Australia, operated by RES Australia on behalf of Banpu Energy, was forced offline due to a grass fire burning under its modules on April 24, 2023. This incident was not initially identified in the rebid reasons, which typically do not account for such physical limitations in semi-scheduled units. The farm had stopped operations fully by 13:05 on that day. Ongoing discussions suggest changes in bid processes to better account for physical limitations are anticipated in the near future. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Grass fire under solar modules. Its accident scale is: Localized to the Beryl Solar Farm. Its dates are: 24-04-2023 to 25-04-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: External physical event. Its causes are: Grass fire impacting solar module operation, lack of transparent rebid reasons reflecting physical limitations.

title: Liddell’s unit 4 closes
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Liddell’s unit 4 closes" posted at 24-04-2023 10:05:00 AM is: Liddell’s Unit 4 has ceased operations, reducing its output to zero by the end of the 08:25 dispatch interval on 24 April 2023, after operating steadily around 320 MW for the past month. Despite being bid at the market floor price, ensuring continued operation up to this point, about 30 MW of capacity was not dispatched due to bids above 10,000 $/MWh. The news anticipates the shutdown of Unit 2 on the 26th and Unit 1 on the 28th of April 2023. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Planned shutdown of an aging power unit. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24 April 2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Shutdown of Liddell’s Unit 4, market floor pricing, and future anticipated unit shutdowns.

title: Case Study of Tuesday 16th February 2021 (Part 1) … large instance of collective over-performance for Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of Tuesday 16th February 2021 (Part 1) … large instance of collective over-performance for Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 23-04-2023 08:54:00 PM is: The article discusses instances of large aggregate raw off-target (AggROT) deviations, particularly focusing on an event from February 16, 2021, where there was a significant collective over-performance of semi-scheduled units at -652MW. This incident remains a record as of Q1 2023. These fluctuations indicate notable irregularities in energy dispatch stability for semi-scheduled units. Similar instances of deviations in performance were noted in January and February 2023 and August 2022.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since it has set a record as of Q1 2023. Its root_cause is: Over-performance of semi-scheduled units leading to deviations from expected energy dispatch. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-02-2021. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Performance deviation. Its causes are: Large-scale deviations in grid performance due to over-performance of semi-scheduled units.

title: Case Study of Tuesday 23rd August 2022 (Part 1) … large instance of collective over-performance for Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of Tuesday 23rd August 2022 (Part 1) … large instance of collective over-performance for Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 23-04-2023 03:32:00 PM is: The news discusses a significant event of collective over-performance by Semi-Scheduled units in the electricity market on 23rd August 2022. At 13:15 on this date, the units collectively over-performed with an Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) deviation of -650MW, marking it as the second-largest instance of collective over-performance recorded at that time. This analysis is part of the GenInsights Quarterly Updates for Q1 2023, where similar deviations were also noted on 20th January and 3rd February 2023. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23rd August 2022. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Performance deviation. Its causes are: Large collective over-performance by Semi-Scheduled units.

title: Case Study of Friday 20th January 2023 (Part 1) … large instance of collective over-performance for Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of Friday 20th January 2023 (Part 1) … large instance of collective over-performance for Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 23-04-2023 03:11:00 PM is: On Friday, January 20th, 2023, there was a significant shift from collective under-performance to over-performance in Semi-Scheduled units concerning Aggregate Raw Off-Target. Notably, the largest over-performance recorded was AgROT = -497MW at 16:25. This event may be subject to further exploration in future analyses.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Operational variability among Semi-Scheduled units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-01-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Variability in performance of Semi-Scheduled units.

title: Case Study of Friday 3rd February 2023 (Part 1) … two consecutive large instances of collective under-performance for Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of Friday 3rd February 2023 (Part 1) … two consecutive large instances of collective under-performance for Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 23-04-2023 01:09:00 PM is: The article discusses a case study focusing on the under-performance of Semi-Scheduled units on February 3rd, 2023, particularly during the 15:35 and 15:40 dispatch intervals. This performance trend is a part of the GenInsights Quarterly Updates for Q1 2023, contrasting with a declining number of coal-fired units. The study acknowledges the high demand observed in Queensland as it approached record levels for Grid and Market Demand on that day.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-04-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market performance issue. Its causes are: Under-performance of Semi-Scheduled units and high demand in Queensland.

title: Glitch with Victorian SCADA data triggers (>24 hour) Market Suspension in Victorian Region – Saturday 22nd April 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Glitch with Victorian SCADA data triggers (>24 hour) Market Suspension in Victorian Region – Saturday 22nd April 2023" posted at 22-04-2023 06:12:00 PM is: A significant interruption in the SCADA data for Victoria led to the suspension of the electricity spot market from 16:20 on April 22, 2023, to 17:00 on April 23, 2023. This incident was initially communicated through multiple market notices by AEMO, indicating a major IT incident affecting energy management services and SCADA data for Victoria. Although SCADA links were restored early on April 23, the underlying issue persisted, causing the suspension to continue until sufficient measures were taken to prevent recurrence, as ensured by AEMO's resumption declaration. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Approximately 24 hours from April 22, 2023, to April 23, 2023. Its root cause is: SCADA data delivery glitch.
