Its accident scale is: Regional (Victoria). Its dates are: April 22-23, 2023. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: NEM electricity market participants and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Technical/System failure. Its causes are: SCADA data delivery interruption, major IT incident, unresolved root cause, and market process influence.

title: A (very slight) delay to closure of Liddell unit 4 … but LD01 and LD02 slightly earlier
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A (very slight) delay to closure of Liddell unit 4 … but LD01 and LD02 slightly earlier" posted at 17-04-2023 01:32:00 PM is: The Liddell power station’s remaining units are set for closure, though unit 4's operation may extend slightly until the evening of April 21 or the morning of April 22 due to recent changes in MT PASA DUID Availability data. Meanwhile, unit 2 and unit 1 will be closing one day earlier than initially scheduled, on April 24 and April 28, respectively. This adjustment reflects ongoing volatility and reserve conditions in NSW and QLD, emphasizing pressure on the electricity market amidst high import dependencies. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Scheduled closure and phased-out operations of coal-fired units. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 17-04-2023 (note unit 4's potential closure shift to 22-04-2023). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy Market Participants and Energy User Representatives. Its cause type is: Operational adjustment in line with closure schedules. Its causes are: Expected closure of Liddell units impacting market stability and reliance on energy imports, with adjustments due to recent forecasts and availability data.

title: Delving deeper into dispatch availability self-forecasting performance
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper into dispatch availability self-forecasting performance" posted at 14-04-2023 01:14:00 PM is: The article discusses the upgrades and changes in the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) forecasting systems for wind and solar assets, specifically the transition from a vendor-based approach to an internally developed system that commenced in November 2022. The article highlights the performance assessments of participant self-forecasts, detailing a grace period where the suppressions of self-forecasts were paused to allow participants to adjust to the new benchmark. The discussion includes the outcomes of the performance assessment procedure updates, the impact of the revised gate closure time from 15 seconds to 10 seconds, and the analysis of sufficient data intervals for assessment. Despite the assumption that more upgrades would lead to more suppressions, the results differed from expectations, indicating only a slight change in forecast performance status. Several procedural peculiarities and sensitivities in forecast submissions and automatic suppression status are explored.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short to medium term, as the assessment process changes aim to refine market operations. Its root cause is: System upgrades and policy changes within AEMO's forecasting procedures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-11-2022 (upgrade commencement), 28-02-2023 (end of the grace period), 11-04-2023 (new procedure application). Its affected region is: Australia (specifically QLD and SA regions for mentioned DUIDs). Its affected key users are: Wind and solar power generators issuing self-forecasts. Its cause type is: Procedural change. Its causes are: AEMO's forecasting system upgrades, change in performance assessment procedures, and the tightening of self-forecast benchmarks.

title: Questions about electricity bills with closure of Liddell looming
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Questions about electricity bills with closure of Liddell looming" posted at 11-04-2023 03:20:00 PM is: The article discusses the impending closure of AGL's Liddell power station in New South Wales, noting that government figures, such as the NSW Energy Minister, insist that the closure will not result in higher electricity bills. This event is cast against the backdrop of historical challenges faced by the plant in its operational twilight years and the broader energy market context. The technical capacity and operational issues of Liddell, including chronic underperformance due to aging infrastructure, are underscored. The article speculates about the potential impact on spot prices in the short term, influenced by Liddell's phased closure, which sees units sequentially decommissioned starting April 19, 2023. It anticipates minimal long-term retail price impact given the closure’s long anticipation in the market, allowing hedging strategies to mitigate price shocks, though the resilience of such strategies against unforeseen supply-demand balance changes raises uncertainty. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short to medium term. Its root cause is: The scheduled closure of the Liddell power station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: April 19, April 25, and April 29, 2023, for the sequential shutdown of units. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy-driven closure. Its causes are: Policy decision to close aging coal-fired power plants, operational inefficiencies, and emissions considerations leading to scheduled decommissioning.

title: Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022… Part 4: the geographical spread of +861MW AggROT
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022… Part 4: the geographical spread of +861MW AggROT" posted at 11-04-2023 12:06:00 PM is: The article analyzes a significant instance of collective under-performance across all Semi-Scheduled units in the National Electricity Market (NEM) that occurred at 17:05 on 27th October 2022. This event marked one of the largest deviations from target in NEM history, with units predominantly situated in southern and south-western regions showing extreme under-performance. The analysis highlights geographic clustering of these deviations and suggests that weather patterns, including dappled cloud cover affecting solar irradiance and strong southerly winds, may have contributed to the under-performance. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate aftermath of the event on 27th October 2022. Its root cause is: Under-performance of Semi-Scheduled units due to weather impacts. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27th October 2022. Its affected region is: Southern and south-western parts of the NEM, particularly in South-Western NSW, Western VIC, and Mid-north and northern SA. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Weather patterns including cloud cover impacting solar output and strong winds.

title: Some analysis of electricity usage (and solar production) at home
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Personal experiences with Distributed Energy Resources
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some analysis of electricity usage (and solar production) at home" posted at 11-04-2023 09:03:00 AM is: The article discusses a personal experience in managing household electricity consumption and production using a rooftop solar system, highlighting observation from data collected over several years. The individual notes decreased cash refunds from their electricity retailer and observes a reduction in net solar exports due to increased home consumption during the COVID-19 pandemic, La Nina weather patterns, and potential degradation of solar system performance. The analysis also touches on the evolving electricity billing components and considerations for finding better retail offers. The 44c Feed-in-Tariff from the Queensland Government is identified as a source of significant financial return since the solar installation. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: residential surveys. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing from 2012 to present. Its root_cause is: residential shift to renewable energy resources, solar export reductions, and policy influences. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2012 through present, with specific observations in 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential energy consumers. Its cause type is: Policy and consumer behavior. Its causes are: Installation of rooftop solar, COVID-19-induced remote work, weather patterns, and evolving retail electricity offers.

title: New low-point for demand in NSW on Easter Sunday, 9th April 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New low-point for demand in NSW on Easter Sunday, 9th April 2023" posted at 09-04-2023 02:47:00 PM is: On Easter Sunday, 9th April 2023, the New South Wales region of Australia experienced a new record low in market demand, hitting 3,957 MW at 12:50 PM. This new low surpasses the previous record of 4,130 MW set on 29th October 2022 by 173 MW. Using the AEMO's preferred 'Operational Demand' measure, the new lowest point was recorded at 4,101 MW, down from the previous record of 4,356 MW also set on 29th October 2022, marking a reduction of 254 MW. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) confirmed these figures on 11th April 2023; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact, observed within the same day. Its root_cause is: Seasonal variations, specifically the decrease in electricity demand typical during holidays such as Easter. Its accident scale is: Not applicable as this event is not an accident but a demand fluctuation. Its dates are: 09-04-2023 for the event and 11-04-2023 for AEMO confirmation. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown, the article does not specify any particular key users affected. Its cause type is: Demand fluctuation due to seasonal holiday effects. Its causes are: A decrease in electricity demand during the Easter holiday in New South Wales.

title: AEMO published Preliminary Report into NSW brief market suspension on 17th March 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO published Preliminary Report into NSW brief market suspension on 17th March 2023" posted at 06-04-2023 08:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released a preliminary report detailing the brief market suspension in New South Wales that occurred on 17th March 2023. The suspension lasted for 55 minutes and was attributed to service interruption issues concerning the SCADA systems. The report aims to provide clarity on the incident, as well as guide any future assessment related to this event. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Brief impact during and immediately after the 55-minute suspension. Its root cause is: Service interruption of SCADA systems. Its accident scale is: Localized impact affecting New South Wales. Its dates are: 17-03-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and suppliers within the New South Wales market. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Service interruption of SCADA systems leading to market suspension.

title: Bye X5, Hi X5
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bye X5, Hi X5" posted at 05-04-2023 12:41:00 PM is: The article discusses the replacement of the N^^N_NIL_3 constraint, known as the "X5 constraint," with two new constraints, N^^N_NIL_X5_BESH and N^^N_NIL_X5_BEKG, by AEMO. These constraints have significant market impacts, particularly affecting daytime power transfers from southern NEM regions into New South Wales and Queensland. The changes aim to manage flow paths more effectively between Victoria and New South Wales and address emerging transmission limitations in southern New South Wales. The new constraints provide more headroom due to updated handling of a network protection scheme related to the Murraylink DC interconnector, easing limitations under specific conditions. Despite these updates, the article notes that other constraints and market design features contribute to ongoing transmission and price separation issues between regions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing with potential long-term effects. Its root_cause is: Transmission limitations and market structural factors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-04-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Consumers in New South Wales and Queensland. Its cause type is: Market and transmission constraints. Its causes are: Replacement of the N^^N_NIL_3 constraint, transmission limitations, market design features.

title: AEMC’s Reliability Panel releases 2021-22 FY Annual Market Performance Review
author: Dan Lee
topic: Reliability Panel's Annual Market Performance Review
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC’s Reliability Panel releases 2021-22 FY Annual Market Performance Review" posted at 30-03-2023 11:06:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission's (AEMC) Reliability Panel released its Annual Market Performance Review for the 2021-22 Financial Year, highlighting market performance issues, particularly during the June 2022 suspension. The report emphasized the efforts by AEMO to avoid load shedding and maintain reliable supply amid volatility through measures like RERT activation and industry collaboration. The report further identifies evolving market risks such as exposure to international fuel supply volatility, a more limited energy system, and weather-related reliability risks.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Fuel prices. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: The review covers the 2021-22 Financial Year, notably June 2022. The root cause is: Challenges in evolving market and physical reliability risks. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2021-22 Financial Year, notably June 2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and physical system reliability risks. Its causes are: International fuel supply volatility, more energy-limited power system, weather-related reliability risk, less available coal generation fleet.

title: AFR reports Callide C co-owner (with CS Energy) goes into voluntary administration
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "AFR reports Callide C co-owner (with CS Energy) goes into voluntary administration" posted at 24-03-2023 09:46:00 PM is: The Callide C Power Station, co-owned by CS Energy and Genuity (formerly InterGen Australia), faces uncertainty as Genuity has gone into voluntary administration. This development surfaced as Callide C units 3 and 4 have been offline due to major operational incidents since 2021 and 2022, respectively. CS Energy, maintaining their commitment to restoring the power station, is assessing options for the joint venture. The broader context involves a separation of ownership interests between Callide C and Millmerran assets, with complexities surrounding Sev.en’s part-ownership. Additionally, CS Energy is addressing leadership challenges following their CEO's resignation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing until at least late 2023. Its root_cause is: Financial collapse and operational downtime of the Callide C units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25th May 2021 and 31st October 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers relying on Callide C Power Station. Its cause type is: Operational and financial failure. Its causes are: Explosion in May 2021, cooling tower failure in October 2022, and voluntary administration of Genuity.

title: Reviewing what happened in NSW and QLD on Thursday 16th March 2023 (with Actual LOR2, Volatility and Reserve Trader almost triggered, etc) … part 1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reviewing what happened in NSW and QLD on Thursday 16th March 2023 (with Actual LOR2, Volatility and Reserve Trader almost triggered, etc) … part 1" posted at 21-03-2023 09:57:00 PM is: On Thursday, 16th March 2023, the regions of New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) witnessed electricity market stress due to Actual LOR2 conditions, significant price volatility, and the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) preparing to implement Short-Notice Reserve Trader provisions. The situation was exacerbated by the upcoming closure of Liddell Unit 4, scheduled for 19th April 2023. Contributing factors included constrained interconnectors limiting support from southern generators, unexpected high demand levels driven by weather, and several coal units being offline due to planned outages. The event underscores ongoing challenges in grid management and the increasing risk levels across the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with potential ongoing implications due to the upcoming plant closure. Its root_cause is: Multiple factors including grid constraints, high demand, and planned outages at crucial generation units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16th March 2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown, though generally impacts market participants within the NEM. Its cause type is: System operators' response to forecast and actual system conditions. Its causes are: LOR2 conditions, high price volatility, interconnector constraints, demand forecasts exceeding capacity, and upcoming plant closures.

title: Spot price volatility in QLD and NSW on Sunday 19th March 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in QLD and NSW on Sunday 19th March 2023" posted at 19-03-2023 05:39:00 PM is: The article reports on the electricity spot price volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) due to high temperatures on March 19, 2023. The increased market demand driven by the heat, along with constrained electricity imports from Victoria (VIC), resulted in an 'Actual LOR1' level notice from the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and a low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) alert for the QLD+NSW economic island. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: High temperatures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-03-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: High temperatures, constrained electricity imports from VIC, increased market demand, 'Actual LOR1' level notice, low IRPM alert.

title: Brief (55 minute) Market Suspension on 17th March 2023 in NSW due to service interruption of SCADA systems
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief (55 minute) Market Suspension on 17th March 2023 in NSW due to service interruption of SCADA systems" posted at 17-03-2023 08:22:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) temporarily suspended the electricity spot market in New South Wales (NSW) for 55 minutes on 17th March 2023 due to a failure of SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems. The market suspension commenced at 8:15 PM and ended at 9:10 PM after the issue was resolved. During the suspension, dispatch prices were manually reviewed. This incident has raised concerns about the capacity of SCADA systems to handle the data volume required for new projects in the National Electricity Market (NEM); Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate (55 minutes on the day of the event). Its root cause is: Failure of SCADA systems in NSW. Its accident scale is: Localized (affecting only NSW region). Its dates are: 17-03-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and operators within NSW. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: SCADA systems failure leading to the impossibility of operating the spot market according to the rules.

title: New all-time record for Operational Demand in QLD on Friday 17th March 2023 … but not for Market Demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record for Operational Demand in QLD on Friday 17th March 2023 … but not for Market Demand" posted at 17-03-2023 07:20:00 PM is: On March 17, 2023, Queensland's electricity market witnessed a new all-time record for 'Operational Demand' at 10,070MW, surpassing the previous record by 12MW. However, the anticipated new record for 'Market Demand' did not materialize, as demand peaked at 10,100MW, missing the forecasted level by 51MW. The high demand aligned with the National Electricity Market (NEM) operational considerations but fell short in market demand metrics. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Extreme heat leading to high operational demand. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 17-03-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-related demand fluctuation. Its causes are: Extreme heat conditions leading to increased electricity demand.

title: AEMO forecasts point towards a possible record total demand in QLD this afternoon on 17th March 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts point towards a possible record total demand in QLD this afternoon on 17th March 2023" posted at 17-03-2023 04:40:00 PM is: AEMO's forecast indicates a potential record for electricity demand in Queensland due to hot and humid conditions on 17th March 2023. As of 4:30 PM, the temperature at Brisbane Airport was 32°C with 60% humidity, leading to a projected demand of 10,187MW for the 17:30 dispatch interval, surpassing the previous record of 10,119MW set approximately a year ago. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Hot and humid weather increasing electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-03-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Environmental factors. Its causes are: High temperatures and humidity leading to increased electricity demand.

title: Actual LOR2, and a run of volatility in NSW and QLD … only 34 days till the closure of Liddell unit 4
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual LOR2, and a run of volatility in NSW and QLD … only 34 days till the closure of Liddell unit 4" posted at 16-03-2023 07:41:00 PM is: This article discusses recent market events in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on 16th March 2023, highlighting significant price volatility with figures reaching up to $15,500/MWh in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Both regions experienced Actual LOR2-level Low Reserve Conditions within the 17:30 to 18:55 time frame, though the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) chose not to activate the Reserve Trader. The situation forms the context for the impending closure of Liddell Unit 4 in April, signalling a period of potential instability and strategic operational discussions among market participants and authorities; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term on 16th March 2023, with potential discussions leading into future planning as Liddell Unit 4 closes. Its root_cause is: High price volatility and low reserve conditions in NSW and QLD regions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-03-2023 (main event date), closure of Liddell Unit 4 scheduled on 19th April 2023. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Market participants, energy user representatives, and possibly residential and commercial users indirectly. Its cause type is: Market instability and operational planning. Its causes are: Volatile market prices, low reserve conditions in QLD and NSW, and impending closure of Liddell Unit 4.

title: AEMO commences RERT negotiations in NSW on Thursday 16th March 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO commences RERT negotiations in NSW on Thursday 16th March 2023" posted at 16-03-2023 05:18:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued market notices indicating a low reserve condition (LOR2) in New South Wales and commenced negotiations for additional reserve through the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) mechanism for the evening of March 16, 2023. The forecast indicated that the available capacity reserve was below the required level during the expected peak period of 18:00 to 19:00 hrs. AEMO sought market responses and secured a reserve contract to maintain system reliability in anticipation of potential intervention requirements.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate/short-term, specifically for the evening of March 16, 2023. Its root_cause is: A forecasted capacity reserve shortfall leading to a potential power supply insufficiency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-03-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Electricity market reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Forecast low reserve condition (LOR2) indicating capacity shortfall, requirement for additional reserve through RERT negotiations.

title: Late ‘summer’ hot blast drives demand higher in NSW and QLD
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Late ‘summer’ hot blast drives demand higher in NSW and QLD" posted at 16-03-2023 04:57:00 PM is: A hot and dry temperature surge in Sydney along with a hot and humid climate in Brisbane has led to heightened market demand in New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD), pushing electricity usage above typical levels for this time of year. This increased demand, coupled with reduced solar power availability as the day progresses, has driven spot prices upwards. Compounding the situation is the inability to import electricity from Victoria due to constraint equations, forming a "NSW-only Economic Island" with reduced spare capacity and resulting in a formal low reserve condition according to the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO). Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term impact. Its root_cause is: A late-season surge in hot weather conditions combined with system constraints preventing imports from neighboring regions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-03-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Environmental and systemic. Its causes are: A late summer-like hot blast, high electricity demand exceeding local generation capacity, and transmission constraints limiting electricity imports from Victoria.

title: 2023 Gas Statement of Opportunities (GSOO) from the AEMO identifies that southern Australia’s gas supply is declining faster than projected demand
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "2023 Gas Statement of Opportunities (GSOO) from the AEMO identifies that southern Australia’s gas supply is declining faster than projected demand" posted at 16-03-2023 01:09:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released the 2023 Gas Statement of Opportunities (GSOO), highlighting concerns over declining gas supply in southern Australia, which is decreasing faster than the projected demand despite increased production commitments. The report forecasts potential peak day shortfalls in the southern regions during winter 2023 under very high demand conditions, although annual supply is expected to be adequate until 2027. The report calls for investments to ensure operational solutions post-2027, acknowledging a long-term supply gap alongside falling consumption. Media coverage has noted potential future shortages during extreme weather conditions, challenges in gas market balance, and anticipated increases in power bills. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate for peak day shortfalls from winter 2023; long-term measures needed by 2027. Its root cause is: Declining gas supply versus projected demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-03-2023. Its affected region is: Southern Australia. Its affected key users are: Regional households and businesses reliant on gas. Its cause type is: Supply-demand imbalance. Its causes are: Declining gas production in the face of high demand, need for investment in operational solutions.

title: Splitting the ‘X5 constraint’ into two … double trouble?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Splitting the ‘X5 constraint’ into two … double trouble?" posted at 16-03-2023 11:16:00 AM is: AEMO has implemented a modification to the Murraylink Very Fast Runback (VFRB) scheme as of 08:00 NEM time to address increased generation in north-west Victoria. This modification divided the existing 'X5 constraint' into two separate constraints, namely N^^N_NIL_X5_BEKG and N^^N_NIL_X5_BESH, to manage voltage stability limits better and avoid voltage collapse at Balranald for contingency trips in NW Victoria. This action follows prior notifications in Market Notice 106643 and was driven by updates from Transgrid. The precise impacts will likely need further analysis, potentially from Allan O’Neil, to understand implications fully and detail changes to the RHS formulations of these constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing, starting from the implementation on 16th March 2023. Its root_cause is: The need to adapt to increased generation demands and manage voltage stability in the network. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-03-2023. Its affected region is: North-west Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity operators and consumers relying on the NEM. Its cause type is: System operational adjustment. Its causes are: Increased generation in the area, system stability management, and AEMO's adjustment of system constraints based on Transgrid's advisories.

title: Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022 … part 3 … (+861MW AggROT – source of AvailGen)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022 … part 3 … (+861MW AggROT – source of AvailGen)" posted at 14-03-2023 10:30:00 AM is: This article examines a case study regarding a significant deviation from target generation levels, called Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT), recorded on 27th October 2022 at 17:05, involving 152 Semi-Scheduled units like wind and solar, resulting in a +871MW deviation. This incident ranks as the second-largest deviation ever noted for these units, raising notable concerns due to its magnitude, though it didn't occur as a simultaneous trip like the record-setting case on 28th September 2016. Through this analysis, a split between forecasting methods was identified as influencing performance, with examples provided from Darlington Point and Limondale 1 Solar Farms. The event suggests a need to further analyze dispatch intervals to uncover any irregular patterns affecting electricity generation targets; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term. Its root_cause is: Forecasting variability in Semi-Scheduled units. Its accident scale is: Affected multiple Semi-Scheduled units with collective deviation of +871MW. Its dates are: 27th October 2022. Its affected region is: Potentially across regions in the National Electricity Market, exact location unspecified. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and operators depending on accurate generation forecasts. Its cause type is: Operational misalignment and forecasting discrepancies. Its causes are: Variability in self-forecast versus AEMO forecasts (ASEFS/AWEFS), under-performance in solar farms like Darlington Point and Limondale 1.

title: Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022 … part 2 … (+861MW AggROT – individual units)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022 … part 2 … (+861MW AggROT – individual units)" posted at 14-03-2023 10:15:00 AM is: This article discusses a substantial deviation in electricity supply at 17:05 on 27th October 2022, with an Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) of +861MW across 152 Semi-Scheduled units, marking the second-largest excursion for these units. The case study examines 15 solar and wind energy units that contributed most significantly to this deviation. While constraints impacted performance, they did not account for all discrepancies, suggesting systemic issues and questioning the Semi-Scheduled category's scalability. The under- and over-performance of several units across regions hints at potential unidentified causes and highlights outstanding industry queries about the category's effectiveness and sustainability. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Systemic issues and potential weather influences. Its accident scale is: Medium (aggregated deviation across multiple units). Its dates are: 27-10-2022. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC), New South Wales (NSW), South Australia (SA), and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in affected regions. Its cause type is: Operational and systemic. Its causes are: Deviation in energy production by Semi-Scheduled units, systemic issues in the Semi-Scheduled category, possible weather influences, and constraint management inefficiencies.

title: Why capacity factor is an increasingly simplistic way to compare solar farm performance
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why capacity factor is an increasingly simplistic way to compare solar farm performance" posted at 13-03-2023 12:00:00 PM is: The article discusses the limitations of using capacity factor as the sole metric for evaluating and comparing solar farm performance, arguing that it overlooks various critical factors such as location, bidding strategies, network constraints, energy prices, and financial incentives. By presenting a case study comparing two solar farms in South Australia, Bungala One and Tailem Bend, it highlights how different designs, operational decisions, and commercial arrangements can significantly affect performance. Consequently, the article suggests that capacity factor should not be the only measure when assessing solar farm efficiency or profitability.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, as it affects the assessment of solar farms' efficiency and investment decisions. Its root cause is: Oversimplification of evaluation metrics for solar farm performance. Its accident scale is: Unknown; no specific accident reported. Its dates are: 13-03-2023 12:00:00 PM. Its affected region is: South Australia (case study focus). Its affected key users are: Solar farm operators, investors, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Evaluation metric limitation. Its causes are: Relying solely on capacity factor for solar farm evaluation, ignoring factors like location, network constraints, and financial arrangements.

title: Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022 (+861MW AggROT for Semi-Scheduled units) … Part 1 of (an uncertain number)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of 17:05 on 27th October 2022 (+861MW AggROT for Semi-Scheduled units) … Part 1 of (an uncertain number)" posted at 09-03-2023 04:53:00 PM is: This case study initiates an examination into the Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) deviations observed in Semi-Scheduled units on 27th October 2022, highlighting a collective under-performance totaling +861MW at 17:05. The study identifies persistent under-performances across both wind and solar farms, and across various regions, emphasizing increasing trends in such deviations over recent years. The analysis focuses on understanding the performance of 152 units in relation to their targets, with a notable observation that 13 out of 15 units with significant deviations were subject to Semi-Dispatch Cap, raising potential concerns about systemic issues or other influencing factors.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, affecting immediate market operations and possibly indicating long-term trends. The root cause is: Deviations in performance of Semi-Scheduled units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27th October 2022. Its affected region is: Various regions across QLD, NSW, VIC, SA, TAS. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational performance deviation. Its causes are: Under-performance of wind and solar farms, implementation of Semi-Dispatch Caps affecting unit outputs.

title: AER notice … ‘Market notice from the WEMDRA – Referral to dispute resolution panel’ … about scheduling error on 10th August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER notice … ‘Market notice from the WEMDRA – Referral to dispute resolution panel’ … about scheduling error on 10th August 2022" posted at 09-03-2023 02:18:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) updated its website to inform National Electricity Market participants of a scheduling error that occurred on 10 August 2022. This issue has been referred to a dispute resolution panel by the Wholesale Energy Market Dispute Resolution Adviser (WEMDRA), with claims for compensation being processed according to the National Electricity Rules (NER). Affected participants can initiate claims with the dispute resolution panel if they believe they incurred a compensable loss, with the deadline for claims set at close of business on 10 March 2023. The panel, led by Tom Clarke, will evaluate the potential for further claims from participants with losses exceeding $5,000. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Scheduling error by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10 August 2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Market participants affected by the scheduling error. Its cause type is: Operational error. Its causes are: A scheduling error declared by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO).

title: Some revelations in GenInsights Q4 2022 about Aggregate Raw Off-Target for Semi-Scheduled units
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some revelations in GenInsights Q4 2022 about Aggregate Raw Off-Target for Semi-Scheduled units" posted at 09-03-2023 06:45:00 AM is: The GenInsights Q4 2022 report reveals significant deviations in the performance of Semi-Scheduled wind and solar farms, in terms of Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT). Throughout 2022, there were over 1,000 instances where wind and solar farms collectively underperformed by more than 300MW, while coal units rarely exceeded such deviations. With the growing capacity, large deviations are expected to increase, presenting challenges to system stability. Notable extreme cases include a +861MW collective underperformance in October 2022 and significant incidents in previous years. The report emphasizes the increasing frequency and magnitude of these deviations, signaling potential risks to the power system's security. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing due to increasing installation of renewable capacity. Its root cause is: Collective performance deviations of Semi-Scheduled wind and solar units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27th October 2022 and ongoing throughout 2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly affecting regions with significant wind and solar installations. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Performance variation and grid imbalance due to renewable energy integration. Its causes are: Increased capacity of Semi-Scheduled wind and solar units leading to collective performance deviations, incidents of underperformance and overperformance, systemic challenges within renewable energy integration.

title: Observation 13/22 from GenInsights21 – is the Semi-Scheduled category sustainable or scalable?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Observation 13/22 from GenInsights21 – is the Semi-Scheduled category sustainable or scalable?" posted at 09-03-2023 06:00:00 AM is: The article discusses the ongoing analysis of the sustainability and scalability of the Semi-Scheduled category for wind and solar farms in the National Electricity Market (NEM). As the proportion of these renewable energy sources increases, issues have emerged with the current arrangements, which were designed when wind and solar contributed a smaller share of energy. The analysis, part of the GenInsights21 report, highlights challenges such as off-target forecasting and the integration of hybrid facilities. The increasing supply uncertainty is now impacting the scheduled sector as well. The article calls for developers, owners, investors, and financiers to prepare for more complex deployment and nuanced business cases. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Increasing integration of renewable energy sources requiring reevaluation of regulatory and operational frameworks. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-03-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Wind and solar farm operators, developers, and investors in the NEM. Its cause type is: Regulatory and operational framework issues. Its causes are: Challenges with off-target forecasting for semi-scheduled units, integration of hybrid systems, and increasing supply uncertainty.

title: Coal generation gets a run in the UK to ensure supply meets demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Coal generation gets a run in the UK to ensure supply meets demand" posted at 08-03-2023 12:06:00 PM is: The National Grid Electricity System Operator in the UK activated coal-fired generators for the first time during this winter season to meet electricity demand during a cold snap with low wind generation, highlighting contingency measures during extreme weather events; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: A cold snap causing increased demand and low wind energy availability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-03-2023. Its affected region is: United Kingdom. Its affected key users are: National electricity users. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Cold snap, low wind generation, increased demand.

title: The never-ending story? More delays in return-to-service at both Callide C3 and C4
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "The never-ending story? More delays in return-to-service at both Callide C3 and C4" posted at 08-03-2023 11:27:00 AM is: The news discusses delays in the return-to-service dates for the Callide C Power Station's generating units, C3 and C4, both of which have experienced significant outages due to a cooling tower failure and an explosion, respectively. The C3 unit, offline since 31st October 2022, has had its partial availability pushed to 30th September 2023, with full availability expected by 31st December 2023. Meanwhile, the C4 unit, offline since 25th May 2021, has its return-to-service date pushed back to 31st October 2023, marking it as one of the longest outages in the history of the National Electricity Market. These delays are attributed to the need for rebuilding both cooling towers and reevaluating contracts with contractors, as stated in a CS Energy release. The delays are considered detrimental to future coal unit unavailability statistics and are influencing market prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: until at least the end of 2023. Its root_cause is: Cooling tower failure and explosion causing extended outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31st October 2022 for C3 failure and 25th May 2021 for C4 explosion. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and market stakeholders in Australia. Its cause type is: Technical and maintenance-related. Its causes are: Cooling tower failure at Callide C3 and explosion at Callide C4, rebuilding requirements, delays in contract processes with contractors.

title: NSW ‘Market Demand’ above 13,000MW on a hot Monday 6th March 2023 … and Actual LOR1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ above 13,000MW on a hot Monday 6th March 2023 … and Actual LOR1" posted at 06-03-2023 05:05:00 PM is: On March 6, 2023, New South Wales experienced high market demand exceeding 13,000MW due to hot weather, leading to an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition reported by AEMO. The demand surge led to tight supply-demand conditions persisting until 19:00 NEM time, although prices remained elevated but controlled. The forecast reserve requirement was 1365 MW, with only 794 MW available. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically until 19:00 on the same day. Its root_cause is: High demand driven by hot weather. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-03-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand-related. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand, resulting in a tight supply-demand balance and LOR1 condition.

title: Unavailability of coal units hits 24% across calendar 2022 (but they are *not* collectively tripping more often)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unavailability of coal units hits 24% across calendar 2022 (but they are *not* collectively tripping more often)" posted at 06-03-2023 08:30:00 AM is: The article, extracted from GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q4 2022, highlights an increasingly concerning trend of unavailability in coal units within the National Electricity Market (NEM), where these units collectively reached a 24% unavailability in 2022. This rise in unavailability is primarily due to prolonged forced outages at several key coal stations rather than increased frequency of trips. Such outages include a 19-month outage at Callide C4, significant downtime at Loy Yang A2, and Yallourn Unit 4. Despite these issues, some coal units maintain good performance, with five out of thirteen stations operating under 25% unavailability. The situation is anticipated to have future implications for reliability planning. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, as it affects future reliability planning. Its root cause is: Prolonged forced outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-03-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational failure. Its causes are: Prolonged forced outages at Callide C4, Loy Yang A2, and Yallourn Unit 4.

title: Forecasts for a hot Monday 6th March in NSW, and potential LOR2 in the evening (now also Tuesday evening)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecasts for a hot Monday 6th March in NSW, and potential LOR2 in the evening (now also Tuesday evening)" posted at 05-03-2023 01:29:00 PM is: The article reports on forecasted extreme heat conditions in Sydney and NSW, which could lead to Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) conditions as declared by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) for the evenings of March 6th and March 7th, 2023. AEMO has issued market notices indicating a shortfall between the forecast capacity reserve requirements and the minimum capacity reserve available, urging for a market response to prevent potential electricity shortages. This situation is compounded by the anticipated high electricity demand, marking the highest levels since June 2021 if forecasts hold; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediately, on 6th and 7th March 2023. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat causing increased electricity demand and reduced reserves. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 05-03-2023, 06-03-2023, 07-03-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and providers in the NSW region. Its cause type is: Natural causes and operational system strain. Its causes is: Forecasted heatwave leading to increased electricity demand, resulting in forecasted LOR2 conditions.

title: The NEMs frequency behaviour – how is it going?
author: Bruce Miller
classified_content: The summary of the news "The NEMs frequency behaviour – how is it going?" posted at 01-03-2023 11:49:00 AM is: The article reviews the challenges faced by the National Electricity Market (NEM) in maintaining frequency control as it transitions from a fossil-fuel-based system with high physical inertia to one reliant on wind, solar, and energy storage systems, which lack such inertia. The analysis highlights issues with the data resolution, signal synchronization, and inherent power system delays that make frequency behavior assessment complex. Despite these challenges, initial findings suggest that frequency disturbances are mostly due to market-driven generator dispatches and significant load changes, which occasionally trigger complex interactions with control systems. This ongoing transition requires diligent monitoring to mitigate risks of instability and potential blackouts.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing as the transition to renewable energy persists. Its root_cause is: Inherent challenges of transitioning from a high-inertia system to one with low physical inertia like wind and solar. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-03-2023 11:49:00 AM. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical and operational challenges. Its causes are: Transition to renewable energy sources, lack of system inertia in newer generation technologies, and complex interactions between generator dispatches and market signals.

title: A quick look at EnergyAustralia’s outage plans for Yallourn Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at EnergyAustralia’s outage plans for Yallourn Power Station" posted at 28-02-2023 01:57:00 PM is: EnergyAustralia has announced that its outage plans for the Yallourn Power Station have remained largely unchanged since October 2022, with planned outages for all four units continuing to overlap into 2023 and 2024, notably affecting available capacity. These plans are already factored into the AEMO's supply-demand modeling. Parallel plans at Loy Yang A and B could compound these effects, impacting supply-demand dynamics further. Despite recent media coverage, EnergyAustralia adjusts its outage schedule sparingly, approximately twice a year, aligning with their strategic planning needs.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 2023 and 2024. Its root cause is: overlapping planned outages at Yallourn Power Station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2023 and 2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Maintenance strategy. Its causes are: Overlapping unit outages planned for four units, affecting capacity.

title: Some revelations in GenInsights Q4 2022 about Self-Forecasting
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some revelations in GenInsights Q4 2022 about Self-Forecasting," posted at 27-02-2023 09:00:00 AM, is: The GenInsights Q4 2022 report examines the use of self-forecasting by semi-scheduled wind and solar farms in the National Electricity Market (NEM), detailing the progress and ongoing evaluation of this system since its inception. The report highlights the milestone developments including AEMO's implementation of improved ASEFS and AWEFS forecasts, leading to an impending end of a 'grace period' allowing self-forecasting errors, set for 28th February 2023. This systemic change aims to refine the accuracy of self-forecasting to better manage dispatch intervals, especially during complex semi-dispatch cap periods, and to better align the forecasts with the purpose of self-forecasting - enhancing grid reliability and potentially mitigating 'Causer Pays' liabilities for Regulation FCAS. The article anticipates observing system changes post-28th February as participants adapt to the forecast suppression policy. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market Reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Starting 28th February 2023 with ongoing impacts expected. Its root_cause is: A need to refine semi-scheduled forecasts to align with market reliability and regulation needs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-02-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled wind and solar farm operators. Its cause type is: Regulatory and operational adjustment. Its causes are: Suppression of non-performing self-forecasts, impending policy change by AEMO, market adaptation to improve forecasting precision.

title: Observation 15/22 from GenInsights21 – what is the purpose of Self-Forecasting?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Observation 15/22 from GenInsights21 – what is the purpose of Self-Forecasting?" posted at 27-02-2023 06:00:00 AM is: The article discusses the role of self-forecasting in the electricity market, particularly for wind and solar farms. Self-forecasting was introduced in 2018, allowing generators to provide their own forecasts to AEMO to potentially reduce forecast errors and give more autonomy to semi-scheduled generators. While over 50% of solar and 40% of wind farms have adopted this practice, its use is inconsistent, especially during constrained operations when it would be most beneficial. There is a disparity between the incentives for participants and the benefits to AEMO in terms of power system security, raising questions about whether self-forecasting during semi-dispatch cap conditions should require a rebid reason. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing since 2018. Its root cause is: Implementation of self-forecasting trials by semi-scheduled generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-02-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled wind and solar generators. Its cause type is: Policy and market practice implementation. Its causes are: Introduction and inconsistent application of self-forecasting, disparity in benefits between participants and grid security.

title: Recapping Thursday 23rd Feb 2023 (when hot weather drove demand in South Australia highest in 9 years)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Recapping Thursday 23rd Feb 2023 (when hot weather drove demand in South Australia highest in 9 years)" posted at 24-02-2023 10:26:00 PM is: On February 23, 2023, South Australia experienced its highest electricity market demand in nine years, driven by extreme heat that elevated demand to 3,142MW around 19:00 NEM time. This surge led to electricity prices exceeding $1,000/MWh during several dispatch intervals, peaking at $3,999/MWh. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) warning to alert of the tight supply-demand balance. The market dynamics were influenced by the limited output from solar and wind resources during early high-price periods, with gas and liquid thermal generation supplementing the grid. The use of interconnectors was maximized, with imports from Victoria. The situation revealed that actual demand was significantly higher than AEMO’s forecasts, posing challenges for grid management and highlighting the potential for similar volatility in the future due to limited green bid capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during the evening of February 23, 2023. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 23-02-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand surge. Its causes are: High temperatures, limited renewable output, maximum interconnector use, reliance on thermal generation, and forecast inaccuracies.

title: Has adding wind and solar upped the need for FCAS in the NEM?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Has adding wind and solar upped the need for FCAS in the NEM?" posted at 24-02-2023 01:33:00 PM is: The article explores whether the increase in variable renewable energy (VRE) sources such as wind and solar in the National Electricity Market (NEM) has led to a greater reliance on Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS), specifically Regulation Raise and Regulation Lower products. The analysis indicates that while there was a notable increase in the FCAS enablement quantities from 2018 to 2019, the actual utilisation of these services has not significantly increased over more recent years. Furthermore, mandatory Primary Frequency Response (PFR) implementation in late 2020 has reduced the need for Regulation FCAS by automatically involving almost all generators to address frequency deviations, therefore relieving pressure on centrally-controlled Regulation services; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Longer term, observing trends since 2011. Its root_cause is: Increasing penetration of variable renewable energy sources and implementation of mandatory PFR. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 24-02-2023 01:33:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy generators and market operators. Its cause type is: Systematic changes in energy generation and grid response mechanisms. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Increase in VRE sources, implementation of mandatory PFR, historical stepping up of FCAS enablement.

title: AEMO publishes an Update to the 2022 ESOO
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes an Update to the 2022 ESOO" posted at 21-02-2023 06:36:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released an update to the 2022 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO), addressing expectations for the closure of major power stations like Liddell and Eraring. This update focuses on evaluating the electricity market's readiness and challenges in accommodating these closures and the broader implications for energy supply and demand. This document forms part of a routine yet crucial assessment of the market landscape and was keenly anticipated by energy stakeholders. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term impact over the coming years as plants close and replacements are integrated. Its root_cause is: Scheduled closures of major power stations and the need for updated market assessments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-02-2023. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market stakeholders, including utilities, regulators, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Policy-driven market assessment. Its causes are: Scheduled power station closures necessitating assessments of energy supply and demand.

title: Current expectation for closure dates of Liddell and Eraring Power Stations
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Current expectation for closure dates of Liddell and Eraring Power Stations" posted at 20-02-2023 09:14:00 PM is: The article discusses the forthcoming closure of two major coal-fired power stations in Australia—Liddell and Eraring. Liddell's three units are expected to shut down by the end of April 2023. Meanwhile, Eraring is set for closure in August 2025, although the date might change. This potential reduction in coal capacity challenges transitioning to renewable energy sources and securing energy stability, especially as NSW has been reliant on energy imports. The article also touches on AEMO's ESOO update and the potential impact of delays in other projects like Snowy 2.0. Media coverage highlights the broader implications for energy policy and security as the Australian market transitions away from coal.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to long-term, affecting both 2023 and 2025. Its root_cause is: Imminent closure of Liddell and eventual closure of Eraring creating challenges in energy supply and transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 20-02-2023. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), and regions relying on NSW energy imports. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and industry stakeholders in NSW. Its cause type is: Policy-driven energy transition. Its causes is: Closure of coal-fired power stations, policy shifts towards renewable energy, and grid capacity concerns.

title: Price volatility in VIC and SA on a warm summer evening, Thursday 16th February 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in VIC and SA on a warm summer evening, Thursday 16th February 2023" posted at 16-02-2023 04:52:00 PM is: On February 16, 2023, spot electricity prices surged dramatically in Victoria (VIC) and South Australia (SA), exceeding $1,000/MWh and rising above $10,000/MWh later in the evening. The price hike was driven by elevated temperatures in the regions, leading to increased market demand, and constrained imports from New South Wales and Tasmania, creating an 'Economic Island' scenario where VIC and SA had to rely heavily on local gas and hydro resources. The decline in solar production and minimal wind energy, coupled with tight supply-demand conditions, further escalated the price volatility, prompting the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to issue Market Notices for forecast low reserve conditions (LOR1); Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term, impacting the evening of February 16, 2023. Its root_cause is: High temperature-driven demand and constrained inter-regional electricity imports. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-02-2023. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and weather-related. Its causes are: Elevated temperatures, constrained electricity imports from NSW and TAS, declining solar output, minimal wind energy, reliance on gas and hydro resources, and increased market demand.

title: GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q4 2022 released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q4 2022 released" posted at 16-02-2023 10:47:00 AM is: The article announces the release of the GenInsights Quarterly Update for Q4 2022, a 198-page report created by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting. The update examines various aspects of the energy market transition, focusing on topics such as system inertia, demand changes, wind and solar performance, dispatchable capacity, coal unit performance, and network dynamics. It reviews historical data to analyze trends and challenges in the electricity market, offering insights through an executive summary and six detailed appendices.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-02-2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Analytical Report Release. Its causes are: Analysis of Q4 2022 electricity market trends and performance factors.

title: Darwin to Dili: The contrasting challenges in Timor-Leste during an energy transition
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Darwin to Dili: The contrasting challenges in Timor-Leste during an energy transition" posted at 16-02-2023 10:45:00 AM is: The article discusses the significant energy transition challenges faced by Timor-Leste. The country continues to grapple with the impacts of past infrastructural destruction, leading to ongoing issues with reliable electricity access. Timor-Leste's economy is heavily reliant on dwindling offshore oil and gas reserves, alongside significant poverty and infrastructural challenges. The news also highlights the international dispute over the untapped Greater Sunrise gas field and the Timorese efforts to transition away from fossil fuels, although with limited progress due to financial and engineering obstacles.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Root_cause is: Historical infrastructural destruction and dependency on fossil fuel economy. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 16-02-2023. Affected region is: Timor-Leste. Affected key users are: Local residents and businesses in Timor-Leste. Cause type is: Historical/infrastructural. Causes are: Historical destruction, insufficient infrastructure, reliance on oil and gas revenues, international territorial disputes over energy resources.

title: QLD Market Demand reaches 9,512MW on a hot Sunday afternoon–12th February 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD Market Demand reaches 9,512MW on a hot Sunday afternoon–12th February 2023" posted at 12-02-2023 06:56:00 PM is: On Sunday, 12th February 2023, Queensland experienced a significant electricity market demand, reaching 9,512MW in the National Electricity Market (NEM) at 17:35, which was considerably high following another peak demand of 9,806MW just nine days earlier. This demand surge prompted the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to declare an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition for the QLD region from 18:30 to 19:00, due to a reserve deficit between the forecast requirement of 1187MW and the available minimum capacity of 1163MW; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during the peak demand period. Its root_cause is: High market demand on a hot day leading to reduced reserve margins. Its accident scale is: Not applicable, as no accident occurred. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 12-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in Queensland. Its cause type is: Demand-induced reserve shortfall. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Increased electricity demand due to hot weather, resulting in insufficient reserve capacity.

title: RERT cost estimates released for 3 February 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT cost estimates released for 3 February 2023" posted at 10-02-2023 05:11:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released estimated payments for the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) activation on February 3, 2023, to manage demand peaks. The total cost amounted to $1,475,000, leading to debates about the necessity of pre-activation and activation, considering market demand was 1000 MW lower than forecasted. The dispatch energy averaged $18,000 per MWh, exceeding the market price cap of $15,500 per MWh. Further event details are anticipated. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Overestimation of market demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Forecast errors. Its causes are: Weather-dependent demand forecasting, estimation errors leading to unnecessary RERT activation.

title: Callide C4 generator stator is now at Callide Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C4 generator stator is now at Callide Power Station" posted at 10-02-2023 02:15:00 PM is: The Callide C4 generator, which suffered an explosion and outage in May 2021, has received a key component for its repair—the replacement stator—marking significant progress in its restoration plan. CS Energy confirmed the expected return to service date as 1st May 2023, with incremental capacity increases scheduled through October 2023. However, the cause of the initial failure remains undisclosed. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate upon failure in May 2021, with ongoing impact until full restoration in 2023. Its root_cause is: Unknown, as the cause of the initial failure has not been finalized or released. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-02-2023. Its affected region is: Callide Power Station, Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown, with investigations ongoing. Its causes are: Explosion and fire leading to generator failure on 25th May 2021, ongoing repair process without disclosed root cause.

title: Spot volatility, and an Actual LOR1, in South Australia on Thursday 9th February 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot volatility, and an Actual LOR1, in South Australia on Thursday 9th February 2023" posted at 09-02-2023 08:04:00 PM is: The article reports on a brief period of market volatility in South Australia on February 9, 2023, resulting in an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition declared by the AEMO. This LOR1 condition indicated that the available capacity reserve was below the required level, with a deficit forecasted to persist until 8:30 PM. The minimum capacity reserve available was noted as 360 MW compared to the forecast requirement of 567 MW.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically for the duration of the LOR1 event on February 9, 2023. Its root_cause is: A shortage in electricity market reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-02-2023. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Insufficient available reserve capacity compared to the forecast requirement.

title: Weather! The cause of.. and solution to.. all the electricity market’s problems?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Weather! The cause of.. and solution to.. all the electricity market’s problems?" posted at 09-02-2023 05:31:00 PM is: The news highlights Queensland's electricity market dependency on weather conditions, with demand reaching nearly 9,750 MW amidst high temperatures and humidity, while a late storm alleviated the situation, revealing the challenges of market forecasting and system security. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, primarily on the day of the event. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather conditions causing high demand and affecting supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland, particularly during peak demand times. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: High temperatures, high humidity, low wind output, cloud cover reducing solar generation, late storm easing conditions.

title: Price/MWh Harvest in the 2022 Generator Statistical Digest
author: Nick Bartels
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price/MWh Harvest in the 2022 Generator Statistical Digest" posted at 09-02-2023 02:44:00 PM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2022 (GSD2022) provides detailed insights into electricity generation within the National Electricity Market (NEM) using availability and capacity factors classified by fuel types across various price bands. It highlights how wind and solar capacity is less available during high-price periods compared to fossil fuels and hydro, which exacerbates market challenges during demand peaks. The analysis points to challenges in the energy transition, especially regarding the timing of renewable generation when market capacity is most needed. It shows the critical role of dispatchable resources like gas and batteries during high-price periods. The report underscores the necessity for balancing renewable integration with reliable capacity to ensure market stability and energy affordability. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing. Its root_cause is: The variable availability and capacity factors of different energy sources during high-demand periods. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-02-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy stakeholders, including utilities and policymakers. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and energy generation profile. Its causes are: Renewable energy generation not aligned with peak market demand times; Insufficient capacity during high-price periods; Dependence on dispatchable sources like gas and batteries for reliability.

title: Investigating last Friday’s frequent frequency swings
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Investigating last Friday’s frequent frequency swings" posted at 08-02-2023 04:26:00 PM is: The news article analyzes frequency fluctuations in the National Electricity Market (NEM) observed last Friday, attributed to erratic solar output potentially from both expected and unexpected dispatch changes. The fluctuations were less severe than major system contingencies but noticeable enough to prompt a deeper investigation. Variations in solar generation, especially from grid-scale solar farms, contributed to the imbalances, as coal generation acted through Primary Frequency Response (PFR) to stabilize frequency levels. The analysis highlights the inherent forecasting challenges within the NEM's five-minute dispatch intervals that arise from variability in solar generation and stresses the significant role of fast-acting frequency control mechanisms like PFR and Regulation FCAS. The incident underscores the complexity of maintaining real-time supply-demand balance in light of substantial solar capacity in the NEM. The relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant).
