Its category is: Grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. The root_cause is: Variability in solar generation and dispatch forecasting errors. The accident scale is: System-wide with noticeable but controlled impact. The dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 08-02-2023. The affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), primarily Queensland. The affected key users are: Power system operators and, indirectly, energy consumers. The cause type is: Operational and forecasting challenges in solar power generation. The causes are: Variations in solar output, dispatch forecasting inaccuracies, and rapid changes in solar farm operations.

title: The GSD2022: A labour of love
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "The GSD2022: A labour of love" posted at 07-02-2023 11:18:00 AM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2022 (GSD2022), released by Global Roam and Greenview Strategic Consulting, presents a comprehensive 850-page analysis of 398 operating units from the Australian National Electricity Market with data spanning up to 10 years. The GSD2022 forecasts a significant increase in the share of wind and solar in the electricity generation fuel mix, expected to reach over 70% by 2030, driven by state government policies and expanding renewable resource utilization, particularly in Queensland. Pumped hydro and battery storage are noted as crucial for firming power supply to manage variability in renewable energy. The report highlights the continued relevance of coal, gas, and hydro units and their operating constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (spanning until 2030). Its root_cause is: Changes in the generation fuel mix due to policy-driven renewable energy adoption. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-02-2023 11:18:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia, specifically Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and market dynamics. Its causes are: Transition to renewable energy sources, grid management challenges, role of firming power solutions like pumped hydro, and evolving government policies on energy.

title: Demand shortfall dodges need for large demand side contributions, QLD, 3 Feburary 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand shortfall dodges need for large demand side contributions, QLD, 3 February 2023," posted at 06-02-2023 03:48:00 PM is: On 3 February 2023, AEMO predicted a historic peak in electricity demand in Queensland, prompting the activation of the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) to maintain grid stability. Although initial predictions indicated a lack of reserve, a decline in demand was seen due to unexpected rain and cloud cover. This reduced the need for substantial demand-side interventions. The RERT was dispatched for 2.5 hours, reflecting a total of 31 MWh distributed across 30 intervals, with varying MW levels. Even though additional reserves were pre-activated, the actual activation was significantly lower, with details on the total cost to be released later. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate (3 February 2023). Its root cause is: Unexpected weather changes leading to reduced demand. Its accident scale is: None (this was a grid management event, not an accident). Its dates are: 3 February 2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential consumers in southeast Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand fluctuation. Its causes are: Demand unexpectedly decreased due to rain and cloud cover, leading to less than expected grid strain.

title: QLD Market Demand for 17:30 lands at ‘only’ 9750MW (Fri 3rd Feb 2023)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD Market Demand for 17:30 lands at ‘only’ 9750MW (Fri 3rd Feb 2023)" posted at 03-02-2023 05:35:00 PM is: The National Electricity Market in Queensland observed a peak market demand of 9,750MW at the 17:30 dispatch interval, which was significantly lower than the earlier forecast of 10,656MW. This demand was also 369MW below the all-time maximum recorded on 8th March 2022 and 906MW below the extreme forecast for the day. The lower demand highlights the challenges in forecasting electricity demand, particularly with changing weather patterns impacting predictions.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term. Its root_cause is: Inaccurate weather-based demand forecasts. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand forecast inaccuracy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Discrepancy between forecasted and actual electricity demand due to unpredictable weather conditions.

title: The worm has turned?  Actual demand levels dropping lower than forecast (QLD 3rd Feb 2023) … maybe no record?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The worm has turned? Actual demand levels dropping lower than forecast (QLD 3rd Feb 2023) … maybe no record?" posted at 03-02-2023 04:50:00 PM is: The article discusses how cloud cover and rain have unexpectedly reduced solar energy output from large solar farms across Queensland, leading to lower-than-forecasted market demand levels. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) noted that actual market demand dropped below forecasts from 14:30 due to storm development, preventing a new record demand which was previously anticipated to surpass the 10,119MW peak from March 2022. This highlights the forecasting challenges faced by the AEMO in managing short-term weather-complex electricity market dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Unexpected cloud cover and rain affecting solar output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market operators and consumers in QLD. Its cause type is: Weather-induced supply-demand imbalance. Its causes are: Unanticipated storm development, reduction in solar capability, challenge in demand forecasting.

title: Spot price volatility has begun (in QLD on Friday 3rd February 2023)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility has begun (in QLD on Friday 3rd February 2023)" posted at 03-02-2023 04:31:00 PM is: The spot price for electricity in Queensland experienced significant volatility on February 3, 2023, with prices reaching $1,297/MWh during the 16:30 dispatch interval and an earlier spike to $1,201/MWh at 15:50. This price surge is attributed to a high market demand of 9,794MW, close to the all-time maximum. There is an expectation that demand will soon reach a new peak, potentially further impacting electricity prices throughout the evening. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: High market demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market-driven. Its causes are: High electricity demand, spot price spikes.

title: Wind not blowing in Queensland this afternoon as demand begins to rise
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind not blowing in Queensland this afternoon as demand begins to rise" posted at 03-02-2023 04:12:00 PM is: As demand in Queensland begins to rise sharply, output at the state's four utility-scale wind farms is notably low, generating only 50MW against a total maximum capacity of 813MW. Particularly, the Mount Emerald and Kaban Wind Farms in the north of the state are generating just one megawatt collectively, indicating a significant shortfall in wind energy production amidst increasing demand, which is forecasted to break records; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as demand is currently rising. Its root_cause is: Low wind speeds resulting in reduced wind energy production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Utility companies and consumers in Queensland relying on wind energy. Its cause type is: Natural, due to weather conditions. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Solar Production in QLD dropping faster than previously forecast" posted at 03-02-2023 04:09:00 PM is: Solar production in Queensland has been decreasing at a faster rate than initially anticipated, with rain affecting solar capabilities across semi-scheduled large solar farms in the region. title: RERT dispatched in QLD – at 17:30 on Friday 3rd February 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT dispatched in QLD – at 17:30 on Friday 3rd February 2023" posted at 03-02-2023 03:39:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a Market Notice at 15:31 on the 3rd of February 2023, announcing the dispatch of the Reserve Trader (RERT) in the Queensland (QLD) region due to an intervention event. This action was to maintain the power system in a reliable state, with the reserve contract activated from 17:30 to 20:30 on the same day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. title: Availability at Tarong North Power Station almost halved as it nurses a tube leak
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Availability at Tarong North Power Station almost halved as it nurses a tube leak" posted at 03-02-2023 03:15:00 PM is: A tube leak at the Tarong North Power Station has caused its electricity generation capacity to drop from 420MW to 200MW, leading to increased concerns amidst high demand forecasts in Queensland. Despite a temporary 50MW increase in the QNI limit, the reduction in availability represents a significant setback in handling the state's electricity demands; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Twelve hours of Mainland NEM System Frequency on Friday 3rd February 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Twelve hours of Mainland NEM System Frequency on Friday 3rd February 2023" posted at 03-02-2023 02:37:00 PM is: The article discusses the high electricity demand expected in Queensland on February 3, 2023, which may result in a new peak demand record for the region. Additionally, observations of the System Frequency in the NEM raise questions about variations in frequency during daylight hours, potentially due to solar power fluctuations and skewing low under 50Hz. The article also mentions the implications of these frequency patterns on grid stability and the evolving landscape of energy supply and demand dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate/short-term due to potential record-breaking demand and ongoing frequency variability. Root cause is: High demand in QLD and potential solar power variability. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates is: 03-02-2023. Affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Affected key users are: Unknown. Cause type is: Demand and supply dynamics. Causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High electricity demand in QLD; variability in solar injections; frequency bias issues during daylight hours.

title: Every extra little bit may help: 50MW temporarily added to QNI limit
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Every extra little bit may help: 50MW temporarily added to QNI limit" posted at 03-02-2023 10:18:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has temporarily increased the Queensland to New South Wales Interconnector (QNI) flow limit from 700MW to 750MW to conduct testing on the new limit. This change took effect from 12:00 AM on February 3, 2023, and will last until 11:59 PM on February 5, 2023. The testing is being conducted to monitor oscillatory stability and does not involve tripping and reclosing of a line in northern NSW.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Temporary, specifically from February 3 to February 5, 2023. Its root_cause is: Testing of flow limits on the QNI. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03/02/2023 to 05/02/2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Testing for grid management. Its causes are: Temporary increase in flow limits for QNI for testing purposes.

title: AEMO contracts Reserve Trader (in QLD, on Fri 3rd Feb 2023), but trigger not yet pulled
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO contracts Reserve Trader (in QLD, on Fri 3rd Feb 2023), but trigger not yet pulled" posted at 03-02-2023 07:48:00 AM is: AEMO issued a Market Notice indicating its intention to negotiate reserve contracts under the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) framework for the Queensland region on February 3, 2023. This follows a forecast of high demand and uncertainty in maintaining a reliable power system between 17:30 and 21:30 hrs on that day. The negotiation is a preparatory measure, and AEMO may implement an intervention event by dispatching the reserve contract if necessary, although the actual demand for reserve remains uncertain at this point.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Same day, February 3, 2023. Its root_cause is: High demand forecast. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Preventive market intervention. Its causes are: High electricity demand forecast, potential grid instability.

title: Quick Friday morning look-ahead to Friday evening in QLD
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quick Friday morning look-ahead to Friday evening in QLD" posted at 03-02-2023 06:27:00 AM is: The news article discusses the potential for high electricity demand in Queensland on the evening of February 3, 2023, with forecasts predicting it will surpass previous all-time records. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) notice, indicating tight supply and demand dynamics, with elevated prices expected for several hours. Despite this, there should be sufficient supply to meet expected demand with a small margin. Several coal units remain offline while gas-fired units are operating and expected to handle evening demand peaks. AEMO has noted the possibility of market intervention if necessary. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand forecasts exceeding previous records. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the QLD region. Its cause type is: Demand-driven. title: Forecasts pointing to record demand and other risks in QLD for Friday afternoon.. but caveats aplenty
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecasts pointing to record demand and other risks in QLD for Friday afternoon.. but caveats aplenty" posted at 01-02-2023 03:26:00 PM is: The article discusses AEMO's forecast for record electricity demand in Queensland on Friday, 3rd February 2023, potentially breaking records due to higher temperatures and humidity, coupled with cloud cover reducing solar output. Initially, demand was forecast considerably lower, indicating volatility due to changing weather conditions, which increase reliance on precise forecasting. This uncertainty has prompted the prediction of possible low reserve conditions (LOR2) and Reserve Trader deployment, despite being canceled and reinstated in short succession. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (focused on February 3, 2023). Its root_cause is: Weather-related factors leading to increased demand and reduced solar output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers, specifically those reliant on air conditioning. Its cause type is: Weather conditions and forecast uncertainties. Its causes are: High temperatures and humidity, reduced solar farm output due to cloud cover, and unpredictable demand forecasts.

title: A burst of heat and volatility in QLD on Tuesday 31st January 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A burst of heat and volatility in QLD on Tuesday 31st January 2023" posted at 31-01-2023 07:19:00 PM is: On January 31, 2023, Queensland experienced a significant increase in electricity market demand reaching up to 9,306MW, close to the all-time high, due to extreme weather conditions. This surge led to electricity prices exceeding $500/MWh, sometimes hitting the Market Price Cap of $15,500/MWh. Additionally, constrained imports reduced the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) to 7%, resulting in an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) and subsequently LOR2, as declared by AEMO. These conditions were alleviated by later market responses, leading to the cancellation of these LOR notices by 8:40 PM.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the evening of January 31, 2023. Its root_cause is: Increased demand due to high temperatures and associated stress on the electricity grid. Its accident scale is: Regional. Dates are: Same, 31-01-2023. Affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Cause type is: Market demand and supply constraints. Causes are: Extreme heat increasing demand, limited supply margins, and constraints on electricity imports.

title: Examining the $112/MWh revenue that Genex’ Jemalong Solar Farm generated in Q4 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examining the $112/MWh revenue that Genex’ Jemalong Solar Farm generated in Q4 2022" posted at 31-01-2023 04:17:00 PM is: The news article reviews the revenue figures from Genex Power’s Jemalong Solar Farm in Q4 2022, where the farm averaged $112/MWh including LGC certificates, down significantly from $180/MWh in Q3 and $214/MWh in Q2. This decline reflects a cooling of market spot prices following a coal and gas crisis, with volume-weighted average prices consistently dropping since a high of $303/MWh in June 2022. The article provides a deeper analysis of these figures, examining data from the Generator Statistical Digest 2022 and Green Energy Markets to contextualize Jemalong's performance amidst fluctuating energy prices.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, notable changes observed throughout Q4 2022. Root_cause is: Cooling of spot prices in electricity markets after a coal and gas crisis. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 31-01-2023. Affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Affected key users are: Solar energy stakeholders, market analysts, investors. Cause type is: Economic. Causes are: Decline in spot prices following market cooling, changes in revenue from LGC certificates.

title: Tuesday afternoon look at the (escalating) forecast for Friday 3rd February 2022 … but later the forecast LOR2 was cancelled, and then reinstated
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tuesday afternoon look at the (escalating) forecast for Friday 3rd February 2022 … but later the forecast LOR2 was cancelled, and then reinstated" posted at 31-01-2023 02:44:00 PM is: The article discusses the fluctuating forecast of a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition in the Queensland (QLD) region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) set for the evening of February 3, 2023. Initial forecasts indicated a substantial shortfall in capacity reserve, potentially leading to AEMO intervention. Despite an initial cancellation due to increased available generation, the LOR2 warning was reinstated as demand forecasts surged, surpassing previous records. Several power plant outages, including units at Callide, Gladstone, and Braemar facilities, contribute to concerns over supply adequacy, though availability may improve with peaking plants operating in the evening. The scenario highlights challenges in balancing supply and demand under potential high electricity demand scenarios in extreme weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (specifically for the evening of 3rd February 2023). Its root_cause is: High forecasted demand and ongoing generator outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 31-01-2023 and 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) region in the NEM. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and energy users in Queensland. Its cause type is: System and demand forecast instability. Its causes are: High market demand forecast, ongoing generator outages, including Callide units, fluctuations in available generation capacity.

title: Farewell Liddell
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Farewell Liddell" posted at 31-01-2023 11:57:00 AM is: The announced closure of Liddell Power Station in April 2023 will result in the loss of an aging coal-fired power station, reducing generation capacity by around 1,200 MW, significantly lower than its registered 1,500 MW capacity due to decreased performance over the years. Replacement capacity includes upcoming gas-fired projects and increased renewable energy, which are expected to offset Liddell's contribution by next summer, although the immediate winter period might face capacity constraints. The ongoing growth in New South Wales's renewable energy production has already surpassed Liddell's original output, addressing the bulk energy capacity concerns. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to medium term (2023 to summer 2023-24). Its root_cause is: Closure of aging coal-fired generation units at Liddell Power Station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-01-2023 (and upcoming closure in April 2023). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market and consumers relying on grid stability and energy supply in New South Wales. Its cause type is: Policy decision and aging infrastructure. Its causes are: Imminent closure of Liddell units, political and market pressures, replacement with new gas-fired projects and growing renewable capacities.

title: The Generator Statistical Digest 2022 (GSD2022) is released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Generator Statistical Digest 2022 (GSD2022) is released" posted at 31-01-2023 10:47:00 AM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2022 (GSD2022) has been released, updating prior issues with comprehensive details on participating units in the National Electricity Market (NEM). This extensive document provides insights into the electricity market over the past decade and highlights the shift from larger thermal units to smaller ones. The GSD2022, a collaborative effort involving Greenview Strategic Consulting and others, comprises 856 pages and includes detailed statistics for the year 2022. Analysts have contributed insights into the data, which is useful for market analysis and planning, and the document is available for order in PDF and hard copy formats, with an optional data extract.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing, providing data and insights for strategic planning. Its root cause is: The ongoing evolution and transition within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-01-2023. Its affected region is: Australia, particularly the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Energy analysts, policy makers, and market participants. Its cause type is: Market reporting and analysis. Its causes are: Data collection and analysis of market activities, unit transitions, and performance metrics across the NEM.

title: Forecast LOR2 (and new record demand) for QLD region on Friday 3rd February
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast LOR2 (and new record demand) for QLD region on Friday 3rd February" posted at 31-01-2023 09:18:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast for a Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) in the Queensland region on Friday, 3rd February 2023, due to high electricity demand coinciding with decreasing solar yield. A new record demand of over 10,000 MW is anticipated, surpassing the previous record of 10,119 MW set in March 2022. AEMO is seeking a market response to manage the forecasted deficit, with a capacity reserve requirement of 525 MW, but only 431 MW currently available. The forecast lack of reserve highlights the need for ongoing monitoring as the situation develops.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically on 3rd February 2023 during late afternoon to evening hours. Its root_cause is: High demand with low solar generation as solar yield decreases. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 31-01-2023, 03-02-2023. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Grid/system condition. Its causes is: High electricity demand, reduced solar and wind generation.

title: Might see more price volatility this week … at least in QLD?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Might see more price volatility this week … at least in QLD?" posted at 30-01-2023 09:53:00 AM is: The article discusses the potential for increased price volatility in Queensland (QLD), Australia, during the summer of 2022-23 due to expected high demand and temperature-driven stress on the electricity market. The AEMO issued a Market Notice in anticipation of high temperatures in Dalby, QLD, forecasted for February 3, 2023, urging market participants to adjust their capacity offers. This situation might lead to heightened electricity demand, potentially exceeding 10,000MW in QLD, mirroring everyday occurrences from a decade ago. Such conditions often create price fluctuations, especially when demand nears 'peak' levels, challenging supply-demand balance. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The week of January 30, 2023, to February 3, 2023. Its root_cause is: High temperatures affecting electricity demand and generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: January 30, 2023, to February 3, 2023. title: AEMO releases Q4 2022 Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED) report
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases Q4 2022 Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED) report" posted at 25-01-2023 11:17:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published its Q4 2022 Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED) report, which highlights a 57% decrease in wholesale electricity prices in the National Electricity Market (NEM) from the previous quarter, averaging $93 per megawatt-hour (MWh) during this period. This reduction is despite a continued higher average than Q4 2021’s $52/MWh, amid record renewable energy output and reduced coal and gas contributions. Early in the quarter, high commodity prices, planned coal unit outages, and tight gas supplies kept futures prices elevated but they declined sharply after the Federal Government's temporary caps were implemented on 9 December for domestic gas and thermal coal markets. Additionally, South Australia experienced high price volatility due to a transmission failure causing it to be disconnected from the NEM for a week. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term for Q4 2022, potentially extending into the following months as market adjustments continue. Its root_cause is: Decrease in prices driven by high renewable output and government intervention on gas and coal prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same as the main date. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions, with specific mention of South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and industry stakeholders. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and policy intervention. Its causes are: Record renewable energy output, reduced coal and gas contributions, high commodity prices, coal unit outages, tight gas supply, Federal Government intervention with price caps, and South Australian transmission failure.

title: Coal crunch within sight, with Eraring scheduled closure in 19th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Coal crunch within sight, with Eraring scheduled closure in 19th August 2025" posted at 24-01-2023 01:49:00 PM is: The article discusses the impending closure of the Eraring coal-fired station in New South Wales, scheduled for August 19, 2025. This closure will result in a significant decrease in coal capacity, as all four units (totalling 2,880MW) will go offline simultaneously. The article highlights how this will contribute to a substantial reduction in the available coal capacity in the National Electricity Market (NEM) and anticipates further outages for other coal units, exemplifying the challenges of transitioning energy sources over the coming decade; its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Beginning on 19th August 2025 and potentially extending beyond. Its root cause is: Scheduled closure of the Eraring coal-fired station. Its accident scale is: N/A (not an accident but a planned closure). Its dates are: 24-01-2023 (article date) and 19-08-2025 (closure date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the National Electricity Market. Its cause type is: Policy-driven infrastructure change. Its causes are: The planned transition away from coal-fired electricity generation and the scheduled closure of the Eraring power station.

title: A subdued start to summer 2022-23
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A subdued start to summer 2022-23" posted at 23-01-2023 10:21:00 AM is: The article discusses the subdued electricity market in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the summer of 2022-23, noting that there has not been a single day where the market demand peaked above 30,000MW, which is unprecedented in recent years. This lower demand has resulted in only three days of spot prices rising above $10,000/MWh, and even then, the spikes were brief. This could be attributed to moderate weather and other factors affecting demand. The situation will be monitored for the remainder of the summer.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Residential surveys. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2022-23. Its root_cause is: Lower-than-usual electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-01-2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Demand-side factors. Its causes are: Lower electricity demand due to moderate weather and reduced consumption.

title: Using the (draft of) GSD2022 to investigate those Solar Farms suffering significant curtailment
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Using the (draft of) GSD2022 to investigate those Solar Farms suffering significant curtailment" posted at 20-01-2023 06:21:00 PM is: The article discusses the analysis of solar and wind farm curtailment using the draft of the Generator Statistical Digest 2022 (GSD2022) and a study from the Australian National University (ANU). It highlights significant output losses in solar farms due to grid congestion, especially in Molong and Manildra in New South Wales, which saw curtailments of 53% and 48%, respectively, in 2022. Various curtailment causes are outlined, including network constraints, economic factors, and commissioning. The ongoing constraint equations impacting solar farms, particularly MANSLR1 and MOLNGSF1, are explored, revealing the complexities of determining the extent and cause of curtailments. The article underscores the necessity to consider these factors when analyzing renewable farm performance. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2022, with particular focus on the latter half. Its root_cause is: Network constraints and possibly economic factors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Throughout 2022. Its affected region is: Western New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar and wind farm operators, specifically at Molong and Manildra. Its cause type is: Infrastructure and market-related constraints. Its causes are: Grid congestion, economic curtailment, and constraint equations such as 'N>NIL_94T'.

title: South Australia’s Energy Minister triggers the Retailer Reliability Obligation (RRO) for SA in Q1 2026
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "South Australia’s Energy Minister triggers the Retailer Reliability Obligation (RRO) for SA in Q1 2026" posted at 10-01-2023 12:51:00 PM is: South Australia's Energy Minister, Tom Koutsantonis, has triggered the Retailer Reliability Obligation (RRO) for the first quarter of 2026, indicating a potential risk of electricity supply disruption. This move, which involves activating the Market Liquidity Obligation (MLO) for AGL and Engie, aims to increase investment in generation to support system reliability. This decision was made despite the AEMO's Electricity Statement of Opportunities not identifying a reliability gap for 2025-26; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market Reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: January 12 to March 13, 2026. Its root_cause is: The potential real risk of electricity supply disruption. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-01-2023 12:51:00 PM. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Retailers and large energy users. Its cause type is: Preventive action. Its causes are: Potential risk of electricity supply disruption, unclear plant closures, or changes in plant availability.

title: AER released its ‘Wholesale Electricity Market Performance Report 2024’

title: AEMC Final Determination in ‘Integrating price-responsive resources into the NEM’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC Final Determination in ‘Integrating price-responsive resources into the NEM’" posted at 24-12-2024 04:56:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) released its final determination on December 19, 2024, regarding the integration of price-responsive resources into the National Electricity Market (NEM). This initiative, known as "Scheduled Lite," aims to enable both large energy users and operators of smaller supply-side resources to participate more effectively in the market by responding to spot prices. While intended to facilitate demand response and virtual power plants (VPPs), the reform has spurred questions about its effectiveness and potential benefits, considering the complexity it adds to the NEM and previous reforms that have not met expectations.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. title: Claims of market manipulation against QLD Gencos (Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy) dismissed … part 3
author: James Prest
classified_content: The summary of the news "Claims of market manipulation against QLD Gencos (Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy) dismissed … part 3" posted at 23-12-2024 12:18:00 PM is: The Federal Court dismissed claims that Queensland-owned companies, Stanwell and CS Energy, engaged in unlawful market manipulation through strategic bidding in the National Electricity Market. The class action, led by Stillwater Pastoral Company on behalf of over 40,000 Queensland consumers, alleged that the companies intentionally caused power price spikes from January 2015 to January 2021. However, the court found no evidence of rebidding in bad faith or misuse of market power, stating profit-maximizing behavior was permissible. This ruling dismisses allegations of wrongdoing, but potential reforms or appeals may arise to address competitive dynamics in the electricity sector.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. title: Low ‘Market Demand’ … and Actual MSL1 … in Victoria on Sunday 22nd December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low ‘Market Demand’ … and Actual MSL1 … in Victoria on Sunday 22nd December 2024" posted at 22-12-2024 03:02:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a market notice for an 'Actual Minimum System Load (MSL1)' condition in the Victoria region on 22nd December 2024, due to low market demand. At 11:16 AM, demand was forecast below the MSL1 threshold, posing a risk to maintaining secure operations. By 2:33 PM, AEMO canceled the MSL1 event notice as conditions stabilized. This situation reflects a struggle to balance low demand driven by solar generation at rooftops and the need to keep coal plants operational for stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). title: Victoria soared to 47°C yesterday, for first time since Black Summer
author: Ant Sharwood
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria soared to 47°C yesterday, for first time since Black Summer" posted at 17-12-2024 01:54:00 PM is: An intense heatwave has caused temperatures to soar to 47°C in Victoria for the first time since the Black Summer, affecting large parts of eastern Australia. The extreme heat was recorded in several regions, including the Mallee and Wimmera districts in Victoria, eastern South Australia, western New South Wales, southwest and northwest Queensland, and southeastern Northern Territory. This heatwave resulted from northerly winds funneling hot air southwards, triggered by a high-pressure system over the Tasman Sea. The weather conditions are expected to persist into Tuesday, bringing high temperatures to eastern New South Wales. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Putting Victoria’s high demand levels (on Monday 16th December 2024) in longer-term context" posted at 17-12-2024 11:36:00 AM is: On December 16, 2024, electricity demand in Victoria, Australia, reached significantly high levels, yet it did not surpass historical peaks. Market Demand and Operational Demand, two metrics tracked by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), hit 9,887 MW and 9,851 MW respectively, indicating a substantial rate of consumption. However, these figures remain below the all-time maximums recorded in January 2014 and January 2009. While the demand was high, it reflects typical variations tied to factors like temperature and does not mark a record-breaking event when evaluating the data over the longer history of the National Electricity Market (NEM). title: Very fast FCAS, 2024 in review
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very fast FCAS, 2024 in review" posted at 17-12-2024 09:18:00 AM is: The article reviews the developments in the very fast Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets one year after their introduction on 9 October 2023. It highlights the removal of caps on enabled amounts, the necessity to maintain requirements for raise services, changes in capacities due to bidirectional unit transitions, and the relation of the service requirements to system inertia. The article discusses cost implications, particularly concerning the Raise 1-second service, which could represent the highest cumulative cost of 2024. Changes in market dynamics for South Australia and Victorian Big Battery obligations are also explored. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. title: A quick initial review of bids, for Monday 16th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick initial review of bids, for Monday 16th December 2024" posted at 17-12-2024 09:15:00 AM is: On Monday, 16th December 2024, there was significant activity in the National Electricity Market (NEM) driven by extreme weather conditions, with high temperatures recorded across major parts of the NEM. These temperatures elevated market demand to a four-year peak, which despite being managed without resorting to the previously forecasted load shedding, caused network congestion, particularly in western New South Wales and Victoria. The high temperatures impacted supply capabilities across various types of power plants, while network congestion created challenges, particularly for wind and solar farms. Region-specific data from Victoria showed that brown coal units were heavily utilized, and outages extended beyond planned dates for certain units. Despite these challenges, electricity prices remained stable, driven by strategic bidding patterns, including the use of low-priced bids to ensure dispatch. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term impacts on electricity prices and supply dynamics. Its root_cause is: High temperatures and network congestion. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-12-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Environmental and operational. Its causes are: High temperatures impacting demand and supply capabilities, network congestion, extended planned outages of critical units.

title: NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ peaks at 33,674MW at 17:30 on Monday 16th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide ‘Market Demand’ peaks at 33,674MW at 17:30 on Monday 16th December 2024" posted at 16-12-2024 07:10:00 PM is: The news highlights that on December 16, 2024, the NEM-wide market demand reached a peak of 33,674MW, which was the highest since January 31, 2020. This peak occurred during the 17:30 dispatch interval, slightly earlier and 44MW lower than the AEMO's forecast of 33,718MW for the half-hour ending at 18:00. Despite the elevated demand, particularly in NSW and VIC, electricity prices remained under control, assisted by wind and solar power contributions and manageable interconnector limitations. This reflects well on AEMO's operational forecasting. The article also notes alerts in the yellow range for some ‘Economic Islands’, indicating moderately tight supply-demand situations.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its dates are: 16-12-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions including NSW and VIC. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Demand spike. Its causes are: Elevated market demand, AEMO's accurate forecasting, manageable interconnector limitations, and significant wind and solar power contributions.
