title: Looking ahead (prior to 9am), to Monday evening 16th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking ahead (prior to 9am), to Monday evening 16th December 2024" posted at 16-12-2024 08:49:00 AM is: The article discusses the forecasted peak in market demand across the National Electricity Market (NEM) for Monday evening, 16th December 2024, which is expected to reach 33,653MW, marking the highest demand in nearly four years. Factors contributing to potential supply challenges include declining solar and wind generation due to the sunset and diminished wind capability. This comes as two coal units recently came back online, having faced issues during their return. The evening of 16th December is noted as a critical time for closely monitoring NEM's electricity supply and demand dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – specifically the late afternoon to evening of 16th December 2024. Its root_cause is: High forecasted market demand combined with reduced generation from solar and wind sources. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-12-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) coverage area. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Supply-demand imbalance due to environmental and technical factors. Its causes are: High electricity demand during the forecast period, sunset impacting solar generation, and decline in wind generation capabilities.

title: NEM-wide demand on Monday 16th December forecast to be highest in almost 4 years
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide demand on Monday 16th December forecast to be highest in almost 4 years" posted at 13-12-2024 03:17:00 PM is: The forecast for electricity demand on Monday, 16th December 2024, in the National Electricity Market (NEM) is expected to reach 33,718MW, which would be the highest in almost four years. Historical comparisons indicate that such a demand level has only been exceeded in nine months over the past 17 years. Warnings of possible load shedding for this date have been canceled, partly due to a delay in an outage on Line 17, although there remains a potential for significant activity in the NEM. The peak demand is anticipated for the half-hour ending at 18:00 NEM time on that day. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term (immediate conditions around December 16th and 17th, 2024). Its root cause is: High forecasted electricity demand. Its accident scale is: None (as the load shedding warning was canceled). Its dates are: 13-12-2024 and 16-12-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) region. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and grid operators within the NEM. Its cause type is: Demand forecast-driven. Its causes are: Increasing forecasted electricity demand approaching historical peaks, cancellation of load shedding warnings due to delay in outage on Line 17.

title: Warnings of possible LOR3 (i.e. Load Shedding) for Mon 16th and Mon 17th December 2024 cancelled … at least in (large) part due to delay in Outage on Line 17
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Warnings of possible LOR3 (i.e. Load Shedding) for Mon 16th and Mon 17th December 2024 cancelled … at least in (large) part due to delay in Outage on Line 17" posted at 13-12-2024 01:25:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has announced the cancellation of the forecasted LOR3 (Load Shedding) events for Monday, December 16th, and Tuesday, December 17th, 2024, primarily due to a 48-hour delay in the outage of Line 17, as indicated by the 'N-X_AVMA_KCTX' constraint set. Despite the cancellation, high demand forecasts for NSW, VIC, and NEM-wide remain, with potential lower-level load reduction conditions predicted for NSW and VIC on these dates. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 16th-17th, 2024. Its root_cause is: Outage delay on Line 17. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-12-2024 and 17-12-2024. Its affected region is: NSW and VIC, NEM-wide. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW and VIC. Its cause type is: Constraint set adjustment. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Delay in the 'N-X_AVMA_KCTX' constraint set, persistent high electricity demand forecast.

title: Some things that have changed, in the outlook for Mon 16th and Tue 17th December 2024 … and what’s not (yet) changed?!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some things that have changed, in the outlook for Mon 16th and Tue 17th December 2024 … and what’s not (yet) changed?!" posted at 13-12-2024 09:58:00 AM is: The recent forecast indicates increasing concerns of load shedding in New South Wales (NSW) on December 16th and 17th, 2024, with LOR2 conditions also anticipated in Tasmania. Compared to previous forecasts, demand expectations are slightly higher, and available generation has decreased notably due to an outage at the BW02 unit. Additionally, improvements are observed in solar and wind forecasts, although constraints from line outages remain an issue. The impacts are expected to last during these days, especially as network constraints continue to impose risks.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 16th and 17th, 2024. Its root_cause is: Forecast lack of reserve and network constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: December 13th for update, December 16th-17th for impact. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Electricity market operational challenges. Its causes are: High demand forecast, decreased available generation, network outages, and reduced solar and wind renewables' output.

title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Thursday 12th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Thursday 12th December 2024" posted at 13-12-2024 08:13:00 AM is: The article discusses an instance of evening volatility in electricity prices in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on 12th December 2024, with electricity spot prices exceeding $1,000/MWh between 17:50 and 19:30 NEM time, despite relatively modest levels of market demand. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during the evening hours of 12th December 2024. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-12-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Price volatility in the electricity market, spot price surges.

title: Friday morning forecasts for load shedding (i.e. LOR3) in NSW on Monday 16th and Tuesday 17th December grow more extreme
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Friday morning forecasts for load shedding (i.e. LOR3) in NSW on Monday 16th and Tuesday 17th December grow more extreme" posted at 13-12-2024 05:38:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued forecast notices for Lack Of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) conditions for the NSW region on Monday, 16th December, and Tuesday, 17th December 2024. These forecasts indicate potential load shedding, with the maximum interruptible loads expected at 573 MW and 376 MW respectively. AEMO seeks market responses and has not yet planned interventions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, affecting December 16th and December 17th, 2024. Its root_cause is: Anticipated supply shortfalls in reserve levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-12-2024 and 17-12-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Forecasted insufficiency in electricity reserves leading to potential load interruptions.

title: Victoria’s first 45°C in four years on the horizon
author: Ben Domensino
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria’s first 45°C in four years on the horizon" posted on 12-12-2024 04:15:00 PM is: An intense heatwave is projected to affect Victoria, Australia, with temperatures peaking at 45°C for the first time in four years, creating extreme fire danger conditions over the central and western parts of the state. The northwesterly winds will elevate temperatures, particularly on Monday, December 16, 2024, which will likely impact Melbourne as well. This meteorological event is part of a broader pattern of hot continental air masses influenced by an approaching cold front. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term, specifically through the current weekend and early the following week. Its root cause is: An intense burst of hot continental air mass combined with northwesterly winds. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: December 12 to December 18, 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy, mining, agriculture, ports, aviation, retail, insurance, broadcast media, digital media sectors, and residential consumers. Its cause type is: Meteorological. Its causes are: Hot continental air mass drifting over southeastern Australia, development of northwesterly winds, and approaching cold front.

title: Standalone solar and wind dominate list of 19 successful projects from latest CIS tender
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Standalone solar and wind dominate list of 19 successful projects from latest CIS tender" posted at 12-12-2024 03:09:00 PM is: The Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment, and Water (DCCEEW) announced the selection of 19 projects from 84 bids in the latest Capacity Investment Scheme (CIS) tender. These projects include standalone solar and wind farms, as well as hybrid projects with battery storage, demonstrating strong investor interest in renewable energy in Australia. The tender attracted bids proposing over four times the capacity offered, indicating robust competition. Successful developers include Elgin Energy, Lightsource BP, and Neoen. Future tenders are expected to bring additional capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. The root_cause is: Government renewable energy policy and investment incentives. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-12-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and market-driven. Its causes are: High investor interest in renewable energy projects, successful tender process by DCCEEW, strong participation from developers in CIS tenders.

title: AEMO publishes ‘NEM Local Temperature Alerts for NSW, SA, VIC from 12 Dec 2024 to 16 Dec 2024’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes ‘NEM Local Temperature Alerts for NSW, SA, VIC from 12 Dec 2024 to 16 Dec 2024’" posted at 12-12-2024 10:45:00 AM is: AEMO has issued a market notice regarding forecasted high temperatures in various regions of Australia, including New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria, from 12 to 16 December 2024. These elevated temperatures are expected to surpass the NEM Local Temperature Alert Levels, potentially impacting electricity demand and generation capacity. High temperatures can drive demand higher and negatively affect the performance of coal, gas, liquid-fueled, wind, solar, and battery generation assets, as well as exacerbate network limitations. Market participants are advised to review forecasts and adjust capacity in their dispatch offers accordingly. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 December 2024 to 16 December 2024. Its root_cause is: Forecasted high temperatures exceeding NEM Local Temperature Alert Levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12 December 2024 to 16 December 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity generation and distribution sectors. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand and reduced generation efficiency.

title: Load Shedding … i.e. LOR3 (briefly) *also* forecast for NSW on Tuesday 17th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Load Shedding … i.e. LOR3 (briefly) *also* forecast for NSW on Tuesday 17th December 2024" posted at 12-12-2024 10:40:00 AM is: The article reports an initial forecast of Load Shedding at the LOR3 level in New South Wales for Tuesday, 17th December 2024, which later dropped to LOR2. Additionally, a similar risk of LOR2 is forecast for Victoria on Monday, 16th December 2024, highlighting significant electricity supply challenges as the NEM-wide market demand is expected to exceed 33,000 MW for a period on that day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16th-17th December 2024. Its root_cause is: Projected supply shortage compared to demand. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 12th December 2024 (forecast update); 16th December 2024 (NSW & VIC forecast risk); 17th December 2024 (NSW forecast risk). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in New South Wales and Victoria. Its cause type is: Forecast supply-demand imbalance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Projected high market demand and insufficient supply forecasts leading to risk of load shedding in New South Wales and Victoria.

title: Updated forecast LOR3 (a.k.a. load shedding) in NSW for Monday 16th December 2024 grows wider and deeper … and some concern in VIC
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updated forecast LOR3 (a.k.a. load shedding) in NSW for Monday 16th December 2024 grows wider and deeper … and some concern in VIC" posted at 12-12-2024 10:24:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued updates on forecast electricity reserve conditions for New South Wales (NSW) and Victoria (VIC) for December 16, 2024. In NSW, a Lack Of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) condition may require load shedding between 15:00 and 19:30, with up to 733 MW potentially interrupted at peak time. Concurrently, LOR2 conditions were forecasted, indicating reduced reserve levels from 14:00 to 21:00. In Victoria, LOR2 conditions are expected from 15:00 to 18:00. The exact intervention measures by AEMO, if necessary, remain undecided, and the organization has sought generator recall information for both regions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Anticipated on December 16, 2024. Its root cause is: Reduced electricity reserve levels forecasted in NSW and VIC. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-12-2024 and 16-12-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW and VIC. Its cause type is: Grid reserve inadequacy. Its causes are: Lower than required reserve margins forecasted, potentially necessitating load shedding in NSW and VIC.

title: A first look into the LOR3 (a.k.a. ‘load shedding’) forecast for NSW on Monday 16th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A first look into the LOR3 (a.k.a. ‘load shedding’) forecast for NSW on Monday 16th December 2024" posted at 12-12-2024 09:42:00 AM is: This article discusses an alarming forecast for load shedding in New South Wales set for December 16, 2024, as announced by AEMO. The report highlights sustained concerning forecasts over eight hours, indicating high demand levels reaching around 12,700 MW, which exceeds previously observed figures. Contributors to this forecast include network congestion, particularly on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, limitations in renewable energy generation (solar and wind), and coal unit outages in the region. The root cause involves network constraints, especially an outage in the ‘Avon to Macarthur’ line and transformer issues at Kemps Creek. Continued generator outages and part-load operations further contribute to this potential power disruption; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: December 16, 2024. Its root_cause is: Network constraints and generator outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-12-2024 and 16-12-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and logistical. Its causes are: High demand forecast, network congestion, limitations on renewable energy, and multiple generator outages.

title: Surely not again so soon?! …. AEMO forecasts LOR3 (i.e. load shedding) for NSW on Monday 16th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Surely not again so soon?! …. AEMO forecasts LOR3 (i.e. load shedding) for NSW on Monday 16th December 2024" posted at 12-12-2024 05:33:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast for a Lack Of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) condition, suggesting potential load shedding in New South Wales on December 16th, 2024, due to anticipated insufficient reserves. This forecast warns of up to 116 MW load interruptions during two periods in the afternoon and evening. While the event is yet to occur, it follows a recent LOR3 forecast in November that led to high electricity prices and invoked reserve trader interventions. Concern arises over the ongoing challenges in the energy transition process; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short term, specifically on December 16th, 2024. Its root_cause is: forecasted insufficient electricity reserves in the NSW region. Its accident scale is: potential large impact with up to 116 MW load shedding. Its dates are: 12-12-2024 for the notice and 16-12-2024 for the forecasted event. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: general electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: operational reserve shortfall forecast. Its causes are: anticipated supply-demand imbalance and insufficient electricity reserves leading to a potential LOR3 event.

title: Mainland grid frequency: cat ears and golf
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland grid frequency: cat ears and golf" posted at 11-12-2024 04:54:00 PM is: The article discusses recent observations of the mainland grid frequency patterns showing a "cat ears" formation, which indicates significant variations around the nominal frequency of 50 Hz, due to the primary frequency response (PFR) deadband settings which begin to act beyond a 0.015 Hz deviation. The shifts result in frequency swings above and below 50 Hz with less stability at this level; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing as of 2024. Its root_cause is: Primary frequency response (PFR) settings. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-12-2024. Its affected region is: NEM Mainland (National Electricity Market in Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System operation settings. Its causes are: Variations around 50 Hz due to the PFR deadband acting beyond 0.015 Hz deviation.

title: Queensland electricity consumer class action unsuccessful: Stillwater Pastoral Company Pty Ltd v Stanwell Corporation Ltd [2024] FCA 1382
author: Connor James
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland electricity consumer class action unsuccessful: Stillwater Pastoral Company Pty Ltd v Stanwell Corporation Ltd [2024] FCA 1382" posted at 11-12-2024 03:06:00 PM is: The Federal Court dismissed a class action lawsuit involving allegations against Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy by Stillwater Pastoral Company, representing over 47,000 Queensland electricity consumers, for contravening Section 46 of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 through rebidding strategies during 1 January 2012 to 6 June 2017. The court found that the respondents did not possess substantial market power nor took undue advantage of it, and that their bidding practices were legal under the National Electricity Rules, reflecting on the complexities of market dynamics in the National Electricity Market.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown; Its root_cause is: Alleged misuse of market power and rebidding strategies.; Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 1 January 2012 to 6 June 2017; Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Over 47,000 Queensland electricity consumers; Its cause type is: Legal judiciary ruling. Its causes are: Rebidding strategies of Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy during peak demand intervals.

title: Submissions open today for the ‘Nelson Review’ into the NEM
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Submissions open today for the ‘Nelson Review’ into the NEM" posted at 11-12-2024 11:01:00 AM is: Two weeks after an announcement by Chris Bowen, a new independent panel led by Tim Nelson has been established to review wholesale market settings for the National Electricity Market (NEM) and propose reforms. The panel has released the Terms of Reference and opened submissions from the public, which will be accepted until February 14, 2025, with final recommendations expected in late 2025. The review seeks input from stakeholders across the NEM, focusing on five main topics: investment incentives, consumer interaction, changing nature of spot prices, essential services, and enhancing competition. It aims to adopt a holistic approach by considering global perspectives, academic and policy literature, and engaging in modeling and analysis to inform policy options.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as final recommendations are expected in late 2025. Its root_cause is: Initiative for market reform and policy optimization. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-12-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Market stakeholders including states, territories, market bodies, generators, retailers, and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Policy and market settings review. Its causes are: Establishment of an independent panel to review and provide reform recommendations for the NEM.

title: A quick look at bidding in QLD … back on on 18th February 2016
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at bidding in QLD … back on on 18th February 2016" posted at 11-12-2024 10:12:00 AM is: The article revisits and analyzes the bidding activities and market behavior of electricity suppliers in Queensland on 18th February 2016 utilizing a modern version of ez2view. The focus is on a specific dispatch interval that occurred within the 30-minute settlement framework of the National Electricity Market at the time, offering insights into past market dynamics discussed in the QUD19/2021 QLD Class Action.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Residential satisfaction, energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Historical analysis, no direct impact on current electricity market. Its root_cause is: Examination of past market practices as part of a legal case. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18th February 2016, 11-12-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Examination of historical bidding data. Its causes are: Revisiting historical market data, analysis of past bidding and settlement practices in the electricity market.

title: Dismissal of the class action against QLD Gencos (Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy) … part 1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Dismissal of the class action against QLD Gencos (Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy) … part 1" posted at 10-12-2024 09:47:00 AM is: The Federal Court of Australia dismissed a class action lawsuit against Queensland's state-owned electricity generators, Stanwell Corporation and CS Energy, which was initially filed on the grounds of anti-competitive practices, specifically short-notice rebidding that allegedly influenced electricity spot prices. The case was relevant as it involved market dynamics from 2013 to 2019, highlighting the complexity of electricity trading practices within the NEM (National Electricity Market). The dismissal concluded that the rebidding practices in question were not unlawful profit-maximizing behavior. Remaining matters like costs are deferred to March 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as the proceeding has been dismissed but may influence short-term market perceptions. Its root_cause is: Alleged anti-competitive rebidding practices by QLD Gencos that were explored in court but found to not constitute unlawful behavior. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-12-2024; the filing date is 20th January 2021. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and potentially consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Legal/judicial. Its causes are: Alleged short-notice rebidding intended to manipulate electricity spot prices, profit-maximizing strategies, and competition within the National Electricity Market.

title: Curtailment concerns provide a view of the future
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment concerns provide a view of the future" posted at 09-12-2024 03:13:00 PM is: The news discusses the curtailment of electricity supply in New South Wales (NSW) on November 27, 2024, particularly between 14:30 and 16:10, where up to 2,000 MW was curtailed due to network congestion rather than supply shortages. This curtailment led to low reserve levels, dipping into the LOR2 (lack of reserve) zone, although there was enough supply to avoid LOR1 conditions. The situation highlights the potential challenges in meeting demand by 2027, especially after the closure of the Eraring power station, despite anticipated additions of renewable energy capacity. The main concern is network congestion rather than supply, emphasizing the importance of strategic infrastructure placement. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potential future impact (2024-2027). Its root_cause is: Network congestion leading to curtailment. Its accident scale is: Regional (NSW). Its dates are: 27-11-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Infrastructure/operational constraints. Its causes are: Curtailment due to network congestion, closure of Eraring power station, reduced availability from Bayswater units, and anticipated additions of renewable energy sources.

title: ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria (apparently!) drops 3,100MW in 5 minutes, on Monday 9th December 2024 … a data glitch?!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'Market Demand' in Victoria (apparently!) drops 3,100MW in 5 minutes, on Monday 9th December 2024 … a data glitch?!" posted at 09-12-2024 03:03:00 PM is: The article reports an unexpected and rapid drop in 'Market Demand' in Victoria, where a decrease of 3,100MW was recorded in just 5 minutes, causing speculations of a data glitch. Despite the significant drop, grid frequency remained relatively stable, casting doubt on the legitimacy of the data. The incident is likely due to an anomaly in the AEMO data systems. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate potential impact with short-term anomalies. Its root_cause is: Suspected data system error. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-12-2024. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical error. Its causes are: Suspected gremlin in AEMO data systems.

title: ‘Market Demand’ in Victoria ramps by 903MW in 10 minutes, on Monday 9th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'Market Demand' in Victoria ramps by 903MW in 10 minutes, on Monday 9th December 2024" posted at 09-12-2024 02:42:00 PM is: During the afternoon of December 9th, 2024, the Victorian electricity market experienced an unprecedented surge in demand, with a 903MW increase over a span of ten minutes, notably detected by alerts set to trigger for changes exceeding 400MW. This significant ramp-up is regarded as highly unusual, with the overall demand rising from 2,679MW at 14:00 to 4,481MW at 14:25, marking a 1,802MW increase and a 67% growth. Speculation suggests that a sudden reduction in rooftop solar output due to cloud cover may have contributed to this spike. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate (within the day). The root_cause is: Sudden increase in market demand potentially due to cloud cover affecting rooftop solar generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-12-2024 (no different date was provided). Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand fluctuation. Its causes are: Uncharacteristic market demand increase, potential cloud cover affecting solar generation.

title: Mercury Rising: Recent NSW prices and the impact on customers
author: Oliver Nunn
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mercury Rising: Recent NSW prices and the impact on customers" posted at 09-12-2024 01:34:00 PM is: The New South Wales (NSW) region is experiencing unprecedented spot price levels in the National Electricity Market (NEM), comparable only to the 2022 energy crisis, primarily due to the onset of hot weather. Lack of Reserve (LOR) warnings have been issued, with load shedding narrowly avoided by triggering Reserve Trader (RERT). Prices exceeding $300 per MWh are at record highs, driven significantly by a rising market price cap and high-priced events even at moderate demand levels. This has caused considerable strain on system stability, with gas and hydro generators capturing significant market value, whereas solar returns remain modest. The situation indicates a turbulent market, suggesting potential further price increases, particularly affecting residential consumers during upcoming hot weather. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: As immediate and continuing into the summer of 2024-25 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather and market characteristics, including a rising market price cap. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-11-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential consumers and market participants. Its cause type is: Environmental and market-driven. Its causes are: Extreme weather conditions leading to high electricity demand, rising market price cap, and increased frequency of high-priced market intervals.

title: Eraring Unit 2 offline for ~13 days, with tube leak
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring Unit 2 offline for ~13 days, with tube leak" posted at 09-12-2024 01:32:00 PM is: Eraring Unit 2 experienced an unplanned forced outage due to a tube leak, going offline at 21:46 NEM time on 8th December 2024. It is expected to remain offline until 21st December 2024. This incident is part of a broader pattern of outages affecting other coal units like GSTONE5, Kogan Creek, and Millmerran Unit 2. These outages highlight the operational challenges faced by coal units amid the ongoing energy transition, revealing issues in maintaining energy supply without the services provided by stations like Eraring.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until 21st December 2024. Root_cause is: Tube leak causing the unit to go offline. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 08-12-2024 to 21-12-2024. Affected region is: Unknown. Affected key users are: Unknown. Cause type is: Technical failure. Causes are: Tube leak causing unplanned forced outage.

title: Frequency Performance Payments project commences non-financial operation, 9th December 2024
author: Adam Kent
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency Performance Payments project commences non-financial operation" posted at 09-12-2024 11:39:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO)'s Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) project has begun its non-financial operation phase. During this trial period, market participants will familiarize themselves with the new system, which reallocates incentive payments to generators based on their contribution to maintaining system frequency. This replaces the existing "Causer Pays" scheme and involves data reporting through new MMS tables. The trial will continue until mid-2025, with full financial operation scheduled for June 8, 2025, alongside the current Causer Pays arrangements until July 8, 2025. The trial introduces high-resolution frequency data and will update its reporting capabilities starting mid-January. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Mid 2025. Its root_cause is: Implementation of the new Frequency Performance Payments project. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-12-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants. Its cause type is: Policy change and implementation trial. Its causes are: Introduction of FPP as a successor to Causer Pays; commencement of non-financial operation phase; scheduled transition to financial operation.

title: Evening volatility (and high demand) in QLD on Sunday 8th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility (and high demand) in QLD on Sunday 8th December 2024" posted at 08-12-2024 06:07:00 PM is: The article highlights a period of volatility in the Queensland electricity market on the evening of December 8, 2024. During the 18:00 dispatch interval, Queensland experienced a significant supply-demand imbalance, indicated by an IRPM (Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin) of less than 10%, largely due to constraints on the Queensland grid and the Directlink interconnector being down. This situation marks the onset of a volatile period, with less impact observed in New South Wales. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate night-time impact with potential longer-term effects if constraints persist. title: Initial Review of price volatility in NSW in early afternoon Friday 6th December 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial Review of price volatility in NSW in early afternoon Friday 6th December 2024" posted at 07-12-2024 05:33:00 PM is: The article reviews a significant price volatility event in the New South Wales (NSW) electricity market on December 6, 2024, characterized by spikes, particularly a peak of $17,480/MWh at 2:30 PM. Factors contributing to this volatility included rising market demand combined with a gradual decline in available generation, inter-regional congestion that isolated NSW from Queensland (QLD) and Victoria (VIC) support, a thinly distributed supply curve with high bids, crucial constraint equations especially 'N::N_CTMN_2', and less effective auto-bidders. These dynamics, aggravated by a limited energy bid and possible commissioning or storage issues, highlighted the tight supply-demand balance and the importance of constraints in pricing.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Imbalance in supply-demand alongside operational constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-12-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Economic/Operational. Its causes are: Rising demand, inter-regional congestion, constraints impacting dispatch, high bid prices due to low supply volume, and imperfect auto-bidding systems.

title: Today’s discussion (at the Senate Committee) of planned or unplanned outages in the NSW supply fleet on Wednesday 27th November 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Today’s discussion (at the Senate Committee) of planned or unplanned outages in the NSW supply fleet on Wednesday 27th November 2024" posted at 05-12-2024 07:47:00 PM is: The Senate Committee on Energy Planning and Regulation in Australia discussed planned and unplanned outages that affected New South Wales (NSW) on November 27, 2024. The outages, which involved five significant units in the NSW energy supply, coincided with a period of tight supply-demand balance, leading to actual and forecasted Lack of Reserve (LOR) conditions and the activation of the Reserve Trader mechanism. Three units were reported as planned outages, while two were unplanned. The distinction between planned and unplanned outages remains complex due to the subjectivity in how outages are classified. This issue's scrutiny comes as outages—both planned and extended without explicit reasons—impact the reliability and cost of electricity supply. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, around the time of the outages on November 27, 2024. Its root_cause is: Simultaneous generator outages during critical supply-demand balance period. The accident scale is: Regional impact within New South Wales. The dates are: 27th November 2024. The affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. The affected key users are: Electricity consumers in New South Wales, including residential and industrial users. The cause type is: Equipment failures and maintenance. The causes are: Planned and unplanned outages of five significant generator units, extended maintenance periods for some units.

title: Mothballed diesel generators might be brought back in SA
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mothballed diesel generators might be brought back in SA" posted at 05-12-2024 02:03:00 PM is: The South Australian (SA) Government is urgently seeking to reactivate the mothballed Snuggery and Port Lincoln diesel power stations by proposing a rule change that would allow these stations to be eligible for Interim Reliability Reserves (IRR) under AEMO's RERT mechanism. This initiative is driven by delays in the Project Energy Connect (PEC), which is critical for SA's future reliability, alongside the limited pool of out-of-market reserves within the state. The state government has requested these generators be considered for a period of two years to address the identified electricity supply shortfall. The PEC project involves a critical transmission link upgrade from SA to New South Wales and Victoria, impacting SA's import capacity, although risks concerning negative settlements residues exist. Coverage of this proposal has been observed in several media outlets, highlighting its importance. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to two years (for proposed generator activation). Its root cause is: Delays in Project Energy Connect affecting supply reliability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2024, with a noted prior date of relevance on 1 July 2024 (date of the previous market dispatch of generators). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System and policy-driven. Its causes are: Project Energy Connect delays, limited out-of-market reserve options in SA, and insufficient reliability reserves under current conditions.

title: AEMO’s 2024 Intermittent Generator Forum provides renewed awareness for wind and solar generators
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO’s 2024 Intermittent Generator Forum provides renewed awareness for wind and solar generators" posted at 04-12-2024 12:13:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) hosted its annual Intermittent Generator Forum on December 3, 2024, covering a wide range of topics pertinent to wind and solar generators. Key discussions included recent improvements in AEMO's forecasting systems, developments in managing intermittent generator availability, and the need for enhanced control of inverter-based resources for system security. Notable advancements showcased were enhancements in forecasting models allowing for improved dispatch forecast accuracy and faster self-forecast submissions. A new weather data initiative was introduced, aiming to leverage weather observation data for better wind and solar forecasts. Future plans include further upgrades to forecast models and enhanced control systems for inverter responses. The forum highlighted the operational challenges faced by AEMO, especially around real-time management of inverter settings, often necessitated through numerous phone calls due to the ambiguity of withdrawing inverter energy systems under various conditions. Anticipated improvements and collaboration opportunities with the industry were emphasized throughout the session. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short to medium term. Its root cause is: Evolving operational needs and forecasting system enhancements for renewable energy integration. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: December 3, 2024. Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Wind and solar generators (semi-scheduled). Its cause type is: Operational and policy considerations. Its causes are: Enhancements in forecasting systems, operational challenges with inverter management, and the introduction of a new weather data initiative.

title: AER sends clear message on self-forecasting behaviours
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER sends clear message on self-forecasting behaviours" posted at 04-12-2024 12:12:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) expressed significant concerns over the biasing of self-forecasts for commercial benefit in the electricity market. During the December 2024 AEMO Intermittent Generator Forum, AER emphasized the importance of accurate and unbiased self-forecasts to support power system security and efficient market operations. AER plans to monitor compliance continuously and is hopeful that the upcoming Frequency Performance Payments (FPP) mechanism will deter biased forecasting behaviors. The AER remains open to considering rule changes if these issues persist. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Biasing of self-forecasts by generators for commercial benefit. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 04-12-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market operators and semi-scheduled generators. Its cause type is: Market behavior and compliance issue. Its causes is: Concerns about the biasing of self-forecasts, impacts on system security, dispatch outcomes, and competitive advantages.

title: Wrap-up of price spikes in November 2024
author: Carl Daley
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wrap-up of price spikes in November 2024" posted at 04-12-2024 10:34:00 AM is: The article provides an analysis of extreme electricity price spikes experienced in the last week of November 2024, drawing parallels to similar events in November 1997. The price spikes in New South Wales (NSW) were attributed to high demand, climatic conditions, and market predictions. The article highlights a peculiar instance on 29 November where demand forecasts were relatively low but extreme prices were still predicted, with a significant price spike occurring at 7:00 AM. Contributing to the volatility, as noted by WattClarity, were inconsistencies in the AEMO dispatch engine's forecasting, leading to price moderation rather than sustained spikes. The forward market forecasts also adjusted downwards after these events. The observations across different regions, including Victoria and Queensland, show varied spot price performances, with NSW in the top quartile due to recent extreme prices and Victoria in the bottom quartile having not yet shown strength this quarter. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, occurring end of November 2024. Its root_cause is: High demand forecasts, climatic conditions, and dispatch forecasting inconsistencies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27 and 29 November 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Market players and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Market and environmental. Its causes are: High electricity demand, AEMO forecasting inconsistencies, historic price patterns.

title: High prices in NSW eventuate early, and briefly, on Tue 3rd Dec 2024 as Collector-Marulan 330kV outage invoked
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "High prices in NSW eventuate early, and briefly, on Tue 3rd Dec 2024 as Collector-Marulan 330kV outage invoked" posted at 03-12-2024 12:47:00 PM is: This morning, NSW experienced a brief period of extremely high electricity prices, with prices spiking above $10,000/MWh for four intervals due to the invocation of the 'N-CTMN_4_WG_CLOSE' constraint set, which coincided with the outage on the Collector-Marulan 330kV line. This event is scheduled to last until December 14. Prices have since stabilized, though forecasts suggest further high prices later in the day. The spike was impacted by negative local price adjustments for wind farms in the Goulburn area, significant interconnector flows, and a counter-price flow from NSW to VIC. Key dynamics included constraints like the negative residue management affecting the "NRM_NSW1_VIC1" and increased generation at Tumut 3. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Brief period on December 3, with potential further impacts through December 14. Its root_cause is: Collector-Marulan 330kV outage and associated constraint set. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-12-2024 and potentially impacting until 14-12-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Constraint and market dynamics. Its causes are: Collector-Marulan 330kV outage, invoked constraint set, negative local price adjustments for Goulburn wind farms, interconnector flow limits, counter-price flow from NSW to VIC, negative residue management constraint, Tumut 3 generation changes.

The report aggregates data by fuel type, discussing dispatch availability, targets, and the non-dispatched energy due to network curtailment and pricing. Key insights include considerable offline coal capacity, hydro usage impacted by network constraints, solar curtailment due to cloud cover, wind dispatch challenges in the afternoon, and brief gas and battery storage curtailments. title: AEMO releases Transition Plan Report, December 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases Transition Plan Report, December 2024" posted at 02-12-2024 10:04:00 AM is: AEMO has released its Transition Plan for System Security, marking the first of what is expected to be an annual publication. The report outlines a structured approach to maintaining power system security amidst the transition to a low-emission power system, categorized into three planning horizons: operational, mid (2-5 years), and long (5+ years). It also discusses AEMO's initial steps in employing the new Transitional Services procurement framework to uphold system security during this transition. Coinciding with this release, AEMO highlighted a report on managing minimum system load, emphasizing that they have not requested emergency powers to control rooftop solar but have warned about potential blackouts due to high solar power output. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. title: High NSW price forecasts not materialising in dispatch, 29th November 2024
author: Adam Kent
topic: How you might use our software
classified_content: The summary of the news "High NSW price forecasts not materialising in dispatch, 29th November 2024" posted at 29-11-2024 02:04:00 PM is: The article discusses an anomaly in predispatch electricity price forecasts in NSW on November 29, 2024. Despite alarming forecasts predicting prices near the Market Price Cap (MPC), actual dispatch prices have remained moderate throughout the day. Factors such as a constraint equation N::N_CNSD_2, meant for a scheduled transmission maintenance, are mentioned. The dispatch and predispatch outcomes have varied significantly due to differing equations used in forecasting, leading to less severe pricing impacts in the dispatch phase. The investigation into these forecasts was conducted using the software ez2view. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. title: How western Sydney was the hottest place in the world on Wednesday morning
author: Ben Domensino
classified_content: The summary of the news "How western Sydney was the hottest place in the world on Wednesday morning" posted at 27-11-2024 02:45:00 PM is: A severe early-season heatwave has engulfed Sydney and surrounding areas in eastern New South Wales, resulting in extraordinarily high temperatures, particularly in western Sydney. On Wednesday morning, Penrith recorded a soaring 36.5°C, making it the hottest place globally at that time. This extreme heat is attributed to a low-pressure trough drawing warm air towards eastern NSW, combined with the urban heat island and foehn effects in the Sydney Basin. The Southern Hemisphere's seasonal differences also contributed to Australia experiencing such extreme temperatures, with other global heat-prone regions relatively cooler. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. title: The forecasts for LORx in NSW on Tuesday 26th November 2024 have been dialled down considerably … why is that the case?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The forecasts for LORx in NSW on Tuesday 26th November 2024 have been dialled down considerably … why is that the case?" posted at 22-11-2024 10:02:00 AM is: The article discusses the revised forecasts for Lack of Reserve (LOR) conditions in New South Wales (NSW) on 26th November 2024, which have been downgraded from more severe predictions. Step changes in the forecasts indicate a shift from LOR3 to LOR2, and later from LOR2 to LOR1, primarily due to changes in power line outage schedules and increased wind power generation forecasts. Originally scheduled outages on key transmission lines were deferred and resubmitted for a later date, reducing the immediate risk to electricity supply. Both the reassessment of network outages and increased wind energy estimates contributed to easing the forecast constraints.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. title: Delta’s ‘relatively urgent’ Rule Change reverts to the (slower) ‘standard rules consultation process’ at the AEMC
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delta’s ‘relatively urgent’ Rule Change reverts to the (slower) ‘standard rules consultation process’ at the AEMC" posted at 21-11-2024 10:48:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has reverted to the standard rules consultation process to assess Delta Electricity's rule change proposal, which aims to amend the National Electricity Rules. This switch occurred after AGL Energy objected to using an expedited process, leading to the delay. The rule change involves allowing cash as credit support, which is seen as urgent due to the upcoming expiration of Delta’s current credit support arrangements by the end of 2024. Stakeholders were invited to submit feedback by 21 November 2024, and while the AEMC is yet to publish these submissions, the ongoing consultation aims to address credit support challenges before the closure of Vales Point in 2033. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, potentially impacting arrangements by the end of 2024. Its root_cause is: Objection to expedited process for rule change. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-11-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Delta Electricity, AEMC stakeholders. Its cause type is: Procedural change in consultation process. Its causes are: AGL Energy’s objection to the expedited process, expiration of Delta’s credit support arrangements.

title: Here’s a few things to watch, w.r.t. the forecast tight supply-demand balance in NSW (and QLD) for early next week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Here’s a few things to watch, w.r.t. the forecast tight supply-demand balance in NSW (and QLD) for early next week" posted at 20-11-2024 07:28:00 PM is: The article discusses the anticipated tight electricity supply-demand balance in New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) on the afternoons and evenings of 25th and 26th November 2024. AEMO forecasts a potential LOR3 condition in NSW, indicating risks of load shedding and volatility. Significant constraints are expected on interconnectors and transmission pathways, including the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector and certain 330kV lines, which could limit support from other regions. Demand forecasts for NSW are notably high, and wind generation is expected to contribute modestly, while solar outputs decrease post-sunset. Constraints and planned outages on the network are highlighted, with potential market responses expected to mitigate risks. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, affecting 25th and 26th November 2024. Its root cause is: Network constraints and outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25th and 26th November 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Network infrastructure constraints and outages. Its causes are: Constraints on VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, outages on 330kV lines, and forecast high electricity demand.

Forecast LOR3 for NSW on Monday 26th November 2024" posted at 20-11-2024 05:54:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a Forecast Lack Of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) condition for the New South Wales (NSW) region, indicating a potential risk of electricity supply shortfall from 1530 to 1900 hours on 26th November 2024. A maximum load interruption of 12 MW is forecasted for 1730 hours. AEMO is actively seeking market responses to address this potential shortfall and has not yet determined the need for intervention. title: An inter-regional interconnector refers to *the whole of* the transmission path between RRNs
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An inter-regional interconnector refers to *the whole of* the transmission path between RRNs" posted at 20-11-2024 12:21:00 PM is: The article from Global-Roam Pty Ltd sheds light on the definition and function of inter-regional interconnectors in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It explains how these interconnectors are not just boundary markers but represent the entire transmission path between Regional Reference Nodes (RRNs). The article addresses previous confusion about their role in NEMDE and highlights their relevance by discussing recent constraints and northern volatility issues, particularly looking ahead to less favorable forecasts for New South Wales on 25th and 26th November 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Potential short-term impact during forecasted dates. Its root_cause is: Network constraints and volatility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-11-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical/Operational. Its causes are: Confusion about interconnector functions and recent constraint events causing northern volatility.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bayswater Unit 4 begins to return to service – Wednesday morning 20th November 2024" posted at 20-11-2024 08:58:00 AM is: Bayswater Unit 4 has started its return to service process, operating at 6MW as of 07:36 NEM time, which is crucial given the forecasted deep LOR2 conditions for New South Wales early the following week. This development brings relief amidst concerns over possible load shedding under potential LOR3 on 25th and 26th November 2024. The BW04 unit's return offers some reassurance to market participants, especially as its sibling, BW03, is expected to go offline for an unplanned outage soon. Its relevance level is: Level 1. title: Load shedding (LOR3) projected for NSW on 26 November 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Load shedding (LOR3) projected for NSW on 26 November 2024" posted at 19-11-2024 04:27:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a market notice for a projected Lack of Reserve level 3 (LOR3) condition expected in New South Wales on 26 November 2024. This occurs when reserves are insufficient to meet forecasted demand, prompting alerts to the market to encourage responses. The notification highlights a potential load interruption of 199 MW between 1600 hrs and 1900 hrs, driven by factors like low wind turbine generation and multiple planned and unplanned outages at key facilities, including Bayswater Unit 3 and other planned outages affecting substantial capacity. As the market is expected to respond, changes in the situation over the coming days are anticipated. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. title: Interruption to electricity market data on 19 November, 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Interruption to electricity market data on 19 November, 2024" posted at 19-11-2024 03:17:00 PM is: An operational glitch caused an interruption to the NEM wholesale market data service on November 19, 2024, at approximately 13:20, which was resolved by 13:55. The incident, identified as INC0138395 and communicated in market notice 120661, involved a temporary disruption attributed to maintenance activities involving the movement of EMMS production systems to an alternative datacentre. Consumers experienced a delay in Dispatch cycles and bid submissions, and four Dispatch Intervals were missed during the outage. The Electricity Market Management System service was restored, and the situation resolved without lasting consequences. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Maintenance activities involving system transition. Its accident scale is: Localized (affecting only data services). Its dates are: 19-11-2024. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Industry participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational glitch during system maintenance. Its causes are: Movement of EMMS production systems to an alternative datacentre, maintenance activities not anticipated to impact but led to delays.

title: AEMO MT PASA update on 19th November 2024 notes Low Reserve Conditions for NSW and Victoria for summer 2024-25
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO MT PASA update on 19th November 2024 notes Low Reserve Conditions for NSW and Victoria for summer 2024-25" posted at 19-11-2024 08:38:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a Reserve Notice identifying Low Reserve Conditions (LRC) for New South Wales and Victoria for the summer 2024-25 period. This is due to forecasted annual unserved energy surpassing the Reliability Standard, indicating a tight supply-demand balance. Specifically, New South Wales faces potential supply shortfalls from November 2024 through February 2025, while Victoria's critical periods include November 2024 and January to March 2025. The warning aims to alert the electricity market, providing an opportunity for response and possibly averting mandatory interventions by AEMO. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: November 2024 to March 2025. Its root_cause is: Forecasted shortfall in electricity supply versus demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-11-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast issue in electricity reliability. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Forecast demand exceeding supply, unserved energy levels surpassing Reliability Standard, declaration of Low Reserve Conditions by AEMO.

title: AEMO updates forecast LOR2 for NSW for Monday 25th November 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO updates forecast LOR2 for NSW for Monday 25th November 2024," posted at 19-11-2024 08:27:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) warning for the New South Wales (NSW) region, indicating potential risks of electricity supply shortage on Monday, 25th November 2024. This alert is due to anticipated market conditions during a period of high demand between 15:30 and 19:30 on the specified day, where the forecasted reserve requirement is 730 MW with only 93 MW of minimum available capacity. The forecast aims to prompt a market response to stabilize supply-demand balance and mitigate market volatility. AEMO's notice serves as an early indicator and does not yet necessitate intervention. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 18th November 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW on Monday 18th November 2024" posted at 18-11-2024 08:49:00 PM is: The article details the evening electricity price volatility experienced in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on 18th November 2024. It highlights that several constraint equations, particularly 'N::N_MNYS_2', 'N>>5_998_4', and 'N>>5_998_61', contributed to the volatility during a two-hour period. These constraints are part of the ‘N-MNYS_5_WG_CLOSE’ Constraint Set, which has been observed to limit the northward flow of electricity from Victoria to NSW, thereby exacerbating the price volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, affecting market volatility on the day of the event. Its root cause is: Constraint equations limiting power flow. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-11-2024. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Grid management issues due to constraint equations. Its causes are: Constraint equations 'N::N_MNYS_2', 'N>>5_998_4', 'N>>5_998_61' affecting electricity flow.

title: A question about (currently) planned outages for Eraring Power Station through until (currently planned closure) in August 2027
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A question about (currently) planned outages for Eraring Power Station through until (currently planned closure) in August 2027" posted at 18-11-2024 04:07:00 PM is: The article discusses the planned outages for the Eraring Power Station, which was originally set to close in August 2025 but received a closure extension to August 2027, with a potential further extension to August 2029. The focus is on the two planned outages: a lengthy one for Unit 4 from September to November 2025, and a shorter one for Unit 1 in April 2026. The issue emphasizes the impact of such extensions on maintenance plans, hedge contract prices, and potentially evolving outage plans over the extended service life of the power station. title: Burst of volatility in QLD and NSW, on Friday evening 15th November 2024

It highlights that spot prices rose in these regions due to constraints on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector, preventing electricity flow in both directions. title: NEM Mainland frequency patterns – historical overview from 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM Mainland frequency patterns – historical overview from 2024" posted at 15-11-2024 03:46:00 PM is: The article takes an updated historical overview of frequency patterns in the NEM mainland using data from Global-Roam's high-speed frequency monitor, highlighting several shifts over the years. Initially, grid frequency was tight around 50 Hz in 2011 but widened significantly by 2017 due to regulatory changes and the introduction of semi-dispatch caps impacting supply. By late 2020, mandatory primary frequency response helped return to a narrower frequency distribution, albeit with some deviations. The analysis underscores the impact of growing variable renewable energy in the NEM and the frequency shifts in extreme weather events. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Historical impact from 2011 to 2024 with future implications. Its root_cause is: Regulatory changes and the introduction of semi-dispatch caps affecting grid supply and frequency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 15-11-2024. Its affected region is: NEM Mainland (Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Regulatory and operational factors. Its causes are: Regulatory changes, semi-dispatch caps, increased variable renewable energy integration.

title: With YWPS1 and LYA2 back online, Victorian directions for System Strength cancelled
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "With YWPS1 and LYA2 back online, Victorian directions for System Strength cancelled" posted at 15-11-2024 10:42:00 AM is: On 14th November 2024, several Victorian coal units, including Loy Yang A2, were offline, leading AEMO to direct 4 units for System Strength. The unexpected outage of Loy Yang A2 was speculated to have triggered the direction owing to the decrease in operational coal units from 7 to 6 in Victoria. title: Snippets from AEMO’s summer readiness briefing for 2024-25
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Snippets from AEMO’s summer readiness briefing for 2024-25" posted at 14-11-2024 04:55:00 PM is: The AEMO's summer readiness briefing for the NEM 2024-25 highlights increased risk factors for high impact outages in Victoria due to maintenance and commissioning works, as well as planned outages of large generating units expected during November and December. This could impact generation availability in January. Extreme demand is anticipated, particularly referencing the record peak demand observed in Queensland in January 2024. To address supply reliability shortfalls, AEMO plans to procure interim reliability reserves for SA and NSW, and use short notice RERT in VIC. Gas Powered Generation will play a crucial role, but uncertainties due to reduced gas production and the need for gas storage drawdown might lead to higher gas prices. Weather forecasts suggest cooler average temperatures, but with risks of heatwaves in certain locations, and potentially increased tropical cyclone activity in QLD which could impact power lines. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Summer 2024-25. Its root cause is: planned outages and maintenance work, extreme demand potential, and weather impacts. Its accident scale is: regional. Its dates are: 14-11-2024. Its affected region is: VIC, SA, NSW, QLD (within the NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational and environmental factors. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Maintenance and commissioning works in VIC, planned outages of generating units, uncertainties in gas supply and prices, potential increase in demand, weather conditions influencing demand and infrastructure.

title: Why the directions (to 4 units) with 6 coal-fired units still online in Victoria?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why the directions (to 4 units) with 6 coal-fired units still online in Victoria?" posted at 14-11-2024 02:18:00 PM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) directive to bring four units online in Victoria to cover a System Strength shortfall after Loy Yang A2 joined three other offline coal units, reducing the total operational coal units from seven to six. This directive is linked to an outage on the Moorabool to Sydenham line and is supported by AEMO's 'Victorian Transfer Limits Advice – Outages' document. Such situations have occurred previously, indicating a system constraint issue. The V-MLSY_NOEMTT_R constraint, invoked on 13th November and expected to remain until 20th November, highlights the need for operational adjustments to maintain system stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: from 13th November 2024 (the constraint was invoked) to 20th November 2024 (expected end date of the constraint). Its root cause is: the outage of the Moorabool to Sydenham line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13th November 2024 to 20th November 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: operational and infrastructure failure. Its causes are: outage of Loy Yang A2 coal unit and the Moorabool to Sydenham line outage necessitating system adjustments to maintain grid stability.

title: AEMO directs 4 units on in Victoria, to cover System Strength shortfall
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO directs 4 units on in Victoria, to cover System Strength shortfall" posted at 14-11-2024 09:27:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued directions to activate four generating units in Victoria to address a system strength shortfall. This intervention was deemed necessary due to the offline status of three coal units in the region, namely Loy Yang A4, Yallourn unit 1, and Yallourn unit 3, which left the power system in a potentially insecure state. These outages are attributed to planned maintenance for Loy Yang A4 and Yallourn unit 1, and a tube leak outage for Yallourn unit 3. The activated units include Jeeralang B1 and B2, operated by EnergyAustralia Ecogen Pty Ltd, and Murray 2-2 and 2-3, operated by Snowy Hydro Limited, providing synchronous condenser services to maintain system security. These interventions are expected to last until 3:00 PM on 14/11/2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: until 3:00 PM on 14/11/2024. Its root_cause is: system strength shortfall due to multiple coal units offline. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-11-2024. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: NEM participants in Victoria. Its cause type is: generator outages. Its causes are: planned outage of Loy Yang A4, planned outage of Yallourn unit 1, tube leak outage of Yallourn unit 3.

Unit 3 was taken offline due to a leak, while Units 2 and 4 experienced trips, with Unit 2 returning to service the same day. title: Data Intensity of operating in the NEM, and implications for algorithmic bidding
author: Philippe Orhan
classified_content: The summary of the news "Data Intensity of operating in the NEM, and implications for algorithmic bidding" posted at 12-11-2024 12:56:00 PM is: The article discusses Neoen's experiences in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, highlighting the critical role of data and the challenges of operating a large renewable energy portfolio. Neoen's approach includes establishing a 24/7 Operations Control Centre and developing automated bidding algorithms to optimize operations in the highly transparent and real-time data-rich environment of the NEM. The speech, delivered by Philippe Orhan, also contrasts the NEM's data handling capabilities with the Western Energy Market (WEM) and emphasizes the balance needed between automated systems and human oversight for efficient decision-making. Key challenges include managing vast data sources, real-time decision-making, and avoiding analysis paralysis. The insights underline the complexities and opportunities presented by the NEM's unique data-driven environment.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its affected key users are: Renewable energy operators and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Structural adaptation to market dynamics and data utilization. Its causes are: Transition to renewable energy, the need for real-time data-driven optimization in energy markets, and the integration of automated bidding systems.

title: Evening volatility in QLD and NSW (and low IRPM) on Monday evening 11th November 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD and NSW (and low IRPM) on Monday evening 11th November 2024" posted at 11-11-2024 06:03:00 PM is: The article discusses electricity market volatility on the evening of November 11th, 2024, focusing on price spikes in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). At the 17:55 dispatch interval, Queensland's spot price rose to $4,000/MWh, while NSW experienced somewhat elevated prices, contrasting with low prices in other regions. The situation was compounded by constraints on the QNI and Directlink interconnectors, which forced them to export southwards despite price differentials. This led to a low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) of 9% in the Queensland 'Economic Island' due to increased demand and low available generation. The 18:00 dispatch interval continued to see high prices in both QLD and NSW; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Grid and generation constraints. Its causes are: Elevated spot prices, interconnector constraints, low IRPM, and increased demand.

An important factor was a network outage on the ‘Marulan – Yass (5) 330kV line,’ leading to the invocation of the constraint set, which results in transmission limitations and varied bidding behaviors. title: The effect of the ‘S_PPT+SNPT+BLVR_220’ Constraint Equation on Monday 23rd September 2024 (Part 4 of a Case Study)

This constraint, imposed due to a network outage, limited the combined output of specific generation units to 220 MW, creating a bottleneck that prevented gas-fired generation from fully dispatching even when prices were negative. The article explores how this affected the dispatch intervals throughout the day, resulting in price spikes and market complexities. Its relevance level is: Level 2. The market has not yet responded to these forecasts, but monitoring is ongoing as the date approaches.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability.
