Its dates are: 01-05-2024 (outage start), 15-05-2024 (initial return attempt), 16-05-2024 (snag discovered), potentially 21-05-2024 (expected resolution). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: NSW electricity consumers and dependent industries. Its cause type is: Technical failure during return-to-service operations. Its causes are: Boiler tube leak, startup technical issues.

title: Low wind conditions to persist, NEM-wide, for at least another week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low wind conditions to persist, NEM-wide, for at least another week" posted on 16-05-2024 03:19:00 PM is: The news discusses the continuation of low wind conditions across the National Electricity Market (NEM) for at least another week, as revealed by the live-updating dashboard of ez2view ‘Trends Engine’. With reference to a 21-day trend analysis, it highlights that May 2024 may be notable for having low wind yield, with the highest historical production point recently being around 5,500 MW and predictions indicating possible exceedance of this level by a small margin. However, peak wind production remains significantly lower than historical peaks, despite an increase in installed capacity. The article refers to previous contexts around wind production, noting a significant peak in July 2023 and a comparably low peak in April 2022. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: At least one week. Its root cause is: Low wind conditions persisting across NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 8th May 2024 to 16th May 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM)-wide. Its affected key users are: Energy suppliers and consumers dependent on wind-generated electricity. Its cause type is: Natural phenomena. Its causes are: Continued low wind conditions predicted by AEMO’s UIGF for Wind and historical data analysis indicating lower wind yield for the month compared to recent history.

title: News update in the AFR about Callide C
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "News update in the AFR about Callide C" posted at 16-05-2024 09:11:00 AM is: The article discusses a bid by CS Energy to acquire full ownership of the Callide C power plant, following the extensive outage due to the explosion and fire at Callide C4 in 2021. CS Energy is moving towards purchasing the half of Callide C they do not currently own. The delay in the public release of the Brady Report, which is expected to shed light on the root cause of the original incident, is a point of frustration for Queensland's Premier. The Callide C3 unit has recently returned to service after an extended outage due to cooling tower issues. Meanwhile, Callide C4 is expected to begin its return-to-service process at the end of June 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since May 2021, with expected developments in June 2024. Its root_cause is: Explosion and fire at Callide C4. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-05-2024; initial incident on 25th May 2021. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Explosion at Callide C4 leading to prolonged outage and operational delays.

title: ‘Another challenging week’ for VRE NEM-wide, beginning Wednesday 8th May 2024
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2024-05-08 NSW exceeds the CPT and enters Administered Pricing
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another challenging week for VRE NEM-wide, beginning Wednesday 8th May 2024" posted at 15-05-2024 05:03:00 PM is: The New South Wales electricity market experienced a week-long phase under Administered Pricing starting from May 8, 2024, due to significant price volatility on May 7 and May 8, 2024. The volatility was attributed to disappointing wind farm performance and resource constraints, especially in the evening on May 8, which triggered the cumulative price threshold. The period ended on May 15, 2024, exposing ongoing challenges for the integration of renewable energy (VRE). Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Affected the market from May 8, 2024, to May 15, 2024. Its root_cause is: Disappointing wind resource availability and constraints leading to spillage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: May 8, 2024, to May 15, 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in New South Wales. Its cause type is: Resource inadequacy and market mechanism activation. Its causes are: Prolonged volatility due to wind resource underperformance and constraints, spillage, and triggering of the cumulative price threshold leading to Administered Pricing.

title: So how much did Eraring Power Station supply, of the NSW electricity consumption, over 5 days to 11th May 2024?
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2024-05-08 NSW exceeds the CPT and enters Administered Pricing
classified_content: The summary of the news "So how much did Eraring Power Station supply, of the NSW electricity consumption, over 5 days to 11th May 2024?" posted at 15-05-2024 03:50:00 PM is: During the 5-day period from 6th to 9th May 2024, the Eraring Power Station faced operational challenges with two units offline due to unplanned outages. Despite these difficulties, Eraring managed to supply between 14% to 18% of NSW's electricity demand during peak volatility periods, aided by limited imports from Queensland and Victoria and constrained wind energy generation. The article reflects on how the expected closure of the Eraring station in August 2025 may present extreme challenges for future energy reliability in the region; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (5-day period). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outages of Eraring units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 6th to 9th May 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: NSW electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Operational failure. Its causes is: Unplanned outages of Eraring units, lack of energy import capacity from neighboring states, and limited wind energy due to transmission constraints.

title: Bruce Mountain’s commentary about Eraring (and the Energy Transition) is worth a read
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2024-05-08 NSW exceeds the CPT and enters Administered Pricing
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bruce Mountain’s commentary about Eraring (and the Energy Transition) is worth a read" posted at 15-05-2024 10:42:00 AM is: Bruce Mountain's opinion piece in The Australian discusses the challenges of the energy transition, with a focus on the closure of the Eraring power station and its implications. On 8th May 2024, NSW encountered Administered Pricing due to price spikes, attributed to reduced coal-fired generation and low solar output, which led to increased reliance on costly gas-fired generation. The piece highlights the complexity of the energy transition and calls for realistic planning and reform to avoid negative outcomes. The commentary also addresses the disconnect between optimistic plans and on-ground realities in energy transitions; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term. Its root_cause is: Reduced coal generation combined with low solar and wind output, leading to increased gas usage and high prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 15-05-2024. Its affected region is: NSW, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Multiple operational and planning challenges. Its causes is: Reduced capacity in coal-fired stations, network curtailment of wind, reliance on expensive gas generation due to low solar output.

title: Administered Pricing ends in NSW from 04:05 Wednesday 15th May 2024
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2024-05-08 NSW exceeds the CPT and enters Administered Pricing
classified_content: The summary of the news "Administered Pricing ends in NSW from 04:05 Wednesday 15th May 2024" posted at 15-05-2024 05:48:00 AM is: The administered pricing in New South Wales (NSW) concluded at 04:00 on May 15, 2024, as per the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) notice. This change was due to the Cumulative Price in NSW falling below the Cumulative Price Threshold over the preceding days, leading to normal pricing resumption. The market had been under Administered Pricing since May 8, 2024. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Decline in cumulative pricing below the set threshold. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-05-2024. Its affected region is: NSW, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Market condition change. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Will the rolling Cumulative Price for NSW fall back below the CPT on Tuesday evening 14th May 2024" posted at 14-05-2024 02:09:00 PM is: The Cumulative Price in New South Wales (NSW) is expected to drop below the Cumulative Price Threshold this evening, following a week of volatility that began on Tuesday, 7th May 2024, leading to Administered Pricing measures; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). title: Low wind harvest in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low wind harvest in South Australia" posted at 14-05-2024 09:43:00 AM is: The article discusses the recent low wind conditions in South Australia and the lack of wind curtailment impacting electricity generation. Tim Ryan inquired about curtailment, but data from NEMreview v7 confirms no noticeable curtailment in semi-scheduled wind farms recently, although there were periods earlier this month. Joel Gilmore's comments emphasize the need for flexible, firming technologies to supplement wind power, suggesting a shift from natural gas to more adaptable resources like batteries. A recent working paper argues for maintaining gas peaking capacity, highlighting principles for future energy systems valuing dual-fuel capabilities, minimal operation, but readiness for critical demand events. Market structures must ensure these plants are maintained and fueled efficiently. It also reflects on the potential redefinition of the Electricity Statement of Opportunities to better identify commercial potential for variable renewable energy and firming. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (possibly several days). Its root_cause is: Low wind generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-05-2024. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Natural phenomenon. Its causes are: Low wind speed, necessity of firming capacity, potential market arrangements for peaking plants.

title: Spot price volatility in May 2024 in NSW flows through to ‘baseload’ futures prices
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in May 2024 in NSW flows through to ‘baseload’ futures prices" posted at 14-05-2024 08:45:00 AM is: The article discusses the significant volatility in electricity spot prices in New South Wales (NSW) on May 7th and 8th, 2024, which led to substantial increases in the baseload futures contract prices for Q2 2024 and subsequent quarters. Rumors regarding the possible extension of Eraring Power Station were mentioned as a contributing factor to the price changes. Traders' sentiment has been influenced, leading them to adjust their expectations for future quarters, as evidenced by the trends in contract prices. The implications of this volatility might eventually affect retail contract prices; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: May 2024 and future quarters. Its root_cause is: Spot price volatility and rumors of Eraring Power Station extension. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: May 2nd to 9th, 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Traders and potentially retail consumers. Its cause type is: Market sentiment and price movements. Its causes are: Spot price volatility, rumors of Eraring Power Station extension, changes in trader sentiment.

title: Delving deeper, into gas-fired peaker performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper, into gas-fired peaker performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May" posted at 10-05-2024 07:46:00 PM is: This article explores the bidding and performance of gas-fired peaker stations in New South Wales during periods of extreme market volatility from the evening of Tuesday, May 7th, to Thursday, May 9th, 2024. The focus is on four gas-fired stations, emphasizing their strategic bids for dispatch during peak demand times and volatility, especially on Wednesday evening, where bids ranged from -$1,000 to over $10,000/MWh, affecting dispatch levels and market dynamics. Distinct patterns at each station, including Colongra, Smithfield, Tallawarra, and Uranquinty, are highlighted, with special notes on constraints affecting certain stations like Uranquinty. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (Primarily from May 7th to May 9th, 2024). Its root_cause is: Market dynamics and volatility in bidding patterns and dispatch of gas-fired peaker stations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-05-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and operators in NSW. Its cause type is: Market mechanics and bidding strategies. Its causes are: Strategic bidding during peak times and market volatility, constraint equations affecting dispatch.

title: How severe thunderstorms impact energy infrastructure
author: Ashleigh Madden
topic: beginning 2024-02-13, with Victoria transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "How severe thunderstorms impact energy infrastructure" posted at 10-05-2024 04:48:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of severe thunderstorms and winds equivalent to a category two cyclone on Victoria's energy infrastructure in mid-February 2024. These extreme weather events, driven by a cold front and localized microbursts, damaged transmission towers and left thousands without power. The piece explores whether thunderstorms are becoming more severe in Victoria, noting the potential influence of climate change on storms, which may increase the risk of extreme winds due to more moist and unstable air. The summary also highlights that while lightning detection is a new phenomenon, recent data suggests a higher number of thunder days than average in several regions, indicating potentially worsening patterns. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Beginning 2024-02-13. Its root_cause is: Severe thunderstorms and microbursts. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-05-2024 04:48:00 PM. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Thunderstorms, strong wind gusts, localized microbursts.

title: Delving deeper, into battery (discharge) performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper, into battery (discharge) performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May," posted at 10-05-2024 03:55:00 PM is: The article provides an in-depth analysis of battery discharge performance in New South Wales (NSW) during May 7th and 8th, 2024. It highlights a study observing whether battery units—integral components of the energy supply chain—were depleted during highly volatile periods within a 30-day review, confirming suspicions of discharge during key times. The focus on seven operational batteries showcases ongoing concerns with energy reliability in volatile conditions; its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Less Relevant. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, largely covering the two days analyzed, but it may highlight ongoing reliability issues. Its root_cause is: Battery discharge during high volatility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: May 7th-8th, 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Performance issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High volatility leading to suspicion of battery discharge.

title: Delving deeper, into Wind Farm performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper, into Wind Farm performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May" posted at 10-05-2024 03:20:00 PM is: The article examines the performance of wind farms in New South Wales (NSW) during volatile market conditions on 7th and 8th May 2024, highlighting that wind generation was constrained significantly due to lower-than-ideal wind conditions and transmission constraints. This led to substantial energy 'spilled,' particularly during severe constraints on 8th May. Various wind farms experienced different levels of constraint and volatility impacts; some were highly constrained, while others showed fluctuations for unexplored reasons.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term, affecting specific periods on 7th and 8th May 2024. Its root_cause is: Lower-than-ideal wind conditions and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-05-2024, 7th and 8th May 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental and systemic. Its causes are: Lower-than-ideal wind conditions led to reduced wind harvest; transmission constraints resulted in energy spillages; some wind farm units were severely constrained.

title: Delving deeper, into coal generator performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Delving deeper into coal generator performance in NSW on Tue 7th May and Wed 8th May" posted at 10-05-2024 12:22:00 PM is: The article analyzes coal generator performance in New South Wales (NSW) during volatile periods from 7th to 8th May 2024, focusing on coal-fired stations' bidding strategies under the 'NSW under Administered Pricing' regime. Significant energy bids above $10,000/MWh were recorded, particularly on Wednesday evening. It details individual station performances: Bayswater operated four units with numerous rebids citing commercial reasons, Eraring experienced unplanned outages, keeping two units offline with low bid prices during volatility, Mt Piper maintained operational capacity during volatile periods with some bids above $10,000/MWh, and Vales Point faced a unit outage affecting its load capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: The impact mainly spans from 7th to 8th May 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and bidding strategies. Its causes are: Unplanned outages at Eraring and Vales Point, high energy bids, and adjustments due to commercial and operational considerations.

title: Thar she blows!   NSW hits the CPT and enters Administered Pricing (until Wed 15th May at least) … I think for only the 2nd time ever?
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2024-05-08 NSW exceeds the CPT and enters Administered Pricing
classified_content: The summary of the news "Thar she blows! NSW hits the CPT and enters Administered Pricing (until Wed 15th May at least) … I think for only the 2nd time ever?" posted at 08-05-2024 09:33:00 PM is: The New South Wales (NSW) region of Australia has entered an Administered Pricing period for both ENERGY and ancillary services due to surpassing the Cumulative Price Threshold (CPT) of $1,490,200.00, as declared by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO). This decision followed a period of significant spot price volatility, with prices often exceeding $1,000/MWh, and marks only the second instance in 25 years where Administered Pricing has occurred in NSW, the first being in June 2022 during the energy crisis. Contributing factors include low electricity demand, network import limitations, maintenance on supply-side assets, and several coal units being offline due to unplanned outages. Administered Pricing is expected to remain in effect until May 15th, 2024, with daily assessments. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until at least May 15th, 2024. Its root_cause is: Exceedance of the Cumulative Price Threshold due to prolonged spot price volatility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-05-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: Market and operational issues. Its causes are: High spot price volatility, network import constraints, low renewable energy generation, and unplanned outages of coal units.

Responses from entities like Delta Electricity and former NSW Treasurer Matt Kean emphasize the complexities of managing coal closures. Given these developments, more information is anticipated on the potential extension.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early reflections on the concerns of large energy users, from the 2024 EUAA National Conference" posted at 08-05-2024 08:56:00 AM is: The article provides reflections from the 2024 EUAA National Conference in Melbourne, highlighting the concerns of large energy users regarding rising energy costs amidst the transition to firmed renewables. Despite modeling suggesting that firmed renewables are cost-effective, energy consumers express unease about increasing costs compared to previous decades. Additionally, the difficulties for large energy users, such as aluminum smelters, in securing suitable power purchase agreements amid the shift to renewable energy are discussed. Potential optimism is noted with references to new "baseload renewable" contracts associated with big battery projects, although details and feasibility remain uncertain. Conversations during the conference also touched on the critical role of large energy users within the electricity system and potential support mechanisms, such as a Capacity Retention Scheme.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Additionally, there was a planned outage affecting the Dapto to Marulan line. The issues are under review for deeper analysis. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Regulation FCAS Costs in 2023
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Regulation FCAS Costs in 2023" posted at 03-05-2024 03:59:00 PM is: The article discusses the Regulation Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) costs for wind and solar units in the National Electricity Market (NEM) for 2023. Regulation FCAS covers costs incurred to correct grid frequency imbalance between supply and demand. The analysis shows that larger units generally incur greater Regulation FCAS costs, with costs varying by fuel type and dispatch adherence. Notably, a small number of wind and solar units offset these costs by participating in FCAS markets. The implementation of self-forecasting has generally improved Regulation costs, with fewer units engaging in this activity seeing higher costs. The findings illustrate potential cost management strategies for renewable energy sources in maintaining grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: During the year 2023. Its root cause is: Imbalance between supply and demand causing grid frequency deviations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-05-2024. Its cause type is: Variability in renewable energy output and dispatch instruction adherence. Its causes are: Larger unit size, variability in solar and wind energy output, and inconsistent adherence to dispatch instructions.

title: Keeping up with the curtailment Part 2: The what and the where
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Keeping up with the curtailment Part 2: The what and the where" posted at 02-05-2024 01:39:00 AM is: The article delves into the curtailment of semi-scheduled solar and wind farms within the National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting two primary factors—network and economic curtailment. Network curtailment, referred to as 'forced curtailment,' occurred due to network constraints, notably affecting solar farms in New South Wales and Victoria. Economic curtailment, or 'self-curtailment,' happened when market conditions made generation uneconomical. Data from GSD2023 shows significant curtailment, with solar farms facing greater curtailment challenges compared to wind, accentuated by seasonal variations. The analysis underscores the complexities of curtailment as a function of supply, demand, and network dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its cause type is: Market and Network Constraint. Its causes are: Network constraints, economic decisions based on market prices, seasonal variations influencing supply and demand.

Its dates are: The main analysis period concluded on 31st December 2023. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy suppliers and market participants. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and external influences. title: Simshauser/Gilmore Working Paper speaks to challenges for gas-fired firming
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Simshauser/Gilmore Working Paper speaks to challenges for gas-fired firming" posted at 29-04-2024 08:35:00 AM is: The working paper by Paul Simshauser and Joel Gilmore, highlighted in a recent article, addresses the challenges posed by the reliance on gas turbines for firming in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), as the country transitions to a low-emission energy system dominated by variable renewable energy (VRE). The paper critiques current electricity market models that assume a perpetually flexible gas market, which may not hold true given the forecasts of significant spikes in gas turbine output during low renewable periods, especially in winter. Additionally, the paper suggests that without the development of new firming assets, such as pumped hydro, the NEM might face shortfalls in gas supply during critical demand days, potentially impacting the system's reliability. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as these issues are projected in the context of future energy system configurations. Its root_cause is: The need to align electricity market models with realistic gas market capabilities amidst the transition to a VRE-dominated system. Its accident scale is: Unknown as it pertains to projected scenarios rather than current events. Its dates is: 29-04-2024 08:35:00 AM. Its affected region is: Eastern Australia, particularly New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Large Energy Users and potentially energy market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Systemic issues in energy market modelling and infrastructure planning. Its causes is: Inadequate firming capacity development, reliance on gas-fired firming during low-renewable periods, and insufficient alignment between gas market capacity and electricity system needs.

title: Brief Case Study of Sunday 4th February 2024, with low IRPM following high Market Demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief Case Study of Sunday 4th February 2024, with low IRPM following high Market Demand" posted at 27-04-2024 01:24:00 PM is: The news discusses a situation of high market demand for electricity on February 4, 2024, which led to a low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) of 17.7% in Australia. The drop in IRPM occurred due to the demand outpacing the supply from large-scale Variable Renewable Energy (VRE) sources as detailed in the 2024 Q1 GenInsights Quarterly Updates. The focus is on understanding these dynamics using NEMreview v7, covering February 3-5, 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (over several days in early February 2024). Its root_cause is: High market demand and insufficient supply from large-scale VRE sources. Its accident scale is: System-wide (NEM-wide). Its dates are: February 3 to February 5, 2024. Its affected region is: NEM (National Electricity Market, likely encompassing regions in Australia). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market operators. Its cause type is: Supply-demand imbalance. Its causes are: High demand on February 4, 2024, coupled with a decrease in VRE supply, leading to low IRPM.

title: More unplanned outages than would be desired, at YWPS3, since the Planned Major Outage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More unplanned outages than would be desired, at YWPS3, since the Planned Major Outage" posted at 27-04-2024 11:27:00 AM is: The article discusses a series of unplanned outages at the Yallourn Power Station, specifically affecting Unit 3 (YWPS3), which have occurred shortly after a Planned Major Outage was conducted to improve performance. Following an analysis of recent data, the unit experienced four unplanned outages in a short period, raising concerns about the effectiveness of the maintenance efforts. The article notes that an evaluation of the outages' impacts is ongoing. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate through to ongoing analysis. Its root cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 27-04-2024 11:27:00 AM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical Performance Issues. Its causes is: Multiple unplanned outages, possible inefficiencies post maintenance.

title: Yallourn unit 1 comes offline for the ‘performance improvement’ planned outage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yallourn unit 1 comes offline for the ‘performance improvement’ planned outage" posted at 26-04-2024 01:24:00 PM is: This article reports on the planned outage of Yallourn unit 1, which commenced overnight, as part of a strategy aimed at performance improvement following poor coal unit performance during the 2022 Energy Crisis. The outage, initially scheduled to begin on 15th March 2024, was delayed and is now underway, expected to last until 2nd July 2024, making it a 68-day outage. The focus of these planned outages is to enhance performance post-crisis, which EnergyAustralia has initiated due to a significant percentage of unavailable coal units in 2022. Additionally, there are changes in the planned outage schedules for Yallourn unit 4, set to start on 11th November 2024, extending for 63 days, with a shorter outage in August 2024. These changes will be further discussed in the GenInsights Quarterly Update series.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 68 days starting from 26th April 2024 to 2nd July 2024. Its root cause is: scheduled performance improvement outage due to poor previous performance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26th April 2024 (main date), 2nd July 2024 (expected RTS date for Yallourn unit 1), 11th November 2024 - 13th January 2025 (YWPS4 planned outage). Its affected region is: Yallourn, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Planned operational activities. Its causes are: poor coal unit performance during 2022, scheduled maintenance and performance improvement.

title: Murraylink back in service, from Friday 19th April 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Murraylink back in service, from Friday 19th April 2024" posted at 24-04-2024 08:58:00 AM is: The article discusses the resumption of service for the Murraylink electricity interconnector, which was out of service from March 27, 2024, until it came back online on April 19, 2024. title: NEM-wide wind generation above 6,000MW for the first time in ~3 weeks
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide wind generation above 6,000MW for the first time in ~3 weeks" posted at 24-04-2024 08:28:00 AM is: The article discusses the recent increase in wind generation across the National Electricity Market (NEM) in southeastern Australia, surpassing 6,000MW for the first time in approximately three weeks, following a period of reduced wind activity. Weather intelligence services had predicted this shift, noting the return of stronger winds. This development is significant in breaking the lengthy wind lull experienced in the region and will be a key point of analysis in upcoming GenInsights Quarterly Updates for April 2024.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market: Immediate to ongoing into Q2 2024. Its root cause is: Return of wind patterns. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 24-04-2024. Its affected region is: Southeastern Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Natural phenomena. Its causes are: Increased wind activity, breaking wind lull.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Presentation to BofA Securities audience about Risk, Uncertainty and Volatility in the energy transition" posted at 23-04-2024 04:41:00 PM is: The article discusses a presentation given to the Bank of America Securities audience on the risks, uncertainties, and volatility in the energy transition, coinciding with the release of the Australian Energy Market Operator's QED for the first quarter of 2024. While the detailed analysis of 2024 Q1 is still pending, the presentation builds on previous reports from Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting, highlighting the ongoing complexities of the energy market. title: AEMO’s Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QEC) for 2024 Q1 … second in the triptych
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO’s Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QEC) for 2024 Q1 … second in the triptych" posted at 23-04-2024 01:54:00 PM is: The AEMO's Quarterly Energy Dynamics report for the first quarter of 2024 highlights a significant increase in renewable energy generation, which grew to 39% of total generation, up from 37.4% in Q1 2023. Despite extreme weather and heatwave conditions causing increased electricity demand, renewable energy has led to an 8% decrease in wholesale electricity prices on Australia's east coast compared to Q1 2023, with prices settling at $76 per megawatt hour. This trend underscores the pivotal role of renewables in driving down electricity costs, even amid volatile demand conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: First quarter of 2024. Its root cause is: Increased renewable energy generation amid high temperatures and volatile weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-04-2024. Its affected region is: Australia's east coast. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: High temperatures increasing demand, growing renewable energy penetration driving prices down, extreme weather events causing price volatility.

title: A precis of the ‘Exploring Wind Diversity’ analysis completed in Appendix 27 of GenInsights21
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A precis of the ‘Exploring Wind Diversity’ analysis completed in Appendix 27 of GenInsights21" posted at 21-04-2024 04:49:00 PM is: The article reviews the analysis presented in Appendix 27 of GenInsights21, focusing on wind diversity and its impact on energy reliability. It highlights the findings regarding the variability of wind speeds across Renewable Energy Zones (REZ) in Australia and discusses the implications for system reliability due to wind production inconsistencies. The study also constructed a hypothetical grid reliant solely on wind farms and storage to understand the phenomenon of 'wind droughts' and the required size of energy storage. Although results showed some significant wind droughts, the analysis underscored the necessity of multi-decade historical analyses for credibility. It also stressed the large storage volume needed for a grid composed solely of wind and highlighted the utility of incorporating other renewable energy sources and storage solutions, like gas, to reduce reliance on wind capacity alone. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term, considering potential policy responses and future energy grid structures. Its root cause is: Variability in wind speeds impacting energy reliability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same as the news date, 21-04-2024. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market analysts, renewable energy planners, and policy makers. Its cause type is: Natural variability. Its causes are: Inconsistent wind speeds leading to periods of low wind energy production, necessitating exploration of diverse energy storage and generation options.

title: We can’t say yet if grid-breaking thunderstorms are getting worse – but we shouldn’t wait to find out
author: University of Melbourne Researchers
topic: beginning 2024-02-13, with Victoria transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "We can’t say yet if grid-breaking thunderstorms are getting worse – but we shouldn’t wait to find out" posted at 18-04-2024 04:33:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of extreme winds from thunderstorms, which destroyed six transmission towers in Victoria on February 13, leading to widespread power outages affecting about 500,000 people. The analysis highlights the difficulty of assessing whether climate change is contributing to the increasing frequency or severity of such thunderstorms due to the complexity of the small-scale processes involved. While there is evidence that climate change might be increasing conditions favorable to thunderstorms, especially in inland Australia, further research and modeling are required. The authors emphasize the importance of improving grid resilience by considering risks from such extreme weather events in designing energy systems, suggesting methods like burying powerlines and integrating smart grid technologies. The discussion underscores the necessity for government-led reforms to ensure the energy grid can withstand future challenges posed by extreme weather and climate change. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to medium-term, given the destruction of infrastructure and subsequent prolonged outages. Its root_cause is: Extreme wind gusts from thunderstorms. Its accident scale is: Approximately 500,000 people affected with outages. Its dates are: 13-02-2024. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residents and businesses relying on the grid. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Thunderstorm-induced extreme winds resulting in structural damage to transmission towers and subsequent power outages.

title: Generation gone with the wind
author: Ashleigh Madden
classified_content: The summary of the news "Generation gone with the wind" posted at 18-04-2024 01:57:00 PM is: A prolonged period of low wind power generation in the National Electricity Market (NEM) of southern Australia ended abruptly on Wednesday evening, April 17, as stronger winds returned with the onset of a weak cold front, causing wind power to peak above 3,000 MW. This weeklong lull, attributed to blocking high-pressure systems and a positive Southern Annular Mode, resulted in wind providing approximately 6% of the NEM’s electricity, significantly below the previous year's average of 13.1%. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: April 10 to April 17, 2024. Its root_cause is: Blocking high-pressure systems over southern Australia and a positive Southern Annular Mode. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: April 10 to April 17, 2024. Its affected region is: Southern Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-induced. Its causes are: Blocking high-pressure systems, a positive Southern Annular Mode, and subsequent disruption of typical wind patterns.

title: AER Wholesale Markets Quarterly published for 2024 Q1 … first in the triptych
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER Wholesale Markets Quarterly published for 2024 Q1 … first in the triptych" posted at 18-04-2024 01:48:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator's (AER) Wholesale Markets Quarterly report for the first quarter of 2024 highlights that extreme summer weather conditions contributed to increased wholesale electricity prices, particularly in Queensland and Victoria. The summer of 2023-24 was noted as Australia's third warmest, leading to Queensland setting new daily demand records multiple times due to the heat. Conversely, reduced demand in Victoria and South Australia at certain times was attributed to high solar generation on mild days. Overall, Queensland and Victoria experienced a rise in average electricity prices by 20% and 9% respectively, compared to the same period in 2023. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q1 2024. Its root_cause is: Extreme summer heat causing high electricity demand and price increases. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-04-2024 01:48:00 PM. Its affected region is: Queensland and Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland and Victoria. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Third warmest summer on record, increased electricity demand during heatwaves, and reduced demand due to solar generation on mild days.

title: Summer reflections
author: Carl Daley
topic: 2024-01-22 QLD (massively higher!) all time max demand
classified_content: The summary of the news "Summer reflections" posted at 18-04-2024 12:57:00 PM is: The article reviews the electricity market dynamics during the summer of 2024, highlighting volatile spot prices, market price fluctuations, and the emergence of the 'twin peaks' phenomena, driven by solar generation, which results in peak demand and price not aligning. Notably, Queensland experienced maximum demand records, while Victoria faced a storm that caused transmission tower failures, leading to load shedding and price spikes. Additionally, the article discusses the impacts of weather forecasts, such as how the predictions affected agricultural decisions related to livestock. The article also details the return-to-service of Callide C’s Unit 3 and its implications on electricity prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Summer 2024. Its root cause is: Weather events and solar generation dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13 February 2024 and 22 January 2024, among others. Its affected region is: Victoria and Queensland. Its affected key users are: 500,000 customers load shed in Victoria. Its cause type is: Various energy market dynamics and weather-related events. Its causes are: Extreme weather events, solar generation impact, battery storage challenges, and delayed return-to-service of Callide C Unit 3.

title: The wind is back … some days earlier than suggested in AEMO’s older forecasts
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The wind is back … some days earlier than suggested in AEMO’s older forecasts" posted at 18-04-2024 09:47:00 AM is: The article discusses unexpected early wind production in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on April 17, 2024, which surpassed earlier Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) forecasts by several days. The peak production reached above 3,000 MW, and forecasts suggest potential yields of 4,000 MW in the coming days, though these outputs could be affected by curtailment. This unexpected surge in wind generation was primarily driven by meteorological conditions, such as an approaching cold front initially affecting Tasmania and Victoria. The article highlights the variability and challenges in forecasting wind production outcomes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term (impact felt from April 17, 2024 onwards). Its root_cause is: Changing meteorological conditions affecting wind generation predictions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-04-2024, main observation date; 18-04-2024, report date. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, primarily Tasmania and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Energy providers and consumers reliant on wind energy in the NEM. Its cause type is: Meteorological variability. Its causes are: Changing weather conditions, including a cold front and forecast inconsistencies.

title: Brief Case Study of low aggregate VRE (Wind + Large Solar) on Saturday 13th April 2024 … Part 1?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief Case Study of low aggregate VRE (Wind + Large Solar) on Saturday 13th April 2024 … Part 1?" posted at 17-04-2024 04:10:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant low in aggregate variable renewable energy (VRE) production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on April 13, 2024, particularly highlighting wind and large solar generation, which reached the lowest point of 783MW at 17:55. This low production led to discussions about wind farm performance and AEMO forecasts predicting a quicker recovery in wind generation. An analysis of individual regions showed that none had high performance, suggesting a widespread lull in wind activity. The situation was monitored to evaluate the peak firming requirements for potential insights in future reports. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, spanning approximately two weeks beginning prior to April 13, 2024. Its root_cause is: Low wind and solar generation across NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-04-2024 and discussed through 17-04-2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, encompassing regions like QLD, NSW, VIC, TAS, and SA. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Generation shortfall. Its causes are: Low aggregate wind and solar output, forecasted low wind contributions, and regional underperformance in renewable energy generation.

title: Has the wind lull of early 2024 Q2 spooked the futures market?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Has the wind lull of early 2024 Q2 spooked the futures market?" posted at 17-04-2024 10:47:00 AM is: The news discusses a notable lull in wind production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) starting from April 15th, 2024, which is expected to continue for another week. This wind lull is causing increased spot prices and influencing 2024 Q2 baseload futures prices. This development has generated discussions on various platforms about the potential pricing outcomes. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Early Q2 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy traders and market participants. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Prolonged period of low wind production.

title: Updated long-range trend of aggregate wind production statistics
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updated long-range trend of aggregate wind production statistics" posted at 16-04-2024 09:59:00 AM is: The article provides an updated analysis of long-term wind production statistics in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the peak production of 8,132MW on 7th July 2023 and discusses average monthly capacity factors oscillating between 25% and 35%. It also notes low points in production, particularly due to curtailment and highlights the historical wind drought of June 2017 and potential significant events in Q2 of 2024. New wind farms contribute to the increasing aggregate production, though output remains seasonally affected.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its cause type is: Fluctuations in wind energy production. Its causes are: Variations in wind energy capacity factors, curtailment, seasonal effects, and the introduction of new wind farms.

title: NEM-wide wind lull stretches extends to a full week (Tue 16th April 2024), and has another week to go!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide wind lull stretches extends to a full week (Tue 16th April 2024), and has another week to go!" posted at 16-04-2024 09:51:00 AM is: The article discusses a prolonged period of low wind production across the National Electricity Market (NEM), with aggregate wind production falling below 10.5% of grid demand for nearly a full week. Forecasts suggest this lull will continue for another week, affecting energy supply dynamics. This period of reduced wind power was anticipated, offering time for grid operators to manage energy reserves accordingly. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Approximately two weeks. Its root_cause is: Prolonged low wind production across the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-04-2024 to 16-04-2024 (extending another week). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). title: Tasmania’s grid frequency on Friday 12 April 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tasmania’s grid frequency on Friday 12 April 2024" posted at 15-04-2024 09:14:00 AM is: On Friday, 12th April 2024, a sudden trip of 550MW industrial load in Tasmania caused a significant disturbance to the grid frequency, which witnessed an immediate spike from 50.019 Hz to 51.233 Hz. A gradual return to the normal frequency of 50 Hz was achieved approximately 8 minutes later. The incident primarily affected Tasmania's power system, and further analysis is expected. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but short-term impact on 12th April 2024. Its root_cause is: Loss of industrial load. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 12-04-2024. Its affected region is: Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Industrial and possibly residential electricity users in Tasmania. Its cause type is: Electrical grid disruption. Its causes are: Sudden loss of industrial load, resulting in grid frequency disturbance.

title: Low wind production on Monday morning 15th April 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low wind production on Monday morning 15th April 2024" posted at 15-04-2024 07:39:00 AM is: The article discusses a significant dip in wind energy production recorded on the morning of 15th April 2024, with wind output in South Australia and Victoria falling below 50MW and 100MW respectively. This drop led to elevated electricity prices in these regions due to inadequate wind-generated power and insufficient solar input, as autumn solar capabilities had not yet increased; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term. Its root_cause is: low wind energy production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. title: The Australian notes how Callide C was back in court again this week
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Australian notes how Callide C was back in court again this week" posted at 13-04-2024 09:50:00 AM is: The article discusses the continued legal proceedings regarding the Callide C4 unit explosion, fire, and outage that originally occurred on May 25, 2021. It highlights the ongoing secrecy and updates related to the disaster, particularly focusing on the Federal Court processes and the status of Callide C3 and C4 units, with C4 still offline. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. title: Trip of 550MW of industrial load in Tasmania on Friday 12th April 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trip of 550MW of industrial load in Tasmania on Friday 12th April 2024" posted at 13-04-2024 09:28:00 AM is: A significant electric power system event occurred in Tasmania on April 12, 2024, when a 550MW industrial load tripped, affecting at least three major energy users. The event was identified as a non-credible contingency due to the Sheffield – Farrell No.2 220 kV line tripping during restoration at 10:48 AM. The incident did not require load shedding, and the cause was unknown initially, but later resolved when the line returned to service by 13:04 PM. AEMO concluded that a recurrence was unlikely under current circumstances and chose not to reclassify the event as credible. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate with potential short-term effects. Its root_cause is: Line fault during restoration. Its accident scale is: Affected 550MW of industrial load. Its dates are: 12-04-2024. Its affected region is: Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Major industrial entities in Tasmania. Its cause type is: Technical fault. Its causes are: Tripping of Sheffield – Farrell No.2 220 kV line, disconnection of significant industrial load.

title: An update on Callide C3 (back online) and Callide C4 (still offline)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "An update on Callide C3 (back online) and Callide C4 (still offline)" posted at 11-04-2024 07:10:00 PM is: The article provides an update on the status of two coal-power generation units, Callide C3 and C4. Callide C3, which had been offline since 31st October 2022 due to a cooling tower failure, came back online on 1st April 2024. Its output is progressively increasing, though not yet at full capacity due to additional system factors like rooftop PV impacts. Meanwhile, Callide C4 remains offline following a turbo-generator explosion on 25th May 2021, with an expected return to service on 30th June 2024 at part-load, and full availability expected a month later. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing as Callide C3 just recently returned and Callide C4 is expected to return by July 2024. Its root cause is: Callide C3 had a cooling tower failure and Callide C4 had a turbo-generator explosion. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Callide C3 - Offline since 31st October 2022, returned 1st April 2024; Callide C4 - Offline since 25th May 2021, expected return June 30th 2024 at part-load. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Mechanical failure. Its causes are: Cooling tower failure for Callide C3 and turbo-generator explosion for Callide C4.

title: Network outage (currently underway) on Murraylink DC interconnector
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Network outage (currently underway) on Murraylink DC interconnector" posted at 09-04-2024 02:05:00 PM is: The article discusses an ongoing network outage on the Murraylink DC interconnector that started during the 10:10 dispatch interval on 27th March 2024 and is expected to continue until late afternoon on 20th April 2024, affecting electricity flow between Victoria and South Australia, regions of net energy export and import respectively. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 27th March 2024 to 20th April 2024. Its root_cause is: Network outage on Murraylink interconnector. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27th March 2024 to 20th April 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and consumers in Victoria and South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical constraint. Its causes are: Network outage on Murraylink DC interconnector; forecasted constraints due to the 'I-MURRAYLINK' constraint set.

title: Increase in NSW to QLD transfer limit on QNI to 850MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Increase in NSW to QLD transfer limit on QNI to 850MW" posted at 09-04-2024 12:58:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced an increase in the Queensland-New South Wales Interconnector (QNI) transfer limit from 800 MW to 850 MW, effective 10th April 2024. This follows a series of tests and reviews conducted with TransGrid and Powerlink. Historical flow data reveals that, over the past decade, daily average flows were predominantly from Queensland to New South Wales, except during the summer of 2023-24 when demand increased in Queensland due to high temperatures and generator outages. These changes indicate improved interconnectivity between the regions, which can positively influence electricity distribution and pricing. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Testing and operational review of the QNI limits. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15 February 2024 (testing) and 10 April 2024 (implementation). Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Power consumers in New South Wales and Queensland. Its cause type is: Operational/system update. Its causes are: Increased demand in Queensland during summer, generator outages, and updated interconnector limits.

title: Optimising a highly renewable NEM from scratch, Part 2
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "Optimising a highly renewable NEM from scratch, Part 2" posted at 08-04-2024 04:45:00 PM is: The article outlines ITK's comprehensive analysis in building a highly renewable National Electricity Market (NEM) aimed at meeting the demand in 2025 by focusing on variable renewable energy (VRE) portfolios. This study incorporates transmission considerations to optimize cost while maintaining a high Sharpe ratio, indicating portfolio stability. The findings emphasize the significance of solar and wind energy, with a greater reliance on renewable energy located in remote areas to reduce emissions and firming costs over a long-term horizon. The optimal energy generation mix includes expanded interconnections, reduced reliance on gas, and enhanced system stability, potentially leading to competitive electricity prices in the $60-$70/MWh range. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to Long term (up to 25 years). Its root_cause is: Integration of renewable energy sources aiming for decarbonization and cost-efficiency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-04-2024 04:45:00 PM. Its affected region is: NEM territories including NSW, Queensland, Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market stakeholders within the NEM. Its cause type is: Strategic energy planning and policy development. Its causes is: Development of a cost-effective renewable energy grid focusing on long-term stability and emission reduction.

title: What was the cause of the coincident trip of Eraring units 1 and 2?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What was the cause of the coincident trip of Eraring units 1 and 2?" posted at "08-04-2024 11:22:00 AM" is: The simultaneous trip of Eraring Units 1 and 2 on April 3, 2024, led to a drop in the mainland frequency in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Although the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) identified the cause as a non-credible contingency event, it affirmed that a recurrence under the current circumstances is unlikely. The specific cause was later identified as faulty software, as stated by Origin. Both units returned to service the same day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: April 3, 2024. Its root_cause is: faulty software. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-04-2024 11:22:00 AM. Its affected region is: mainland NEM. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: technical fault. Its causes is: faulty software causing unit trips.

title: Supply-side responses following the trip of two Eraring units on Wednesday 3 April 2024
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Supply-side responses following the trip of two Eraring units on Wednesday 3 April 2024" posted at 05-04-2024 08:59:00 AM is: The energy market responded to the unexpected outage of Eraring Units 1 and 2 on 3 April 2024, which led to a significant supply disruption with a drop of about 1000 MW in the 13:25 dispatch interval. Various fuel types, including gas, coal, and hydro units, adjusted their outputs to stabilize the market. Gas units like Uranquinty rebid to increase output, with Uranquinty 11 starting ramp up from the 13:40 interval and other units contributing gradually. Coal units such as Vales Point rebid to double upward rates of change, and Eraring Unit 3 remained operational, compensating for the trip. Hydro units showed minimal additional contribution owing to pricing, and battery storage units saw limited dispatch. By the morning of 4 April, Eraring Units 1 and 2 returned to regular service without complications. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate to short-term. The root_cause is: turbine/generator trip at Eraring power station. Its accident scale is: localized. Its dates are: 3 April 2024 to 5 April 2024. The affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. The affected key users are: electricity consumers and market participants in New South Wales. Its cause type is: technical failure. Its causes are: trip of Eraring Units 1 and 2, rebidding and adjustments by other fuel types, insufficient pricing conditions for hydro and battery storage contributions.

title: Eraring Units Back
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Eraring Units Back" posted at 05-04-2024 08:01:00 AM is: On April 3, 2024, two units at the Eraring Power Station in New South Wales, ER01 and ER02, unexpectedly tripped but were subsequently returned to service. ER02 was reactivated at 20:35 with a starting level of 6 MW, gradually increasing to 210 MW, while ER01 was reactivated at 23:10 with a starting level of 7 MW. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced the return to service and deemed the event a non-credible contingency, reassuring that a repeat under current conditions is unlikely.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term due to quick restoration of service. Its root_cause is: Turbine/generator failures at Eraring Power Station. Its accident scale is: Local. Its dates are: April 3, 2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Unexpected tripping of ER01 and ER02 units at the Eraring Power Station.

title: Fast frequency recovery, on Wednesday 3rd April 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Fast frequency recovery, on Wednesday 3rd April 2024" posted at 03-04-2024 04:18:00 PM is: The article discusses the coincident trip of ER01 and ER02 generators which affected the frequency on the National Electricity Market (NEM) on April 3, 2024. These generators experienced a trip at approximately 13:22 NEM time, and the system was able to recover to a frequency of around 50Hz within a 7-8 minute timeframe by 13:30. This swift recovery showcases the robustness of the frequency management system in place.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term during the event and immediate recovery period. Its root_cause is: Generator trips (ER01 and ER02). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-04-2024. Its affected region is: Mainland of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: System failure. Its causes are: Coincident generator trips, Frequency deviation from 50Hz.

title: AEMO Market Notice 115944 about trip of ER01 and ER02
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO Market Notice 115944 about trip of ER01 and ER02" posted at 03-04-2024 04:04:00 PM is: The AEMO issued a market notice regarding a non-credible contingency event involving the simultaneous trip of the Eraring Power Station Units 1 and 2 in New South Wales on the 3rd of April, 2024. This event led to a drop in mainland frequency in the National Electricity Market, but AEMO reported no need for load shedding and no bulk electrical load disconnections were advised. The cause of this event remains unknown; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as it involved a sudden contingency event impacting market frequency. Its root_cause is: Unknown, the cause of the tripping has not been determined. Its accident scale is: Unknown, though impacting major units, immediate effects beyond frequency drop were managed without load shedding. Its dates are: 03/04/2024. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Non-credible contingency event. Its causes are: Simultaneous trip of Eraring Power Station Unit 1 and Unit 2.

title: Replaying four dispatch intervals on Wed 3rd April 2024 with the ER01 and ER02 trip (Part 2)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Replaying four dispatch intervals on Wed 3rd April 2024 with the ER01 and ER02 trip (Part 2)" posted at 03-04-2024 03:25:00 PM is: The news discusses a simultaneous trip of ER01 and ER02 generators in the New South Wales (NSW) region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on April 3, 2024, which led to a drop in mainland frequency. Despite the trip, initially, there were no significant changes in electricity prices or target flows, as dispatch outcomes hadn't yet adjusted. However, by the subsequent dispatch intervals, adjustments were made as rebids were submitted, with available generation in NSW dropping by approximately 1,000MW. Additionally, unit URAN11 began operation to compensate for the loss of output from ER01 and ER02. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with ongoing adjustments in dispatch intervals. Its root_cause is: Simultaneous trip of ER01 and ER02 generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-04-2024. Its affected region is: New South Wales, NEM. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure in generators. Its causes are: Trip of ER01 and ER02, lack of immediate rebid due to time constraints, subsequent adjustments in dispatch intervals.

title: Focusing on VIC exports (and imports) with the ‘Major Power System Event’ on Tuesday 13th February 2024
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Focusing on VIC exports (and imports) with the ‘Major Power System Event’ on Tuesday 13th February 2024" posted at 01-04-2024 06:35:00 PM is: The news discusses the impact of a 'Major Power System Event' on 13th February 2024 in Victoria, triggered by extreme weather including bushfires and strong winds that damaged transmission towers, leading to significant load shedding and affecting electricity exports and imports. Key insights include Victoria's shift from a net energy exporter to importer following the transmission failure and generation loss at Loy Yang A station, along with the analysis of interconnector flows during this event.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on the day of the event with ongoing questions and analysis. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather conditions including bushfires and strong winds. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13th February 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural event. Its causes are: Bushfires, strong winds, transmission tower damage, and subsequent load shedding and grid instability.

title: Callide C3 starts up, after 518 days offline – on 1st April 2024 (no, that’s *not* an April fools tall tale)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C3 starts up, after 518 days offline – on 1st April 2024 (no, that’s *not* an April fools tall tale)" posted at 01-04-2024 04:30:00 PM is: The Callide C3 generator, which had been offline due to a cooling tower failure since 31st October 2022, successfully returned to service on 1st April 2024. Notifications indicated the unit began operating from ~16:00, reaching an output of 200MW. This event followed a minor delay in its return to service, initially expected on 31st March. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, as it may immediately affect supply capacity. The root cause is: Cooling tower failure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: From 31st October 2022 to 1st April 2024. The affected region is: Unknown. The affected key users are: Unknown. The cause type is: Technical failure. The causes are: Cooling tower malfunction leading to extended generator downtime.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Recapping aggregate solar yield in Queensland’s ‘wet week’ prior to Easter 2024" posted at 01-04-2024 02:15:00 PM is: The article reviews the impact of significant rainfall in Queensland on solar energy production, specifically noting a reduced solar yield during the week starting March 25, 2024.
